- Observed: stall events increase when prefetch window set to 2 and microbatch >=8. Root cause points to packing alignment at 4-bit stride when storing to shared memory slab.
- Temporary mitigation: force microbatch cap at 6 for compact path (costly but prevents stalls).

3) Evals: safety & specificity sweep (120 prompt families, 3 seeds).
- Notable deltas: hallucination rate up on dataset "legal-facts-50" (2.7% -> 6.4%); slot filling accuracy down on "invoice-extract-15" (89% -> 83%).
- Hypothesis: quantization loss amplifies low-signal tokens in long-range attention; model hallucination increases when confidences compress to ambiguous logits.

Decisions and temporary policies
- Hold off on canary rollout to hosted API for atlas-13b-compact until KV-cache pack alignment fix merged. (Owners: Omar + Liam, ETA 3 workdays.)
- For Dedicated pools, allow partner teams to opt into experimental compact variants behind a flag but require their traffic to be routed through the safety gate (eval checks) at 10k requests/day.

Open technical questions
- Is the attention-scale sensitivity an implementation bug or an inherent sensitivity to 4-bit block quant? (Next: run layer-wise ablation with per-layer re-scaling in float32 accumulation.)
- Can we design a compact-quant prefetch packing that is identical in bit layout to the int8 cache binary so we avoid dual-decoding paths? (Tradeoffs: memory waste vs code complexity.)
- Do we need a small "debiasing" calibration step post-quant that is part of the model-onboarding pipeline? (Short trial: 2k tokens of calibration to estimate channel-wise shifts.)

Action items (owners + due dates)
- Omar: implement packing-alignment test harness + failing-case snapshotter; due 2028-04-16.
- Liam: prototype layerwise rescale within kernel (prototype branch: gh/pr/582) and add unit tests; due 2028-04-17.
- Priya: run targeted evals on the 7 failing prompt families with 10 seeds and produce prompt-specific mitigation suggestions (e.g., prompting constraints, reject thresholds); due 2028-04-18.
- Zoe: update onboarding checklist to include KV-cache parity verification and the new gating eval (safety + slot accuracy); due 2028-04-15.
- Ben (SRE): scope performance test job to include microbatch sizes 1..12 on compact path and report p95/p99; due 2028-04-19.

Notebook snippets / quick logs (raw)
- decode-log-0423.txt: "seed 12, seqId 0034: token mismatch at pos 94: expected 0x3f -> got 0x3b"
- perf-sample: "mb=8 prefetch=2 p95=222ms (kv-miss 38%), p50=76ms"
- eval-run-2028-04-12.json: summary: {"families_tested":120, "families_regressed":7, "worst_family":"legal-facts-50", "delta_avg":3.8}

Onboarding checklist (draft) — compact model variant
- baseline evals: 120 prompt families, 3 seeds each -> pass if <3 families >5% delta.
- kv-cache parity: unit decode matching on 100 long sequences (>=200 tokens).
- perf: p50 < 100ms, p95 < 250ms on targeted node-class for hosted/dedicated mapping.
- safety: hallucination and toxicity tests (existing safety harness); fail-block if hallucination delta >2x.
- docs: README + kernel micro-ops notes + PRs linked.

Notes to self / things to remember
- Avoid overfitting mitigation to the 7 families — some mitigation (prompt transforms) create regressions elsewhere. Keep a rolling holdout set.
- When debugging packing issues, snapshot raw bytes from both writers (int8 path + compact path) and diff them in hex — misalignment shows up as repeating 0x00 or truncation.
- Communicate to solutions Eng: rollout delay to partners this week; provide short explainer on why compact path is gated.

Meeting followups
- Sync with kernel team Tuesday 10am — agenda: acceptance test for packing alignment, rollout timing, and unit tests required for CI.
- Short demo to product (Thurs 3pm) of eval deltas + mitigation options. Bring clear before/after examples where mitigation succeeds.

References / links (working)
- PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/prod-runtime/pull/582
- Linear: LINEAR-348 (compact-path stalls)
- Confluence: CONFLUENCE-ATLAS-ONBOARD-87 (onboarding page stub)

Closing thought (informal)
- The compact path is promising from a cost/latency angle but the surface area is wide: kernels, cache packing, and eval gating all have to align. This week felt like hitting the first boundary where correctness and perf interact in surprising ways. Prioritize reproducible failing cases — they make the fix straightforward rather than speculative.

-- end --

title: structured-output-truncation-customer-notice-and-rollforward-2028-03-21
preview:
Draft customer-facing summary and guidance (DO NOT SEND until legal & eng sign-off)

Executive summary (one sentence)
Between 2028-03-21T15:07:00Z and 2028-03-21T18:21:00Z a subset of requests that requested structured/JSON outputs experienced truncation at the token boundary during streaming responses, which resulted in malformed or incomplete payloads for some customers using both hosted API and Dedicated instances. We identified the root cause and implemented mitigations; a permanent fix is scheduled for automatic rollout and customers with affected data will be offered assistance to reconcile impacted outputs.

Who is impacted
- Customers using structured output / function-calling or JSON templates where downstream systems parse the model output as strict JSON.
- Primarily observed in US-WEST-2 region and in Dedicated pools with burst batching enabled.
- Estimated impact: ~0.8% of total requests across those buckets during the incident window; ~72 customer integrations showed at least one truncated response.

What happened (customer-friendly)
We recently released an optimization that merges short-lived streaming batches to improve throughput and reduce cost. During a high-concurrency window, a race between the streaming flush logic and KV-cache eviction caused the final streaming chunk to be cut off for some requests. For structured outputs this produced partial JSON objects (cut-off fields, missing closing brackets), which caused downstream parsers to fail or to silently accept incomplete data.

Technical timeline (UTC)
- 2028-03-21 15:02 — Increase in traffic and concurrent streaming sessions detected in US-WEST-2.
- 2028-03-21 15:07 — First customer report (CS) about malformed JSON from structured responses. Ticket: INC-3892.
- 2028-03-21 15:12 — Eng oncall triaged; reproduced issue in staging with enabled burst batching + streaming.
- 2028-03-21 16:04 — Hotfix deployed (temporary) that disables the merging optimization for streaming responses in affected pools. Observed immediate drop in truncation errors.
- 2028-03-21 17:10 — Broad mitigation: rollout of streaming-flush sequencing change to Dedicated pools; host-level fallback to non-streamed responses for new requests for a short window.
- 2028-03-21 18:21 — Error rate returned to baseline; monitoring kept at elevated level for 24h.

Root cause (concise)
A concurrency bug in the streaming merge optimization caused the final token batch flush to race with KV cache eviction/transition for long-running contexts. When the flush happened after eviction, the server returned an incomplete token stream for the request. The bug surface area: streaming merge + burst batching + KV eviction timing.

Immediate mitigations taken
- Disabled streaming-merge optimization for all streaming responses in affected regions (hotfix).
- Added a server-side safety check that ensures response termination tokens and structural closing characters are present before finalizing a stream.
- Automatically retried a small fraction of recent failed structured responses (where retry semantics are safe) and queued them for manual reconciliation when safe retry was not possible.
- Provided a proactive status-page update and customer outreach to the 72 impacted integrations.

Planned permanent fixes
- Code patch (PR: redwood/runtime#1427): Ensure atomic flush of streamed batches or fall back to synchronous final-chunk assembly for structured-output requests. Target rollout: 2028-03-28.
- Regression tests: Add targeted end-to-end tests that recreate high-concurrency streaming + KV eviction scenarios.
- Telemetry: Add specific metrics and alerts for partial-JSON detection at the gateway parsing layer.

Customer-facing remediation and suggested steps
If you received a truncated response during the window above we recommend the following steps:
1) Detect: Look for parse errors or responses missing a closing brace/bracket or ending mid-field.
2) Retry: If your integration is idempotent or supports safe retries, re-issue the same request (same conversation id / prompt) — our logs indicate retries during the window succeeded in the majority of cases once the hotfix was applied.
3) Reconstruct: If you logged the original model response, you may be able to append the final fragment from a successful retry or re-run the request in read-only mode to get a full version. Contact CS for assisted reconciliation if you cannot safely re-run.
4) Prevent (recommended best practice): For high-stakes structured outputs, prefer non-streaming finalization or implement a lightweight post-parse validator that rejects partial payloads and triggers an automatic re-request.

Customer assistance offer
- We will proactively reach out to the 72 impacted integrations with a tailored reconciliation plan.
- For enterprise/Dedicated customers with persistent data sensitivity concerns, we can provide log extracts and run a controlled re-issuance of requests in a replay environment under NDA.
- Where we are at fault for data loss (rare, only where we can verify the original prompt was delivered and no safe retry exists), account credits and engineering-assisted fix/cleanup will be made available.

Suggested public status page update (short)
Title: Partial structured-output truncation affecting some streaming responses — mitigated
Body: Between 2028-03-21 15:07 UTC and 2028-03-21 18:21 UTC a bug related to streaming response assembly caused some structured (JSON/function-calling) responses to be truncated, affecting a subset of customers. We deployed a hotfix that mitigated the issue and are rolling out a permanent fix. Customers that experienced malformed outputs have been contacted directly.

Suggested detailed customer email (long)
Subject: Incident update — structured JSON outputs truncated during streaming (Mar 21 UTC)
Hi <CustomerName>,
We’re reaching out about a recent service incident that affected structured (JSON or function-calling) model responses for a small number of requests. What happened, what we did, and how we’ll help you reconcile: [include Executive summary above].
Next steps for you: See the remediation steps above. If you would like Redwood to assist with replaying or reconciling affected requests, please reply to this message or open a ticket with INC-3892.
We apologize for the disruption and the unexpected engineering debt this created for your pipelines; ensuring correct structured outputs is a top priority.

Internal notes to CS + SRE (bullet points)
- Priority: Reach out to impacted customers within 24 hours if not already contacted.
- For Dedicated customers: coordinate with platform-eng to enable replay without affecting production quotas.
- SRE: keep KV eviction telemetry enabled and add a midpoint counter that flags any stream closure without a terminal brace.
- Product: evaluate making non-streamed finalization the default for function-calling until patch is in place.

Open questions / follow-ups (for eng)
- Can we identify the exact fraction of requests per-customer that were truncated? (SLO for visibility needed)
- Can we implement an automated reconciliation job to attempt safe re-issuance for customer owners who opt-in?
- Legal/compliance: are there customers in regulated sectors where even partial exposures are reportable?

Contacts
- Primary CS lead: Sofia Martinez (sofia@redwood.ai)
- Eng oncall: Ethan Blake (ethan@redwood.ai)
- Escalation L5: Priya Nair (priya@redwood.ai)

Draft sign-off checklist (who needs to approve before sending official emails)
- Eng oncall validation of root cause (required)
- Product review of messaging for accuracy and tone (required)
- Legal review for regulated-customer language (required)
- VP CS sign-off for account credits or remediation offers (if any)

Notes to self (draft-ish tone)
- This doc is intentionally pragmatic; keep the public status blurb brief and non-technical. Customer emails should include actionable next steps and an offer for assisted reconciliation.
- Avoid over-promising a timeline for credits — escalate to VP CS if requested.
- After patch rollout, plan a follow-up note summarizing the PR and tests added to prevent recurrence.

Attachments / artifacts to include with outreach
- Link to the incident timeline (Confluence) and redwood/runtime#1427 PR.
- A short KB: 'How to detect and recover from truncated structured outputs' (draft included in Confluence).

End of draft.

(Version history)
- 2028-03-21: initial incident note and customer list compiled by CS oncall.
- 2028-03-22: added suggested status blurb and reconciliation steps; added eng hotfix/PR links.

title: Relayband latency vs $/QPS quickscan
preview:
Purpose:
This is a short exploratory quickscan of "relayband" scheduling (local term for a hybrid short-slot + background-tiling scheduler) to see whether we can get a Pareto improvement for microburst-heavy conversational workloads: improve p99 without increasing per-QPS dollar burn materially. Not a full experiment log — thinking sketch + annotated numbers and immediate next steps.

Background / motivation:
- We frequently see customers with mixed workloads: many short chat turns (10-60 tokens) interleaved with occasional long generations.
- Conventional tiling / slot approaches either favor throughput (tiling) or latency (dedicated ephemeral slots). At scale, neither dominates: tiling saves $/token but increases p99 for short-turn bursts.
- Relayband idea: keep a small fraction of fast short-lived slots (relay lanes) that handle sub-100-token turns and fall back to tiling when occupancy is high. The relay lanes are charged a small premium but drastically reduce head-of-line blocking for short interactions.

What we ran (high level):
- Workload: synthetic conversational mix derived from convo-mix-v3 (70% short 12-token turns, 25% mid 60-token, 5% long 400-token).
- Hardware: A100x2 machines (same infra as recent slotization runs).
- Models: open-llm-7b-quant (fp16 baseline + int8-compact variant for tiled lanes).
- Configs tested: baseline-tiling, relayband-small, relayband-large (defined below).
- Measurement window: 30m steady-state with injected microbursts (short-turn spikes up to 5x baseline for 30s every 10m). Metrics collected: p50/p90/p99 generation latency (ms), sustained throughput, and $/kQPS (approximate cost normalized).

Config sketches:
- baseline-tiling: 100% tiling lanes (optimized pack-size 16), cold start disabled, KV cache shared.
- relayband-small: 10% of capacity pinned as relay lanes (fast scheduling, higher reserved memory, prewarmed), 90% tiling. Fallback: if relays full -> tiling.
- relayband-large: 30% relay lanes, 70% tiling.

Annotated results (median of runs):

ASCII table (latency in ms, $/kQPS normalized to baseline=1.00):

Config                | p50  | p90  | p99  | sustained QPS | $/kQPS
----------------------+------|------|------|---------------|-------
baseline-tiling       | 48   | 165  | 980  | 1.00          | 1.00  
relayband-small       | 44   | 120  | 430  | 0.98          | 1.05  
relayband-large       | 42   | 105  | 320  | 0.95          | 1.12  

Quick interpretation:
- p99 improved dramatically vs pure tiling: ~2.3x improvement (980 -> 430) with small relayband; ~3x with large.
- p90 also improved and p50 shows small gains due to reduced queuing for short turns.
- Cost: relay lanes do introduce a modest normalized cost increase (5–12%), measured as $/kQPS in this hardware/packing regime.
- Sustained QPS slightly lower for relayband configs due to underutilization of reserved lanes for long-turn traffic; this is expected and tunable.

ASCII sparkline of p99 across the 30m window (one char per 30s sample, relative scale):
baseline-tiling: ▁▂▃▂▇▇▇▆▇▇▇▆▅▆▇▇▇▆▇▇▆▅▃▂▁
relayband-small : ▁▁▁▁▃▃▃▂▃▃▃▂▂▂▃▃▃▂▃▃▂▂▁▁▁
relayband-large : ▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁

What the ascii sparkline shows:
- baseline spikes strongly during injected microbursts and takes many minutes to recover (tail jank).
- small relayband significantly truncates the magnitude of spikes and accelerates recovery.
- large relayband almost eliminates spikes for this workload.

Micro-level observations (traces and traces-of-interest):
- Relay lanes reduce queue depth observed at the head of the decode pipeline for short-turn requests.
- We observed increased fragmentation in token packing for the tiling lanes when relay fraction rises; packing efficiency dropped ~6% in large relayband runs. That accounts for most of the $/kQPS delta.
- Fallback path clarity important: when relay lanes saturate, request handoff to tiling introduces a small routing/serialization penalty (~4–8ms) but avoids tail amplification.
- Cold-start behavior: relay lanes need aggressive prewarm (idle keep-alive) to be effective; otherwise p99 temporarily worsens on rare cold cycles.

Actionable knobs and tradeoffs:
- Relay fraction: sweet spot appears near 10–15% for convo-mix-v3; 30% buys more p99 headroom but increases $/kQPS noticeably. 
- Adaptive relay scaling: consider short-window autoscale for relay lanes tied to short-turn QPS signal (or burst detector). This could recover packing efficiency because relay fraction ramps up only during spikes.
- Packing-aware fallback: when moving from relay to tiling, preserve any partial KV or decoded prefix to avoid restart penalty.
- Prewarm policy: keep N relay lanes warm per region; N tuned by expected microburst amplitude.

Risks / unknowns:
- Real-world customer mixes vary; workload used here is synthetic. Need sampling from prod traces (privacy-safe) to validate.
- Edge/regional constraints: small relay fleets in many regions may increase ops complexity and cross-region cost.
- Integration complexity with current routing/fallback subsystem — requires careful graceful-degrade testing (we saw a minor race in the prototype routing logic).

Next steps (short list):
1) Run the same quickscan on a trimmed set of prod-sampled mixes (three customers) to check sensitivity. (Owner: Diego)
2) Implement adaptive relay autoscaler prototype (scale up relay fraction during detected spikes) and rerun. (Owner: Priya)
3) Add a microbenchmark to measure packing efficiency delta at token-level and simulate cost tradeoffs (Owner: Ava).
4) Document fallback serialization flow and add unit test to prevent routing race. (Owner: Platform eng)

Notes / random observations:
- For conversational SDK customers, latency wins (p99) often justify a modest price premium. When presenting to product, show two variants: latency-first (relayband) and throughput-first (tiling) with clear $/kQPS and p99 numbers.
- UX/Console: expose a "relay fraction" slider with estimated p99 and $ impact (approx formula in linked artifacts) — could be a powerful developer control.

Open questions for review:
- Are we comfortable with per-region relay minima (ops complexity) vs a shared cross-region relay pool?
- How to price reserved relay lanes for Dedicated customers (should they be a separate SKU)?
- Do we want to bake adaptive relay autoscaling into Redwood Optimize as a recommendation?

Appendix (experimental notes):
- bench harness: tools/bench/relayband-run.sh --mix convo-mix-v3 --duration 1800 --burst-schedule 600,30 --relay-fraction 0.1|0.3
- raw logs stored: s3://redwood-bench/relayband/2027-09-14/*
- quickplots: gist: https://gist.github.com/avachen/relayband-quickplot

Status: draft quickscan. Comments welcome — especially from platform and SRE about operational edge cases.

baseline-tiling: ▁▂▃▂▇▇▇▆▇▇▇▆▅▆▇▇▇▆▇▇▆▅▃▂▁
relayband-small : ▁▁▁▁▃▃▃▂▃▃▃▂▂▂▃▃▃▂▃▃▂▂▁▁▁
relayband-large : ▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁

What the ascii sparkline shows:
- baseline spikes strongly during injected microbursts and takes many minutes to recover (tail jank).
- small relayband significantly truncates the magnitude of spikes and accelerates recovery.
- large relayband almost eliminates spikes for this workload.

Micro-level observations (traces and traces-of-interest):
- Relay lanes reduce queue depth observed at the head of the decode pipeline for short-turn requests.
- We observed increased fragmentation in token packing for the tiling lanes when relay fraction rises; packing efficiency dropped ~6% in large relayband runs. That accounts for most of the $/kQPS delta.
- Fallback path clarity important: when relay lanes saturate, request handoff to tiling introduces a small routing/serialization penalty (~4–8ms) but avoids tail amplification.
- Cold-start behavior: relay lanes need aggressive prewarm (idle keep-alive) to be effective; otherwise p99 temporarily worsens on rare cold cycles.

Actionable knobs and tradeoffs:
- Relay fraction: sweet spot appears near 10–15% for convo-mix-v3; 30% buys more p99 headroom but increases $/kQPS noticeably. 
- Adaptive relay scaling: consider short-window autoscale for relay lanes tied to short-turn QPS signal (or burst detector). This could recover packing efficiency because relay fraction ramps up only during spikes.
- Packing-aware fallback: when moving from relay to tiling, preserve any partial KV or decoded prefix to avoid restart penalty.
- Prewarm policy: keep N relay lanes warm per region; N tuned by expected microburst amplitude.

Risks / unknowns:
- Real-world customer mixes vary; workload used here is synthetic. Need sampling from prod traces (privacy-safe) to validate.
- Edge/regional constraints: small relay fleets in many regions may increase ops complexity and cross-region cost.
- Integration complexity with current routing/fallback subsystem — requires careful graceful-degrade testing (we saw a minor race in the prototype routing logic).

Next steps (short list):
1) Run the same quickscan on a trimmed set of prod-sampled mixes (three customers) to check sensitivity. (Owner: Diego)
2) Implement adaptive relay autoscaler prototype (scale up relay fraction during detected spikes) and rerun. (Owner: Priya)
3) Add a microbenchmark to measure packing efficiency delta at token-level and simulate cost tradeoffs (Owner: Ava).
4) Document fallback serialization flow and add unit test to prevent routing race. (Owner: Platform eng)

Notes / random observations:
- For conversational SDK customers, latency wins (p99) often justify a modest price premium. When presenting to product, show two variants: latency-first (relayband) and throughput-first (tiling) with clear $/kQPS and p99 numbers.
- UX/Console: expose a "relay fraction" slider with estimated p99 and $ impact (approx formula in linked artifacts) — could be a powerful developer control.

Open questions for review:
- Are we comfortable with per-region relay minima (ops complexity) vs a shared cross-region relay pool?
- How to price reserved relay lanes for Dedicated customers (should they be a separate SKU)?
- Do we want to bake adaptive relay autoscaling into Redwood Optimize as a recommendation?

Appendix (experimental notes):
- bench harness: tools/bench/relayband-run.sh --mix convo-mix-v3 --duration 1800 --burst-schedule 600,30 --relay-fraction 0.1|0.3
- raw logs stored: s3://redwood-bench/relayband/2027-09-14/*
- quickplots: gist: https://gist.github.com/avachen/relayband-quickplot

Status: draft quickscan. Comments welcome — especially from platform and SRE about operational edge cases.

EdgeOps,60000,2027-07-01,47,2027-06-18,70%,42000,healthy,standard renewal + NPS ask,Tier 1,Sophia G.,Send renewal contract + NPS survey,"Generate a 2-line renewal reminder and attach NPS link",Champion confirmed product fit; just needs procurement signoff.

Tracker calculation rules (operational notes):
- Probability: operationally set by CS owner; suggested probability_decay applied as days_to_decision increases past 60d (probability *= 0.9 every 30 days beyond 60).
- Weighted ARR = ARR * Probability (Probability expressed as decimal).
- Decision Window = days between today and contract end; rows flagged when <=60 days appear in weekly QBR slice.
- Escalation Tier: Tier 1 = no blocker, Tier 2 = needs exec or security, Tier 3 = legal/finance/regulatory blockers.

Quick actions & playbook library references (links in linked_artifacts):
- playbook: Expansion-ROI-v1 (use for accounts with expansion_signal)
- playbook: Security-Pilot-KMS-v2 (use for accounts with security_review)
- playbook: Legal-Bridge-Template (use for Tier 3 negotiations)

Suggested cadence and owner obligations:
- CS lead updates probability after each substantive contact; update 'Last Contact' and 'Notes'.
- For Decision Window <= 60 days: automatic weekly sync with AM + Solutions engineer.
- For Tier 2/3: escalate to Head of CS and include Solutions Eng + Legal in weekly thread.

Playbook snippets (copyable):
- Technical QBR invitation (30 min):
  Subject: Technical QBR — <Account> — action items before renewal
  Body: Hi <Champion>, quick 30-min QBR to align on usage patterns, perf obstacles, and renewal runway. Proposed slots: [three slots]. Attach: token usage export + recent error traces.

- Exec sponsor intro (email):
  Subject: Exec intro — improving inference economics for <Account>
  Body: One para summary of customer value, ask for 20-min intro to discuss SLA and expansion path.

Risk scoring model (reference):
- health_signal mapped to risk multiplier: healthy=0.7, expansion_signal=0.5, usage_drop=1.2, quality_regression=1.4, security_review=1.1
- risk_score = (1 - probability) * risk_multiplier * (Decision Window factor)
- Decision Window factor: <=30d -> 1.5, 31-60d -> 1.2, 61-120d -> 1.0, >120d -> 0.8

Open questions / TODOs:
- Add column: Finance contact + procurement pipeline status (currently manual notes).
- Automate probability decay calculation in sheet (scripting / Apps Script).
- Link automated token usage export from observability console into sheet for 'Health Signal' auto-update.

Revision log (working):
- 2027-06-14 (Ava): initial draft and baseline columns + sample rows.
- 2027-06-18 (Diego): added risk scoring model and escalation tiers.
- 2027-06-22 (Priya): added playbook snippets and suggested cadences.

Footer: This is an ops-focused working sheet for weekly CS QBR prep. Treat probabilities as "working" estimates and annotate the reason in the 'Notes' field when overriding suggested decay.

title: Customer heuristics & ask playbook — prioritizing micro-asks
preview:
Purpose
-----
Quick internal playbook for synthesizing customer feedback (micro-asks) into action lanes that product + eng can execute quickly. Focus is on: velocity (ship small wins), clarity (what problem is solved), and economics (cost/latency impact for inference). This is intentionally lightweight — meant for CS reps during weekly triage + product scoping sessions.

Why this exists
--------------
- We see a high volume of short, tactical asks (e.g., per-route timeout tweak, model fallback config, improved error messages for rate-limits).
- Those asks often sit in queues or get lumped into larger epics and never shipped.
- Small fixes can materially reduce churn and unlock enterprise deals if surfaced with risk/cost impact.

Heuristic scoring (quick 5-point rubric)
-----------------------------------
Use this at the top of every ticket/signal we surface. Score each 1–5, then sum. Prioritize >10 for immediate investigation.
- Customer impact: how many customers affected / severity (1 low — 5 high)
- Conversion or retention upside: revenue/renewal signal (1–5)
- Implementation effort: engineering days (reverse scored: 5 = <=1 day, 1 = >3 sprints)
- Risk & security: compliance/regulatory risk reduction (1–5)
- Operational cost delta: expected change to per-token/per-request cost (1–5)

Example: \"Default model fallback when capacity constrained\"
- Impact 4, Revenue 3, Effort 4, Risk 4, Cost delta 3 -> total 18 -> high priority

Triage lanes (how we route asks)
--------------------------------
- Quick-fix lane (<=3 engineering days)
  - Typical asks: config defaults, error message changes, docs clarifications, dashboard filter tweaks.
  - Action pattern: CS owner writes short spec + repro -> assign to \"flight\" engineer -> ship and QA in staging -> release patch.
- Product-scope lane (2–8 weeks)
  - Typical: UX pattern changes (console flows), rollout policy features, observability enhancements (token-level grouping).
  - Action: PRD-lite with success metrics -> product + 1 eng -> sprint planning.
- Research/Architecture lane (quarter-plus)
  - Typical: runtime kernel changes, new caching strategies, private deployment protocol changes.
  - Action: RFC, perf benchmarks, infra sign-off.

Minimal spec template for Quick-fix lane (1 page max)
---------------------------------------------------
- Short problem statement (one sentence)
- Repro steps or example customer transcript
- Proposed change (concrete: config key, UI label, default value)
- Expected impact (metrics to move)
- Rollback criteria
- Owner (CS + eng) and target date

Common micro-asks we surface (not exhaustive)
-----------------------------------------
- Per-route timeout tweak for long-inference models (customer sets 60s -> need 120s for long-context summarizer)
- Prefer nearest-region routing for privacy-sensitive routes (customer needs EU-only routing)
- Better error classification when KV cache miss + cold start (customers see opaque 500s)
- Fallback to cheaper quantized model when Dedicated capacity saturated (preserve availability)
- Console: show token-cost estimate before calling model for large batch job
- Embeddings: stable idempotency key support for dedup pipelines

Vignettes (real customer notes — redacted)
-------------------------------------
1) \"We get intermittent 502s during night batch runs. Hard to tell if it's our side or Redwood; logs just show 504. Can we show model variant + shard id in response logs?\"
   - Heuristic quick score: Impact 3, Revenue 2, Effort 3, Risk 2, Cost 2 = 12 -> Quick-fix/Investigate
   - Proposed immediate action: Add a debug flag to include shard id in error responses for customers who opt-in.

2) \"Our regulated customers in DE need assurance traffic never leaves EU. We have Private deployment but can't justify cost. Is region pinning possible for Hosted API?\"
   - Score: Impact 4, Revenue 4, Effort 2, Risk 5, Cost 2 = 17 -> High priority.
   - Suggestion: Product-scope: per-tenant region pinning + SLA note; include compliance checklist.

Decision anchors (how CS should present asks to product/eng)
------------------------------------------------------
- Always include: concrete customer quote + business outcome (renewal, POC progression, legal ask).
- Attach minimal repro and data: sample request ids, timestamps, region, model variant.
- Estimate the upside (even a rough $k ARR or % renewal reduction) and downside of not doing it.

Quick triage checklist for CS reps (use during daily review)
-------------------------------------------------------
- Is this a single-customer ask or cross-customer? (single vs many)
- Can we ship a toggle or opt-in first? (low-risk rollout)
- Does the ask require infra changes (GPU/runtime) or config/UI only?
- Is there an obvious mitigation we can propose to the customer today?
- Which lane per heuristic score?

Proposed small projects to unblock backlog (owner + timeline suggestions)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
- \"Opt-in debug headers for opaque errors\" — Owner: SRE + CS (Diego) — target 3 weeks
- \"Per-route region tenancy flag (hosted)\" — Owner: Product Hosted API — target Q2 small project
- \"Embeddings dedupe-key header\" — Owner: Product/Eng Optimize — 2 sprints

Open questions / unresolved trade-offs
----------------------------------
- How much telemetry is safe to surface in error responses without leaking infra topology? Need security review.
- For region pinning: can we support it with minimal infra change (routing policy + tenant metadata) or will it require dedicated pools per region?
- When to escalate a repeated micro-ask into a roadmap feature vs keep it as configurable? Thresholds? (suggest 3 customers + ARR signal)

Notes on communicating to customers
--------------------------------
- For quick fixes, set expectations: we will try a patch in X days, share mitigation in the meantime.
- For product scope asks, propose a timeline window (\"Q2\" or \"within 6–8 weeks\") and offer workaround steps.
- When the ask is regulatory, escalate to security & legal immediately with the full customer context.

Template example (fill-and-send)
-------------------------------
Subject: Follow-up + proposed path for [issue short id]
Hi [customer contact],
Thanks for the details — we reproduced the failure and propose the following low-risk change that we can ship in ~1 week: [one-line change]. This should mitigate X while we evaluate a broader solution. Would you like us to enable an opt-in debug flag for your account?

Next steps
----------
- Pilot this playbook with 4 high-frequency clusters during next week's CS sync (Ava)
- Create a triage label in JIRA: cs-microfix -> ensures quick lane routing (Marcus)
- Draft a short Confluence page with the scoring rubric and template (Leah)

Appendix: quick links
---------------------
- Current backlog query: JIRA filter: project = CSREQ AND labels in (microfix)
- Confluence draft: CONFLUENCE:cs-microfix-template
- Slack triage channel: #cs-triage

---
Notes-to-self: keep this compact. We want CS reps to be able to copy/paste the spec template into Jira in <5 minutes. Track wins and time-to-ship for the pilot.

title: Inference demand blueprint - tokens & QPS forecast (2027 Q1)
preview:
Summary:
This is a working planning sheet for projected request demand (tokens/sec and QPS) across regions for the Redwood Hosted API for 2027 Q1. Purpose: produce conservative GPU fleet targets and headroom policies that satisfy 99th percentile latency SLOs for the majority of end-customer workloads while minimizing overprovisioning.

Key conclusions (top-line):
- Baseline expected 2027Q1 peak (business-as-usual) = 1.85M tokens/sec globally (aggregate at 95th percentile daily peaks).
- Conservative planning target (headroom margin) = baseline * 1.5 => 2.78M tokens/sec to reserve across fleet for predictable latency.
- Operational emergency capacity (fallback / best-effort) target = 1.0x additional (total theoretical capacity target ~= 5.56M tokens/sec when including emergency fallback variants).
- Regional allocation: US-West 38%, US-East 26%, EU-West 22%, APAC-SG 14% (alloc by observed traffic patterns + growth).

Assumptions and definitions:
- Token = model input+output token as counted by the serving runtime billing meter.
- QPS measured at request-level (chat/generation requests per second). Token/sec = QPS * avg_tokens_per_request.
- Avg tokens per request (weighted): 158 tokens (mix of chat short prompts + medium generation).
- Average concurrency profile: median request concurrency per GPU worker = 8 for shorter sequences, up to 32 for long-form. For sizing we use effective concurrency 10.
- Representative model mix for hosted API: 60% medium inference (ca. 25 tokens/s per vGPU on A100 baseline), 30% long-form (lower token/s throughput), 10% embeddings/rerank (high QPS, low tokens).
- Kernel performance assumptions: A100-80GB ~ 25 tok/sec per vGPU (for 2048 context typical), H100-80GB ~ 40 tok/sec for same workload after optimized kernels/FP16 + quant friendliness; L40 ~ 22 tok/sec for cost-optimized quant runs. These are conservative estimates and include overheads.

Regional projections (workbook-style table):
Columns: region | share% | baseline tokens/sec | conservative headroom (x1.5) | QPS-equivalent (avg_tokens=158)
- US-West | 38% | 703,000 | 1,054,500 | 6,675 QPS
- US-East | 26% | 481,000 | 721,500 | 4,568 QPS
- EU-West | 22% | 407,000 | 610,500 | 3,864 QPS
- APAC-SG | 14% | 259,000 | 388,500 | 2,459 QPS
(Notes: numbers rounded to nearest 1k; QPS-equivalent = headroom_tokens / 158)

GPU pool sizing (policy-level):
- Target headroom tokens converted to vGPU counts using conservative per-vGPU token/sec: use 25 tok/sec (safe A100 baseline) unless customer is pinned to H100.
- vGPU needed per region = ceil(headroom_tokens / 25)
  - US-West: 1,054,500 / 25 = 42,180 => 42,180 vGPUs (this is a theoretical count; see pooling model below for effective slices)
  - US-East: 28,860 vGPUs
  - EU-West: 24,420 vGPUs
  - APAC-SG: 15,540 vGPUs
- Practical mapping: we allocate physical GPU pools and slice into vGPUs (e.g., 1 A100-80GB as 4 slices for concurrency reasons). Example mapping: assume 4 vGPUs per physical A100, so physical GPUs = vGPUs / 4.
  - US-West physical GPUs = 10,545 (approx)
  - US-East physical GPUs = 7,215
  - EU-West physical GPUs = 6,105
  - APAC-SG physical GPUs = 3,885
- These numbers are intentionally large because they reflect 25 tok/sec baseline; in practice, use mixed fleet (H100 for hot paths) to reduce counts by ~35-60% depending on model mix.

Cost/efficiency levers (short list):
- Mix H100 in hot slices for 20% of peak requests to compress physical GPU counts (H100 provides ~1.6x token/sec).
- Enable continuous batching and prefix/KV cache for common prompts to reduce token work per request (observed savings 10-25% on mid-tail workloads).
- Use pre-warmed burst pool in target regions sized to 20% of headroom to handle minute-level spikes with sub-second preemption.
- Aggressive autoscale policies for non-SLA tiers: scale CPU-first for short requests and drain low-priority work.

Tail-smoothing and SLO behavior:
- SLO target: P95 latency target 180ms; P99 < 400ms for medium-length tokens. The headroom above covers queuing effects and continuous batching latencies.
- If utilization > 70% on hot-slices, trigger immediate scale-up; aim to keep steady-state utilization in 50-65% range during peak windows.

Operational playbook excerpts (quick ops checklist):
- Daily: ingest token/sec trend + 1h rolling max; run headroom burn-rate check (alerts if >85% of reserved headroom sustained >5m).
- Weekly: renewal check for reserved capacity commitments vs. trending throughput (finance/purchasing notification if >10% variance from plan).
- Incident: if region utilization above emergency threshold, fallback policy = route non-pinned traffic to compatible model variant in adjacent region (subject to privacy/TOS).

Uncertainties / open questions (TO DO):
- Actual average tokens per request may shift with enterprise customers using longer-context modes. Re-run with 300 tokens/request for enterprise lanes.
- Kernel speedups from upcoming compiler change (Q1 2027) could alter per-vGPU token/sec by +20-40%; keep a rolling estimate.
- APAC growth sensitivity: 1.5x month-over-month spikes during partnerships not currently in bookings pipeline.

Action items (owners and ETA):
- Run microbench for new quantized medium model on H100 (Linus) - ETA 2026-11-19
- Update autoscale policy to add short-burst pre-warm bucket per region (Raj) - ETA 2026-11-26
- Add per-customer avg_tokens telemetry to console (instrumentation + schema change) (Maya) - ETA 2026-12-10

Appendix A: worked example conversion (US-West):
- Baseline tokens/sec = 703,000.
- Conservative headroom = 703k * 1.5 = 1,054,500 tokens/sec.
- Using H100 slices instead of A100 (assume 40 tok/sec per vGPU): vGPUs = 1,054,500 / 40 = 26,362 vGPUs => physical H100s (4 slices each) = 6,591.
- If we enable caching that saves 18% tokens, effective headroom reduces to ~864k => vGPUs on H100 = 21,600 (physical = 5,400).

Notes / provenance:
- Traffic shares derived from historical 2026 Q3-Q4 rolling logs (internal query: /metrics/traffic/hosted/2026Q3-4).
- Performance numbers based on internal perf runs in infra/perf-lab (benchmarks/2026-09-models.md).
- This doc is a planning artifact and should be reconciled with finance reserved capacity forecasts before procurement.

Contact / where to discuss: #infra-capacity (Slack), weekly infra sync Tuesday 11:00 PT.

— End working draft —
text:
Summary:
This is a working planning sheet for projected request demand (tokens/sec and QPS) across regions for the Redwood Hosted API for 2027 Q1. Purpose: produce conservative GPU fleet targets and headroom policies that satisfy 99th percentile latency SLOs for the majority of end-customer workloads while minimizing overprovisioning.

Key conclusions (top-line):
- Baseline expected 2027Q1 peak (business-as-usual) = 1.85M tokens/sec globally (aggregate at 95th percentile daily peaks).
- Conservative planning target (headroom margin) = baseline * 1.5 => 2.78M tokens/sec to reserve across fleet for predictable latency.
- Operational emergency capacity (fallback / best-effort) target = 1.0x additional (total theoretical capacity target ~= 5.56M tokens/sec when including emergency fallback variants).
- Regional allocation: US-West 38%, US-East 26%, EU-West 22%, APAC-SG 14% (alloc by observed traffic patterns + growth).

Assumptions and definitions:
- Token = model input+output token as counted by the serving runtime billing meter.
- QPS measured at request-level (chat/generation requests per second). Token/sec = QPS * avg_tokens_per_request.
- Avg tokens per request (weighted): 158 tokens (mix of chat short prompts + medium generation).
- Average concurrency profile: median request concurrency per GPU worker = 8 for shorter sequences, up to 32 for long-form. For sizing we use effective concurrency 10.
- Representative model mix for hosted API: 60% medium inference (ca. 25 tokens/s per vGPU on A100 baseline), 30% long-form (lower token/s throughput), 10% embeddings/rerank (high QPS, low tokens).
- Kernel performance assumptions: A100-80GB ~ 25 tok/sec per vGPU (for 2048 context typical), H100-80GB ~ 40 tok/sec for same workload after optimized kernels/FP16 + quant friendliness; L40 ~ 22 tok/sec for cost-optimized quant runs. These are conservative estimates and include overheads.

Regional projections (workbook-style table):
Columns: region | share% | baseline tokens/sec | conservative headroom (x1.5) | QPS-equivalent (avg_tokens=158)
- US-West | 38% | 703,000 | 1,054,500 | 6,675 QPS
- US-East | 26% | 481,000 | 721,500 | 4,568 QPS
- EU-West | 22% | 407,000 | 610,500 | 3,864 QPS
- APAC-SG | 14% | 259,000 | 388,500 | 2,459 QPS
(Notes: numbers rounded to nearest 1k; QPS-equivalent = headroom_tokens / 158)

- vGPU needed per region = ceil(headroom_tokens / 25)

  - EU-West: 24,420 vGPUs
  - APAC-SG: 15,540 vGPUs

  - US-East physical GPUs = 7,215
  - EU-West physical GPUs = 6,105
  - APAC-SG physical GPUs = 3,885
- These numbers are intentionally large because they reflect 25 tok/sec baseline; in practice, use mixed fleet (H100 for hot paths) to reduce counts by ~35-60% depending on model mix.

- Aggressive autoscale policies for non-SLA tiers: scale CPU-first for short requests and drain low-priority work.

Tail-smoothing and SLO behavior:
- SLO target: P95 latency target 180ms; P99 < 400ms for medium-length tokens. The headroom above covers queuing effects and continuous batching latencies.
- If utilization > 70% on hot-slices, trigger immediate scale-up; aim to keep steady-state utilization in 50-65% range during peak windows.

Operational playbook excerpts (quick ops checklist):

- Weekly: renewal check for reserved capacity commitments vs. trending throughput (finance/purchasing notification if >10% variance from plan).
- Incident: if region utilization above emergency threshold, fallback policy = route non-pinned traffic to compatible model variant in adjacent region (subject to privacy/TOS).

- Kernel speedups from upcoming compiler change (Q1 2027) could alter per-vGPU token/sec by +20-40%; keep a rolling estimate.
- APAC growth sensitivity: 1.5x month-over-month spikes during partnerships not currently in bookings pipeline.

title: 15m-burst-safety-stock-and-tailwind-projections
preview:
Purpose:
This working-sheet captures a focused set of 15-minute sustained burst scenarios ("tailwind" bursts) that historically have caused the largest transient stress on the Hosted API ingress. The aim: compute tokens/sec and QPS headroom required to absorb 15-minute sustained multipliers, map those to GPU slices across regions, and produce a recommended safety-stock (warm prewarmed GPUs + spare rotational slices) target for Q1 2027 planning.

Context & motivation:
- Most existing capacity docs focus on hourly/day peaks. However, product launches, marketing-induced campaigns, and a handful of viral events create 10–30 minute sustained bursts that require different heuristics (prewarm time, cold-start fraction, KV cache warmup).
- We want a short-run safety stock that minimizes tail-latency SLO breaches while keeping monthly committed GPU costs reasonable.
- This file experiments with a 15-minute window as the planning unit (conservative relative to 5-min microbursts and more operationally relevant than 1-hour averages).

Assumptions (explicit):
- Model mix as of Nov 2026 (traffic-weighted): small-gen (20% traffic), mid-gen (55%), large-chat (25%). Effective tokens-per-request distribution taken from ingress logs (sample week Oct 2026).
- Baseline avg token generation per request: 75 tokens. Avg QPS -> token/sec multiplier = QPS * 75.
- Representative GPU performance (tokens/sec sustained per GPU) for 1-shot generative workload (batching enabled, KV cache hit 20%):
  - small-gen GPU slice: 12k tokens/sec
  - mid-gen GPU slice: 5k tokens/sec
  - large-chat GPU slice: 1.2k tokens/sec
- Prewarm time to be fully ready (model loaded + warm KV): 90–180s depending on model size.
- Cold-start fraction during a 15m spontaneous surge (if no prewarm): assume 30% of requests hit cold model loads at burst start for large-chat, 10% for mid-gen, 5% for small-gen.
- Target tail latency SLO: 99th percentile response generation latency < 1.2s for small-gen, < 2.5s for mid-gen, < 4.0s for large-chat.

Key metrics captured per scenario (sheet columns in this doc):
- Scenario name
- 15m sustained tokens/sec (calculated)
- Peak QPS implied (tokens/sec / 75)
- GPUs required by model class (rounded up)
- Warm prewarmed GPUs (recommended)
- Safety stock GPUs (rotational spare per region)
- Cold-start risk notes / KV cache mitigation

Scenario snapshots (conservative rounding applied):
1) Holiday-campaign (regional hit: US-WE)
- multiplier: 5x baseline sustained for 15m
- estimated 15m tokens/sec: 3,600,000 tokens/sec (US-WE only)
- implied QPS: 48,000
- model-class split tokens/sec: small 720k, mid 1,980k, large 900k
- GPUs required (by class): small: 60 GPUs (720k/12k), mid: 396 GPUs (1,980k/5k => 396), large: 750 GPUs (900k/1.2k => 750)
- Recommended warm prewarmed: small: 30, mid: 200, large: 500 (50% of required to cover initial 15m after prewarm + progressive auto-scale)
- Safety stock (rotational warm spares): small: 12, mid: 40, large: 100
- Notes: Large-chat cold-start fraction is high; recommend aggressive KV prefix caching for known prompt templates and a canary prewarm 5 minutes before predicted campaign windows.

2) Product-launch (global multi-region wave)
- multiplier: 3x baseline across top 3 regions (US-WE, US-E, EU-WE) for 15m each (offset by up to 5m)
- estimated global 15m tokens/sec: 2,400,000 tokens/sec (per-region ~800k)
- implied QPS global: 32,000 (per-region ~10.7k)
- GPUs required per region (approx): small: 7, mid: 160, large: 240 (regional split simplified)
- Warm prewarmed per region: small: 4, mid: 80, large: 120
- Safety stock per region: small: 2, mid: 16, large: 24
- Notes: Multi-region offsets reduce simultaneous peak; cross-region routing fallbacks should be enabled if region SLOs degrade.

3) Viral-prompt (unexpected)
- multiplier: 8x baseline in single region, high large-chat proportion (40%)
- estimated 15m tokens/sec: 4,800,000 tokens/sec
- implied QPS: 64,000
- GPUs required (bulk large-chat): small: 16, mid: 528, large: 1,600
- Recommended warm prewarmed (aggressive): small: 8, mid: 264, large: 1100
- Safety stock: small: 4, mid: 80, large: 300
- Notes: This is the worst-case 15m window for tail latencies; consider automatic model-fallback to smaller/chat-compressed variants after 30s of degraded SLOs.

Operational heuristics & prewarm policy (recommended):
- For planned events: schedule prewarm start = T-10 minutes for mid-gen, T-6 minutes for large-chat; ensure at least 50% of required GPUs prewarmed before expected start.
- For unplanned spikes: use a 2-phase autoscale: immediate short-lived burst pool (instantly available reserved slices) + progressive cold pool spin-up. Keep an on-hand reserve of fraction of GPUs per region (safety stock) to soak first 3 minutes.
- Safety-stock sizing: base safety stock target = max(ceil(0.12 * required_gpus), minimum_by_class) where minimum_by_class = {small: 2, mid: 10, large: 25}. This blends proportional and floor-based safety.
- Keep rotational spare pool equal to 30% of safety-stock to enable fast failover without exceeding committed pool limits.

Cost/commitment considerations (rough):
- Warm prewarm is expensive: a prewarmed large-chat GPU slice costs ~x2 the incremental token cost of on-demand scaling if held idle for >20m in expected low windows. We suggest targeted prewarms tied to marketing calendars, not blanket always-on prewarm.
- Safety stock can be implemented using cheaper lower-SLA GPU families (quantized variants) to reduce cost, but must verify accuracy/regression impact via Redwood Optimize before switching.

Quick sensitivity checks (notes):
- If KV cache hits increase to 50%, mid-gen GPU needs drop by ~25% in the scenarios above.
- If average tokens per request climbs from 75 to 110 (longer completions), required GPUs increase by ~47%.

Open questions / action items (TODOs):
- [ ] Run a 5-week historical replays using Oct–Nov ingress logs to validate the 15m sustained model (assigned: Diego). Deadline: 2026-12-10.
- [ ] Hook the 15m-burst detector into the console alerting (low-latency topic) so on-call can trigger prewarm prior to scheduled launches (assigned: Priya).
- [ ] Evaluate cost/benefit of using a cheaper quantized large-chat variant as rotational safety stock (assigned: Liam).

Appendix (one-line table snapshot format):
Scenario | tokens/sec_15m | QPS_imp | GPUs_req_small| GPUs_req_mid | GPUs_req_large | warm_prewarm_small | warm_prewarm_mid | warm_prewarm_large | safety_small | safety_mid | safety_large
Holiday-campaign | 3600000 | 48000 | 60 | 396 | 750 | 30 | 200 | 500 | 12 | 40 | 100
Product-launch | 2400000 | 32000 | 21 | 480 | 720 | 12 | 240 | 360 | 6 | 48 | 72
Viral-prompt | 4800000 | 64000 | 16 | 528 | 1600 | 8 | 264 | 1100 | 4 | 80 | 300

Notes for reviewers:
- This is intentionally conservative on large-chat sizing (1.2k tokens/sec per GPU). If Serve-RT kernel improvements land (expected Q1 2027), we can re-run with 1.6k tokens/sec which materially lowers required GPU count for large models.
- Please add experimental replay links or quick graphs to the Confluence page linked above.

Revision history:
- 2026-11-04: initial draft (Ava)
- 2026-11-19: updated large-chat perf numbers after microbenchmark (Diego)
- 2026-12-02: added product-launch regional splits and TODOs (Ava)

End of sheet (working doc; feedback welcome).

title: Voice archaeology — request traces and synthesis memo
preview:
Purpose / TL;DR:
This is a working synthesis of customer feedback threads from Jul-Sep 2026 focused on request provenance (where asks come from), repeat ask patterns, and lightweight fix proposals. Goal: give PM + Eng a short list of low-effort, high-confidence patches and a provenance map so we can triage ownership. Not a final product — notes + unresolved questions inline.

How I pulled this together:
- Sources: Support tickets (top 150 by frequency), NPS followups, 1:1s, renewal calls, pilot telemetry, and 3 Slack threads (see linked_artifacts).
- Method: Rapid clustering (manual), then trace each cluster to origin channel and last-touch (who responded). Marked whether ask is: UX friction, API/behavior mismatch, observability gap, performance/cost, or security/compliance.
- Confidence: high for frequency and clear asks; medium where requests are ambiguous; low where we only have single anecdotes.

Executive summary (short bullets):
- Three recurring high-impact asks: (1) deterministic model fallback behavior for degraded capacity; (2) token-level cost attribution across multi-route calls; (3) easier way to pin embedding models for reranking pipelines.
- Quick wins (1–2 sprints): response header exposing routed-model-id + fallback-reason; a small console toggle that pins embedding family per project; add per-request token breakdown to the /usage endpoint for last 7 days.
- Longer work (quarter+): a routing policy UI for canary/fallback preferences and per-resource SLA tiers.
- Risk: several requests imply productization of internal playbooks (e.g., bespoke fallback chains) — could increase maintenance surface.

Request clusters (annotated):
1) Predictable fallback & model variants (frequency: very high)
  - Examples: enterprise pilot A: "we had 20% of calls routed to tiny-quant during peak and the outputs were wildly different"; customer B: "behavioural mismatch when fallback used distil model"
  - Channel provenance: Support tickets + renewal call; last-touch: Solutions Eng (Samir), workaround doc exists but not surfaced.
  - Impact: reliability + SLA confidence; churn risk if behavior changes silently.
  - Proposed quick fix: add response headers: model_variant, fallback_trigger, route_policy_id. Also add a transparency doc page. (Effort: small)

2) Token-level cost attribution and split billing (frequency: high)
  - Example: large customer runs a pipeline with call -> embedding -> rerank. They cannot reconcile token spend per stage and under-bill internal teams.
  - Channel provenance: Finance ticket + internal CS billing escalations. Linked to CONFLUENCE-CC-789.
  - Impact: billing disputes, internal chargeback friction.
  - Proposed quick fix: add per-request breakdown to existing /usage endpoint and a CSV export option scoped to route and model. (Effort: medium)

3) Embedding pinning and rerank stability (frequency: medium-high)
  - Example: customers report rerank instability when default embedding family upgrades propagate.
  - Channel provenance: Pilot telemetry + Slack thread.
  - Impact: regressions in downstream ranking pipelines, loss of trust.
  - Proposed quick fix: Console toggle "pin embedding family" and API flag pin_embedding_version=true for new calls. (Effort: small)

4) Observability: token latency ladders & token sampling (frequency: medium)
  - Ask: deeper per-token latency histograms and sampling traces to debug bad tails.
  - Channel provenance: SRE feedback + 2 support tickets.
  - Proposed work: add lightweight token-sampling traces to the tracing pipeline (configurable). (Effort: medium-hard)

5) Debug ergonomics: request replay and pinned inputs (frequency: low-medium)
  - Ask: sandbox replay that reproduces exact upstream route and fallback behavior.
  - Proposed: small internal tool (could be shipped as support utility). (Effort: medium)

Quick-win prioritization (owner suggestions):
- Add response headers (model variant + fallback reason) — Owner: Serving Runtime eng, PM: Priya (ETA: 2 weeks to spec, 2 sprints to ship).
- Embedding pin toggle in console + API flag — Owner: Product-hosted-api, DevEx (ETA: 1 sprint front-end + 1 sprint backend).
- /usage per-request breakdown CSV export — Owner: Billing infra, Finance eng (ETA: 3–4 weeks).

Notes on provenance mapping (why it matters):
- Many asks repeat but with different language across channels. Eg: "different outputs" (Support), "model drift" (sales), "downgrade during burst" (SRE); mapping to a single root ask (fallback semantics) prevents duplicated effort.
- I kept a simple tag per trace: channel / last responder / time window / impact. Happy to surface a spreadsheet if helpful.

Unresolved questions & tradeoffs (callouts):
- How prescriptive should fallback policies be? Option A: allow per-customer policy definitions (flexible, more product complexity). Option B: surface telemetry + warnings but keep server-side auto-fallback opaque (simpler). Recommendation: start with Option B + transparency headers and docs while we prototype a policy UI.
- Pinning embeddings vs auto-upgrades: pinned models reduce surprise but increase security/compliance burden for near-LTS families. Need legal + compliance signoff for pinned older quantized models.

Edge cases / gotchas observed in raw threads (notes-to-self):
- One customer had an internal middleware that mutated prompt order; behavior mismatch blamed on fallback but was actually upstream. Need to mention "inventory of request modifiers" in troubleshooting guide.
- Several asks mixed "latency" and "cost" — often higher cost correlates with under-utilized batching. Suggest including a short section in quickfix docs about batching guidance.

Suggested immediate next steps (for this week):
1) Sync 30-min with Priya, Diego, Samir to align on three quick-wins and owners. (Owner: Ava)
2) Create a simple spreadsheet (columns: customer, channel, example quote, cluster, impact, proposed fix, owner). I'll seed with top 50 traces by Friday. (Owner: Ava)
3) Engineering triage meeting: serve runtime & billing to confirm feasibility and approximate story points for headers + usage export. (Owner: Priya)

Decision log (if we make choices here):
- Placeholder: If we decide to ship transparency headers only, mark decision as "transparency-first" and schedule policy UI exploration for Q1 2027.

Appendix: excerpts (raw quotes):
- "Outputs were OK 85% of time but when fallback kicked in it's a completely different tone." — Renewal call, enterprise X
- "Our cost center owners want to see per-stage token charges to avoid internal fights" — Finance escalation
- "We pin embeddings because our offline models are tuned to v2; upgrades break things" — Solutions eng notes

Misc: Links and artifacts again for easy access: JIRA-1138 (fallback transparency epic), CONFLUENCE-CC-789 (billing chargeback runbook), GitHub PR #542 (initial usage-export sketch).

If you want I can: export the top 50 trace spreadsheet, convert this into a slide 6-up for PM sync, or draft the short transparency doc for public-facing docs.

Notes to self: keep this lightweight and iteratively update. Will re-run clustering after the quick-win shipping window to spot new asks.

-- Ava

Purpose / TL;DR:
This is a working synthesis of customer feedback threads from Jul-Sep 2026 focused on request provenance (where asks come from), repeat ask patterns, and lightweight fix proposals. Goal: give PM + Eng a short list of low-effort, high-confidence patches and a provenance map so we can triage ownership. Not a final product — notes + unresolved questions inline.

How I pulled this together:
- Sources: Support tickets (top 150 by frequency), NPS followups, 1:1s, renewal calls, pilot telemetry, and 3 Slack threads (see linked_artifacts).
- Method: Rapid clustering (manual), then trace each cluster to origin channel and last-touch (who responded). Marked whether ask is: UX friction, API/behavior mismatch, observability gap, performance/cost, or security/compliance.
- Confidence: high for frequency and clear asks; medium where requests are ambiguous; low where we only have single anecdotes.

Executive summary (short bullets):
- Three recurring high-impact asks: (1) deterministic model fallback behavior for degraded capacity; (2) token-level cost attribution across multi-route calls; (3) easier way to pin embedding models for reranking pipelines.
- Quick wins (1–2 sprints): response header exposing routed-model-id + fallback-reason; a small console toggle that pins embedding family per project; add per-request token breakdown to the /usage endpoint for last 7 days.
- Longer work (quarter+): a routing policy UI for canary/fallback preferences and per-resource SLA tiers.
- Risk: several requests imply productization of internal playbooks (e.g., bespoke fallback chains) — could increase maintenance surface.

Request clusters (annotated):
1) Predictable fallback & model variants (frequency: very high)
  - Examples: enterprise pilot A: "we had 20% of calls routed to tiny-quant during peak and the outputs were wildly different"; customer B: "behavioural mismatch when fallback used distil model"
  - Channel provenance: Support tickets + renewal call; last-touch: Solutions Eng (Samir), workaround doc exists but not surfaced.
  - Impact: reliability + SLA confidence; churn risk if behavior changes silently.
  - Proposed quick fix: add response headers: model_variant, fallback_trigger, route_policy_id. Also add a transparency doc page. (Effort: small)

2) Token-level cost attribution and split billing (frequency: high)
  - Example: large customer runs a pipeline with call -> embedding -> rerank. They cannot reconcile token spend per stage and under-bill internal teams.
  - Channel provenance: Finance ticket + internal CS billing escalations. Linked to CONFLUENCE-CC-789.
  - Impact: billing disputes, internal chargeback friction.
  - Proposed quick fix: add per-request breakdown to existing /usage endpoint and a CSV export option scoped to route and model. (Effort: medium)

3) Embedding pinning and rerank stability (frequency: medium-high)
  - Example: customers report rerank instability when default embedding family upgrades propagate.
  - Channel provenance: Pilot telemetry + Slack thread.
  - Impact: regressions in downstream ranking pipelines, loss of trust.
  - Proposed quick fix: Console toggle "pin embedding family" and API flag pin_embedding_version=true for new calls. (Effort: small)

4) Observability: token latency ladders & token sampling (frequency: medium)
  - Ask: deeper per-token latency histograms and sampling traces to debug bad tails.
  - Channel provenance: SRE feedback + 2 support tickets.
  - Proposed work: add lightweight token-sampling traces to the tracing pipeline (configurable). (Effort: medium-hard)

5) Debug ergonomics: request replay and pinned inputs (frequency: low-medium)
  - Ask: sandbox replay that reproduces exact upstream route and fallback behavior.
  - Proposed: small internal tool (could be shipped as support utility). (Effort: medium)

- Several asks mixed "latency" and "cost" — often higher cost correlates with under-utilized batching. Suggest including a short section in quickfix docs about batching guidance.

title: KV-cache friction notes and customer vignettes (rapid field brief)
preview:
Purpose:
Quick synthesis of recent field conversations about KV/prefix cache behavior, tail-latency incidents, and a compact prioritized feature request list for eng/observability to triage. This is intentionally light-weight — raw vignettes + proposed low-effort probes first.

Executive summary (one-paragraph):
Multiple customers running chat-heavy pipelines report intermittent tail latency spikes that correlate with cache-miss patterns (large, variable context windows and frequent model-rolls). Customers want: (1) clearer per-customer cache hit metrics surfaced in Console/APIs, (2) a lightweight cache-warming / pinning primitive for critical prefixes, and (3) safer fallback behaviors when cache thrash occurs (e.g., short-term autoscale to avoid dropped requests or automatic model-variant fallback). These ask lanes are high impact for churn-sensitive accounts and low-to-medium implementation complexity if we scope to metrics + probe APIs first.

Field vignettes (raw quotes + context):
- Vignette A (fintech startup, dedicated): "Every few hours our chat pipeline jumps from 60ms p50 to 800-1200ms p99 for a 10–15 min window. We tracked it to cache cold starts after a background job re-ingested a batch of long chats." Notes: uses long prefix reuse, dedicated pool; worried about SLA credits.
- Vignette B (SaaS doc assistant, hosted): "We see odd sessions where the first 2–3 messages are fast, then suddenly the response time triples; our metrics show KV-cache misses at exactly that time." Notes: multi-tenant workload, dynamic prompt templates.
- Vignette C (enterprise legal, private): "We need guarantees: if KV misses spike we want automatic fallback to a smaller but available variant, not throttling that causes 503". Notes: compliance environment, no cross-tenant sharing.

Observed patterns and hypotheses: (quick bullets)
- Pattern 1: Burstiness aligned with background re-ingest or model version switch (cache invalidation semantics unclear to customers).
- Pattern 2: Large context windows + multi-turn chat produce highly skewed key distributions (few keys hot, many keys cold) — normal batching hides p50 improvements but not p99.
- Pattern 3: Customers lack visibility into per-route / per-customer cache-hit rate and KV-eviction churn.
- Hypothesis: Exposing cache metrics + a small control surface (pin/warm endpoints) will significantly reduce reported p99 incidents for top accounts.

Concrete asks collected (verbatim-ish):
1) "Per-customer cache-hit / miss metrics in Console and API, with time-series and p95/p99 of hit latency." (CS-1143)
2) "A cache-warm API: give us an endpoint to push prefixes and prepopulate the KV state ahead of critical workloads."
3) "Cache pinning for critical prefixes (TTL infinite or long-lived until unpin) for dedicated/private customers."
4) "Automatic fallbacks to a pre-configured model-variant when miss rate crosses a threshold (avoid 503s)."
5) "Alerting rule templates for cache-thrash patterns tied to usage budgets (so customers can auto-notify on anomalies)."
6) "Docs + sample code for designing prompt templates to improve locality and cache re-use (best practices)."

Quick impact x effort sketch (requested by CS):
- #1 Metrics surfaced in Console + API: Impact = HIGH, Effort = M (backend + Console panels + product QA).
- #2 Cache-warm API (push prefixes): Impact = HIGH for a subset, Effort = M (API + runtime support + auth).
- #3 Pinning (dedicated/private only): Impact = MEDIUM, Effort = L-M (policy and eviction exceptions).
- #4 Model-variant fallback policy: Impact = HIGH, Effort = M-H (routing + policy config + safety checks).
- #5 Alerting templates: Impact = MEDIUM, Effort = L (Console rule templates + docs).
- #6 Prompting playbook: Impact = MEDIUM, Effort = L (doc + examples).

Suggested MVPs (phased):
- MVP-1 (2–4 wks): Add per-customer KV hit/miss counts and simple hit-rate time-series to Console; expose API endpoint to fetch recent cache-stats for a customer.
- MVP-2 (4–8 wks): Cache-warm endpoint + example CLI script; add an agent-run job to pre-warm common prefixes during scheduled batch ingest.
- MVP-3 (8–12 wks): Pin/unpin primitives for dedicated/private; routing fallback config (policy with safe defaults).

Low-risk short-term probes (pluggable experiments):
- Probe A: Offer a 'pre-warm when ingest completes' opt-in flag in Dedicated customer config (2 days to enable + monitor).
- Probe B: Add a lightweight, visible cache_eviction gauge to runtime logs for 7 customers and correlate with CS incidents (1 sprint).
- Probe C: Ship a short 'prompt locality' playbook + 3 sample transforms to reduce key cardinality (CS-run workshop).

Open questions & engineering clarifications needed:
- Q1: Current KV eviction semantics across model-variants — do rollouts implicitly rekey caches or are there mapping layers? Need eng doc link.
- Q2: Any security/tenant-isolation constraints for pinning? Private/dedicated ok, hosted more cautious.
- Q3: Can fallback model-variants be wired into smart routing without opening routing loops or cost surprises?

Next steps (who does what):
- CS (Ava): Triage top 6 impacted customers and collect per-customer incident timelines (due 2026-10-29).
- Eng-observability (Diego): Surface a minimal per-customer cache-hit time-series API; prototype Console panel (spike 1 sprint).
- Serving-runtime (Priya): Validate feasibility of pin/unpin semantics and short design doc for fallback policies (draft by 2026-11-05).
- Product (Liam): Consider packaging as ‚Cache Assurance‘ add-on for Dedicated customers and draft pricing/SLAs (initial sketch 2026-11-12).

Appendix: sample customer timeline (anonymized)
- 2026-10-03 03:14 UTC: batch ingest job runs for Customer X (20k docs)
- 2026-10-03 03:17 UTC: p99 jump recorded (baseline 250ms -> 95th 1100ms)
- 2026-10-03 03:17–03:33 UTC: cache-miss ratio 78% (runtime logs), backlog spikes
- 2026-10-03 04:00 UTC: autoscale recovered capacity but incident cost visible in billing

Notes to self: keep this doc lightweight and iterate after eng spike. If eng confirms metrics MVP is trivial, push to customers as a preview and measure p99 before/after for 3 pilot accounts.

End of brief.

title: Surge runway simulations: tokens & QPS (2026 Q4)
preview:
Background:
This is a working runbook for simulated peak events we might see in Q4 2026. Purpose is to produce quick, actionable headroom targets (tokens/sec and QPS) and identify which GPU pools and regions to scale during 30m-2h surges. Not a finalized plan — notes + numbers are intentionally conservative and include assumptions to revisit with metrics team.

Key assumptions (baseline):
- Traffic mix: 70% short-chat (mean 40 tokens/request), 25% assistant/long-form (mean 480 tokens/request), 5% embeddings/rerank (avg 120 tokens/request).
- Avg tokens per request (weighted) = 0.7*40 + 0.25*480 + 0.05*120 = 40*0.7 + 120 + 6 = 28 + 120 + 6 = 154 tokens/request.
- Baseline QPS -> tokens/sec mapping: tokens/sec = QPS * 154.
- Effective tokens/sec per GPU (mixed fleet): see fleet mix below. These are conservative throughput numbers measured from bench runs with batch size 8 and KV cache 20% hit.

Fleet mix (active pools for hosted API):
- pool-a: H100-80GB (model-bert/large variants): 22k tokens/sec per GPU (sustained)
- pool-b: A100-80GB (general serving): 15k tokens/sec per GPU
- pool-c: L40s (cost-optimized quantized): 9k tokens/sec per GPU
- pool-d: burst T4-like (edge cheap): 4k tokens/sec per GPU (used only for short-chat cheap instances)

Region allocation (current reserved capacity):
- us-west: 120 H100 equiv GPUs (mix mapped to pools), reserved+dedicated.
- us-east: 80 H100 equiv GPUs.
- eu-west: 60 H100 equiv GPUs.
- ap-south: 24 H100 equiv GPUs (on-demand heavy).

Simulation scenarios (30-min peak windows):
1) 3x baseline QPS (localized): 3x steady-state QPS in single primary region for product marketing campaign.
2) global 1.8x baseline sustained for 2 hours (gradual growth across regions).
3) flash burst 6x baseline for 3 minutes (bot/abuse spike) with large short-chat ratio.
4) model-version regression: average tokens/request increase by 30% (e.g., longer completions) at baseline QPS.

Representative numbers (example baseline values):
- baseline global QPS = 12,000 qps -> baseline tokens/sec = 12,000 * 154 = 1,848,000 tokens/sec.
- headroom targets: safety margin = 50% for single-region peak, 30% for global sustained, emergency fallback 2.5x with degraded quality.

Scenario 1 (3x localized):
- target QPS in region = assume us-west receives 70% of traffic spike -> region QPS = 12,000 * 3 * 0.7 = 25,200 qps.
- tokens/sec = 25,200 * 154 = 3,880,800 tokens/sec.
- if served primarily by H100 (22k tok/sec/GPU): H100 GPUs required = ceil(3,880,800 / 22,000) = 177 GPUs.
- current us-west H100 equiv = 120 -> shortfall = 57 GPUs. Buffer target: reserve + burst = 80 GPUs to allow headroom and failover.

Scenario 2 (global 1.8x sustained 2h):
- global tokens/sec = 1,848,000 * 1.8 = 3,326,400 tokens/sec.
- allocate by region current split (us-west 40%, us-east 30%, eu-west 20%, ap-south 10%).
- us-west tokens/sec = 1,330,560 -> H100 eq GPUs = ceil(1,330,560 / 22,000) = 61.
- current us-west reserved (120) comfortably covers this scenario, allowing reallocation to cheaper pools for cost optimization.

Scenario 3 (flash 6x, short-chat heavy):
- flash QPS global = 12,000 * 6 = 72,000 qps. If short-chat increases to 90% and avg tokens/request drops to ~45 tokens, weighted tokens/request = 0.9*45 + 0.1*120 = 40.5 + 12 = 52.5 tokens/request.
- tokens/sec = 72,000 * 52.5 = 3,780,000 tokens/sec.
- using pool-d T4-like at 4k tok/sec/GPU for cheap short-chat: GPUs needed = ceil(3,780,000 / 4,000) = 945 GPUs -> unrealistic to burst all to T4; realistic split: 60% to L40/A100 hybrids, 40% to H100. Compute hybrid split: 2,268,000 tokens/sec -> A100/L40 path (avg 12k tok/sec) => 189 GPUs; remaining 1,512,000 tokens/sec -> H100 => 69 GPUs.

Scenario 4 (longer outputs):
- baseline tokens/request increases 30% -> 154 * 1.3 = 200.2 tokens/request. Baseline tokens/sec -> 12,000 * 200.2 = 2,402,400 tokens/sec.
- GPU impact: overall fleet demand increases by 30%; plan headroom to add +30% GPU capacity or apply aggressive caching or response-truncation policies.

Operational headroom targets (recommended):
- regional short-term headroom (30m windows): 60% over current measured 95th. This maps to 'reserve' GPUs kept unallocated in each region.
- regional sustained headroom (2h+ windows): 25% over forecasted steady-state.
- emergency burst (<=15m): ability to autoscale additional 1.5-2x on cross-region burst pool, with automatic fallbacks to quantized models.

Routing & mitigation knobs (to implement in smart routing layer):
- aggressive KV cache priming for known-hot prefixes during campaigns. Aim for 40% hit to reduce tokens/sec by ~25%.
- progressive degradation: shift 25% of traffic to quantized L40 routes when latency < target but cost limit reached.
- per-customer circuit breakers: rate-limit unknown/unverified API keys at the edge during flash events.
- response-truncation policy for long-form agents to cap max tokens and reduce tail.

Open questions / TODOs:
- validate real-world tokens/request distribution with product analytics for last 90 days (ticket JIRA-ENGINF-1428).
- run a dedicated bench with real prompt mix on L40 quantized stack to measure 99th token throughput.
- confirm procurement runway for 80 additional H100-equivalent GPUs by Nov 2026 (finance check).
- add a matched runbook for private/dedicated customers to avoid noisy-neighbor migrations during hosted surges.

Immediate next steps (owner: Ava):
- 2026-10-20: sync with metrics to pull per-customer 95th token and QPS distributions (Priya assigned).
- 2026-10-22: synthetic replay of scenario 3 with traffic replayer (Miguel lead).
- 2026-11-01: cost simulation for different burst split strategies (A100 vs L40).

Appendix: quick formula cheatsheet:
- tokens/sec = QPS * avg_tokens_per_request
- required_GPUs = ceil(tokens/sec / tokens_per_GPU)

- ap-south: 24 H100 equiv GPUs (on-demand heavy).

Simulation scenarios (30-min peak windows):
1) 3x baseline QPS (localized): 3x steady-state QPS in single primary region for product marketing campaign.
2) global 1.8x baseline sustained for 2 hours (gradual growth across regions).
3) flash burst 6x baseline for 3 minutes (bot/abuse spike) with large short-chat ratio.
4) model-version regression: average tokens/request increase by 30% (e.g., longer completions) at baseline QPS.

Representative numbers (example baseline values):
- baseline global QPS = 12,000 qps -> baseline tokens/sec = 12,000 * 154 = 1,848,000 tokens/sec.
- headroom targets: safety margin = 50% for single-region peak, 30% for global sustained, emergency fallback 2.5x with degraded quality.

Scenario 1 (3x localized):
- target QPS in region = assume us-west receives 70% of traffic spike -> region QPS = 12,000 * 3 * 0.7 = 25,200 qps.
- tokens/sec = 25,200 * 154 = 3,880,800 tokens/sec.
- if served primarily by H100 (22k tok/sec/GPU): H100 GPUs required = ceil(3,880,800 / 22,000) = 177 GPUs.
- current us-west H100 equiv = 120 -> shortfall = 57 GPUs. Buffer target: reserve + burst = 80 GPUs to allow headroom and failover.

Scenario 2 (global 1.8x sustained 2h):
- global tokens/sec = 1,848,000 * 1.8 = 3,326,400 tokens/sec.
- allocate by region current split (us-west 40%, us-east 30%, eu-west 20%, ap-south 10%).
- us-west tokens/sec = 1,330,560 -> H100 eq GPUs = ceil(1,330,560 / 22,000) = 61.
- current us-west reserved (120) comfortably covers this scenario, allowing reallocation to cheaper pools for cost optimization.

Scenario 3 (flash 6x, short-chat heavy):
- flash QPS global = 12,000 * 6 = 72,000 qps. If short-chat increases to 90% and avg tokens/request drops to ~45 tokens, weighted tokens/request = 0.9*45 + 0.1*120 = 40.5 + 12 = 52.5 tokens/request.
- tokens/sec = 72,000 * 52.5 = 3,780,000 tokens/sec.
- using pool-d T4-like at 4k tok/sec/GPU for cheap short-chat: GPUs needed = ceil(3,780,000 / 4,000) = 945 GPUs -> unrealistic to burst all to T4; realistic split: 60% to L40/A100 hybrids, 40% to H100. Compute hybrid split: 2,268,000 tokens/sec -> A100/L40 path (avg 12k tok/sec) => 189 GPUs; remaining 1,512,000 tokens/sec -> H100 => 69 GPUs.

Scenario 4 (longer outputs):
- baseline tokens/request increases 30% -> 154 * 1.3 = 200.2 tokens/request. Baseline tokens/sec -> 12,000 * 200.2 = 2,402,400 tokens/sec.
- GPU impact: overall fleet demand increases by 30%; plan headroom to add +30% GPU capacity or apply aggressive caching or response-truncation policies.

Operational headroom targets (recommended):
- regional short-term headroom (30m windows): 60% over current measured 95th. This maps to 'reserve' GPUs kept unallocated in each region.
- regional sustained headroom (2h+ windows): 25% over forecasted steady-state.
- emergency burst (<=15m): ability to autoscale additional 1.5-2x on cross-region burst pool, with automatic fallbacks to quantized models.

Routing & mitigation knobs (to implement in smart routing layer):
- aggressive KV cache priming for known-hot prefixes during campaigns. Aim for 40% hit to reduce tokens/sec by ~25%.
- progressive degradation: shift 25% of traffic to quantized L40 routes when latency < target but cost limit reached.
- per-customer circuit breakers: rate-limit unknown/unverified API keys at the edge during flash events.
- response-truncation policy for long-form agents to cap max tokens and reduce tail.

Open questions / TODOs:
- validate real-world tokens/request distribution with product analytics for last 90 days (ticket JIRA-ENGINF-1428).
- run a dedicated bench with real prompt mix on L40 quantized stack to measure 99th token throughput.
- confirm procurement runway for 80 additional H100-equivalent GPUs by Nov 2026 (finance check).
- add a matched runbook for private/dedicated customers to avoid noisy-neighbor migrations during hosted surges.

Immediate next steps (owner: Ava):
- 2026-10-20: sync with metrics to pull per-customer 95th token and QPS distributions (Priya assigned).
- 2026-10-22: synthetic replay of scenario 3 with traffic replayer (Miguel lead).
- 2026-11-01: cost simulation for different burst split strategies (A100 vs L40).

Appendix: quick formula cheatsheet:
- tokens/sec = QPS * avg_tokens_per_request
- required_GPUs = ceil(tokens/sec / tokens_per_GPU)

2026-09-29,eu-cluster-3,reserved-small,43.0,79.9,96,44,0.31,6,1,try-compaction-schedule-weekend
