2026-09-30,na-cluster-7,spot-mixed,29.0,64.7,175,135,0.44,18,7,aggressive-slot-defrag-weekend

QUICK OBSERVATIONS:
- EU cluster archive-slices show very high fragmentation_index (0.68) driven by a large number of idle tiny slots; candidate for slice decommissioning or reclassification.
- NA reserved-small pool is repeatedly under-utilized during day windows (avg ~40%) with consistent idle slots; compaction recommended during 02:00-05:00 local to NA to avoid disrupting PLG latency windows.
- APAC preemptible pool has high microburst activity and preemptions; suggests workload pattern is batch-heavy and would benefit from opportunistic burst policy rather than steady preemptible allocation.
- spot-mixed pools show recurring p95 spikes indicating occasional high-concurrency jobs; consolidation may increase p95 tail unless autoscaling buffers are tuned.

RECOMMENDATIONS (actionable items):
1) Schedule a weekend compaction run (drain small idle slices in eu-cluster-3 archive-slices) - estimated reclaimed capacity: ~28 GPUs.
2) Increase slot compaction aggressiveness for reserved-small in na-cluster-7: lower threshold from 60% to 50% for nightly compaction; run can be disabled via toggle x-compaction if latency alerts fire.
3) Deploy a small burst-policy experiment in apac preemptible: increase ephemeral cap by 20% and add soft backfill FIFO for batch windows (metrics to measure: preemption_count, job_retries).
4) For spot-mixed pools, add short-lived cushion nodes to absorb p95 spikes instead of fragmenting small slots; track p95_reduction over 2 weeks.
5) Add a daily lightweight report to ops Slack (#ops-gpu-capacity) with top 3 pools by fragmentation_index to catch regressions early.

NOTES / OPEN QUESTIONS:
- Fragmentation index calculation: current heuristic may over-count heterogeneous slot sizes; propose a follow-up to test a weight-normalized metric (ticket JIRA-INFRA-1358).
- Some compaction suggestions will conflict with customer SLA reservations; coordinate with customer-success for any pool merges.

APPENDIX: weekly aggregates (by pool)

title: DevEx scavenger sprint fieldnotes — quickstarts & SDK picks
preview:
Meeting: DevEx — Scavenger Sprint field notes (quickstarts / SDK / samples)
Date: 2026-09-21
Attendees: Ava (owner), Marco, Priya, Jonah, Sasha, Maya (observer)

Purpose: Rapidly surface friction points in the new quickstart flows and lightweight SDK samples. This sprint day was focused on trying the quickstarts end-to-end on macOS and Linux, and running the JS + Python sample apps with minimal credentials.

Summary highlights:
- Overall: quickstarts run but several gotchas for first-run developers (auth, env vars, package versions). Good baseline but not PLG-ready without small fixes.
- Biggest UX blockers today: opaque auth error messages; sample repo has 2 outdated deps; README example for streaming uses old API shape.
- Positive: containerized quickstart (docker-compose) worked consistently for team members with constrained envs — keep as fallback.

Observations / raw notes:
- Onboarding time (rough, manual stopwatch): fresh machine -> success run: 
  - Python quickstart (virtualenv): ~18-28 minutes depending on pip cache. 
  - Node quickstart (nvm + pnpm): ~14-22 minutes but more fragile on Node 20 due to optional native deps.
- Common error seen: 401 with body: "invalid key" (no hint about which step failed). Needs mapping to troubleshooting doc.
- CLI sample (alpha): installs, but telemetry opt-in prompt blocks CI smoke test; add --yes flag.
- Examples folder structure: samples/js/chat/basic -> uses deprecated function call. Devs expected stream() but current example uses legacy generate().

User journeys tested (quick checklist):
- Fresh clone -> README follow -> run -> PASS/FAIL: 6/10 (failures mainly due to env var naming mismatch and token formatting).
- Using sandbox token (team-provided) -> 9/10 success, but streaming examples frequently hang on long responses (timeout not surfaced).
- Containerized run -> 10/10 for macOS M1 with docker for mac, but image size could be trimmed.

Decisions / short-term fixes (agreed):
1) Auth error messages: add a small mapping in sdk-exceptions to surface: "possible cause: missing REDWOOD_API_KEY or extra quotes". Owner: Marco. ETA: Sep 24.
2) Update quickstarts README headings and explicit env var names (REDWOOD_API_KEY, REDWOOD_API_HOST). Owner: Priya. ETA: Sep 23.
3) Add --yes flag to CLI sample to bypass telemetry prompt for CI. Owner: Jonah. PR created: linked_artifacts. ETA: Sep 22.
4) Rename streaming example to streaming-v2 and include note about API shape change + migration snippet. Owner: Sasha. ETA: Sep 27.

Action items (concrete):
- Marco: patch SDK error -> create PR with clearer auth guidance; include reproduction snippet; label as patch. (JIRA-DEVEXP-1023)
- Priya: quickstarts README sweep -> replace ambiguous steps, add copy-paste env block, verify on node/python matrix. Add note about lens for retries on flaky networks.
- Jonah: CLI --yes implementation + update CI smoke test to run headless; open PR (see github link).
- Sasha: samples refactor for streaming example; keep legacy example but mark deprecated with migration notes.
- Maya: run automated install matrix tomorrow across node 18/20, python 3.10/3.11 and report flaky deps.
- Ava: compile a short troubleshooting FAQ to land in repo root and Console docs that lists 5 most common first-run errors.

Follow-ups / things to validate next standup:
- Confirm PRs landed and quickstarts pass fresh install on at least 3 dev machines.
- Measure first-run time before/after README changes to show delta.
- Add a small e2e smoke test that runs one example with a sandbox token in GH Actions. (blocked until CLI --yes is merged)

Notes to self / informal scraps:
- Consider adding a tiny web-based playground (codesandbox) for the JS quickstart — lowers barrier for non-dev reviewers.
- Quickstarts should include a one-liner: "What to expect (30m): install, run, explore" so PMs and early users get realistic timing.
- Potential quick win: script that validates env var format and prints tailored hint. Save as scripts/validate_env.sh

Risks / unknowns:
- On-prem / private deployment docs: need separate token guidance — we currently assume hosted API shape. Make sure quickstarts warn about private host config.
- Some dependency pins are cautious (old libs) — look into why (compat vs security).

Metrics snapshot (hand-collected, approximate):
- Quickstart repo stars/internal bookmarks: 42
- Fresh-run success rate (team manual sample): 60% -> target 90% by next week
- Avg time-to-success (median): ~20 minutes -> target <10 with better docs + one-click container

Open questions for product/SWRE review:
- Are we comfortable with keeping the container image as the canonical reproducible path? Pros: stable. Cons: larger bandwidth.
- For streaming, how tolerant should examples be to long streams (longer than 30s)? Add guidance on socket timeouts?

Appendix: quicklinks (work-in-progress)
- Draft FAQ/README template: CONFLUENCE-DEVEXP-quickstarts-sprint
- PR (CLI): https://github.com/redwood-inference/sdk/pull/456
- Reproduction notes for auth errors: stash in JIRA-DEVEXP-1023

Endnotes: rough, half-baked notes intended as a capture for sprint day. Will tidy once PRs land and Maya's matrix is done.

---
ACTION CHECK: Make sure owners put quick PR link into this doc when ready so we can close JIRA-DEVEXP-1023.

title: muon-feedback-assembler-and-sprint-traction-2026-07-28
preview:
Executive summary:

This note collates 32 customer touchpoints (support threads, partner calls, and in-product telemetry) into a compact set of problem statements and low-effort probe ideas we can land in the next 2–6 weeks. Goal: reduce top-two friction signals feeding into latency/cost complaints and create measurable improvement metrics we can report in the Aug 12 customer forum.

Why \"muon\"?
- Lightweight name for a focused deliverable: fast-moving, penetrating the noise.
- Use when we want short cycles: observe -> small fix -> measure -> iterate.

Context & scope:
- Data sources: support tickets (126–140 range), three enterprise pilot interviews (FinLens, HealthOps, ChatMate), two SRE on-call digests, and token-cost telemetry from hosted-api per-customer rollups.
- Scope: inference-side behavioral asks (not billing/package changes). Primarily kv-cache ergonomics, batching defaults, and model-fallback UX.
- Timebox: this is a sprint-sized synthesis meant to seed 3 micro-experiments.

Top signal clusters (ranked by frequency + customer pain):
1) Short-lived cold-start latency spikes (prefixs that miss KV cache)
   - Raw notes: \"first request after idle is 5-8x slower; users think it's broken\" (FinLens).
   - Observed frequency: daily spikes for ~12% of active customer routes.
2) Unclear model fallback behavior when a requested model is not available in region
   - Quote: \"The SDK silently switched to a smaller model and our hallucination rate doubled\" (ChatMate pilot).
   - Pain: reliability + output quality regressions without clear telemetry.
3) Token cost surprises from aggressive sampling/batch sizing defaults
   - Customers report unexpected daily spend due to large batch sizes for async pipelines.
   - Misalignment: default batching favors throughput but not predictable unit cost for low-volume tenants.
4) Embeddings drift after quantization profile change
   - Edge reports: \"search recall dropped after a server-side quant tweak\" (HealthOps).

Representative customer vignettes (short):
- FinLens (mid-market analytics): Intermittent slow responses during daily report generation cause human-in-the-loop frustration. They want an \"always-warm\" option for critical routes.
- ChatMate (AI chat SaaS): Careful about model parity. When fallbacks happen they need an explicit hook to reject responses rather than silently accept lower-quality ones.
- HealthOps (HIPAA customer): Slight embedding quality shifts result in clinically meaningful misses; they want deterministic rollout windows + opt-out.

Synthesis: root causes & hypothesis
- Root cause A: KV cache warming policy is too conservative for low-volume, high-latency-sensitive routes. Hypothesis: relaxing warm-up thresholds or providing a configurable warm-up policy will reduce cold-start spikes by 60–80% for affected routes.
- Root cause B: Fallback routing lacks explicit telemetry and explicit quality-mode choices. Hypothesis: adding a per-route \"fallback-mode\" (reject | degrade-with-alert | auto-fallback) will give customers control and reduce silent regressions.
- Root cause C: Batching defaults are optimized for throughput not predictable per-token cost. Hypothesis: expose a \"cost-predictable\" profile that adjusts batching heuristics and logs expected unit cost before execution.

Quick experiment lineup (3 prioritized probes)
1) Warmup-lite: per-route warm-up toggles + a 5-minute sticky-preheat policy
   - Implementation sketch: route config flag warmup_policy = [off|default|preheat-5m]. When preheat-5m is enabled, background short-prefix keepalive requests are scheduled when route is idle > 30s.
   - Success metric: median first-token latency for target routes drops by 50% on warmup-enabled customers.
   - Estimated effort: 3 dev days + 2 QA days (serving-runtime + console toggle).
   - Quick rollback: revert toggle; stops background tasks.
2) Fallback transparency & opt-in rejection mode
   - Implementation sketch: SDK server returns code fallback_occurred + model_used header. Add route boolean reject_on_fallback. If set, API returns 409 with diagnostics instead of degraded result.
   - Success metric: zero silent-quality regressions for pilot customers; adoption metric = % routes enabling reject_on_fallback.
   - Estimated effort: 4 dev days (API surface + client SDK handling) + product doc.
3) Cost-predictable profile (preview)
   - Implementation sketch: New profile profile_name = \"cost-predictable\" that reduces max_batch_size, lowers async-buffer-window, and logs an estimated unit-cost preflight when run in \"preview\" mode.
   - Success metric: customers report <10% variance between estimated and realized per-token cost for previewed runs.
   - Estimated effort: 5 dev days + console UX add.

Low-effort patches (can be pushed in days)
- Console: Add explicit model-fallback banner on route page showing last 30-day fallback events. (1 FE day)
- Docs: FAQ entry \"Why did my route switch models?\" with steps to opt into reject_on_fallback. (0.5 day)
- Support playbook: template email + auto-snippet to escalate high-severity cost-surprise tickets. (0.5 day)

Measurement plan
- Baseline KPIs: first-token P50/P95 for affected routes (from telemetry), fallback events per 10k requests, per-route token cost variance (7-day rolling).
- Target windows: 7-day rolling windows; report at Aug 12 customer forum.
- Dashboard: temporary dashboard card in Console -> Route -> Signals showing warmup status, fallback count, and estimated cost variance.

Risks & mitigations
- Risk: background preheat increases GPU bill for low-value routes. Mitigation: preheat only runs if route has >X revenue/requests or explicitly enabled by customer. Use budget cap per route.
- Risk: reject_on_fallback causes visible errors for user-facing apps. Mitigation: default is off; provide safe mode and clear error handling docs.

Open questions (needs input from Eng/Product):
- What is an acceptable budget cap for preheat per route before we require a reserved-capacity flag? (Eng infra input)
- Can the SDK/console expose preflight cost estimates without leaking internal cost models? (Security/product)
- For private deployments, what telemetry is permissible for fallback banners? (Compliance)

Recommended owners & immediate next steps (week 0–2)
- Owner: Ava Chen (CS) — coordinate pilots and customer comms.
- Eng lead: Marcus Patel (serving-runtime) — scope warmup-lite feasibility.
- Product PM: Priya Rao (product-hosted-api) — decide fallback UX and cost-profile flags.
- Week 0: quick alignment call (30m) with Eng/Product/SRE + 3 pilot customers.
- Week 1: land console banner + doc FAQ.
- Week 2: implement warmup-lite toggle behind feature flag for 2 pilot customers.

Appendix: snippets and raw signals (extracts)
- Support ticket #12984 (FinLens): \"After lunch idle period, report generation times out. Restarting helps — looks like a warm cache requirement.\"
- Slack clip (SRE): \"noticed 02:13 rollout correlated with quant tweak and small embedding shift for health-prod-1\"
- Pilot call quote (ChatMate): \"We lost a build of trust when the assistant started making stuff up — hard to debug when we see no fallback logs.\"

Notes to self / tactical items:
- Tag telemetry team to add fallback_occurred boolean event to ingestion schema (ticket: JIRA-INC-3482).
- Put preheat-policy in a config RFC and move to eng backlog if pilot metrics look good.

End of note.

-- Ava

(If you want a slide-quick version for Aug 12, tell me; I can produce a 5-slide deck: problem, data, probes, risks, ask.)
text:
Executive summary:

This note collates 32 customer touchpoints (support threads, partner calls, and in-product telemetry) into a compact set of problem statements and low-effort probe ideas we can land in the next 2–6 weeks. Goal: reduce top-two friction signals feeding into latency/cost complaints and create measurable improvement metrics we can report in the Aug 12 customer forum.

Why \"muon\"?
- Lightweight name for a focused deliverable: fast-moving, penetrating the noise.
- Use when we want short cycles: observe -> small fix -> measure -> iterate.

Context & scope:
- Data sources: support tickets (126–140 range), three enterprise pilot interviews (FinLens, HealthOps, ChatMate), two SRE on-call digests, and token-cost telemetry from hosted-api per-customer rollups.
- Scope: inference-side behavioral asks (not billing/package changes). Primarily kv-cache ergonomics, batching defaults, and model-fallback UX.
- Timebox: this is a sprint-sized synthesis meant to seed 3 micro-experiments.

Top signal clusters (ranked by frequency + customer pain):
1) Short-lived cold-start latency spikes (prefixs that miss KV cache)
   - Raw notes: \"first request after idle is 5-8x slower; users think it's broken\" (FinLens).
   - Observed frequency: daily spikes for ~12% of active customer routes.
2) Unclear model fallback behavior when a requested model is not available in region
   - Quote: \"The SDK silently switched to a smaller model and our hallucination rate doubled\" (ChatMate pilot).
   - Pain: reliability + output quality regressions without clear telemetry.
3) Token cost surprises from aggressive sampling/batch sizing defaults
   - Customers report unexpected daily spend due to large batch sizes for async pipelines.
   - Misalignment: default batching favors throughput but not predictable unit cost for low-volume tenants.
4) Embeddings drift after quantization profile change
   - Edge reports: \"search recall dropped after a server-side quant tweak\" (HealthOps).

Representative customer vignettes (short):
- FinLens (mid-market analytics): Intermittent slow responses during daily report generation cause human-in-the-loop frustration. They want an \"always-warm\" option for critical routes.
- ChatMate (AI chat SaaS): Careful about model parity. When fallbacks happen they need an explicit hook to reject responses rather than silently accept lower-quality ones.
- HealthOps (HIPAA customer): Slight embedding quality shifts result in clinically meaningful misses; they want deterministic rollout windows + opt-out.

Synthesis: root causes & hypothesis
- Root cause A: KV cache warming policy is too conservative for low-volume, high-latency-sensitive routes. Hypothesis: relaxing warm-up thresholds or providing a configurable warm-up policy will reduce cold-start spikes by 60–80% for affected routes.
- Root cause B: Fallback routing lacks explicit telemetry and explicit quality-mode choices. Hypothesis: adding a per-route \"fallback-mode\" (reject | degrade-with-alert | auto-fallback) will give customers control and reduce silent regressions.
- Root cause C: Batching defaults are optimized for throughput not predictable per-token cost. Hypothesis: expose a \"cost-predictable\" profile that adjusts batching heuristics and logs expected unit cost before execution.

Quick experiment lineup (3 prioritized probes)
1) Warmup-lite: per-route warm-up toggles + a 5-minute sticky-preheat policy
   - Implementation sketch: route config flag warmup_policy = [off|default|preheat-5m]. When preheat-5m is enabled, background short-prefix keepalive requests are scheduled when route is idle > 30s.
   - Success metric: median first-token latency for target routes drops by 50% on warmup-enabled customers.
   - Estimated effort: 3 dev days + 2 QA days (serving-runtime + console toggle).
   - Quick rollback: revert toggle; stops background tasks.
2) Fallback transparency & opt-in rejection mode
   - Implementation sketch: SDK server returns code fallback_occurred + model_used header. Add route boolean reject_on_fallback. If set, API returns 409 with diagnostics instead of degraded result.
   - Success metric: zero silent-quality regressions for pilot customers; adoption metric = % routes enabling reject_on_fallback.
   - Estimated effort: 4 dev days (API surface + client SDK handling) + product doc.
3) Cost-predictable profile (preview)
   - Implementation sketch: New profile profile_name = \"cost-predictable\" that reduces max_batch_size, lowers async-buffer-window, and logs an estimated unit-cost preflight when run in \"preview\" mode.
   - Success metric: customers report <10% variance between estimated and realized per-token cost for previewed runs.
   - Estimated effort: 5 dev days + console UX add.

Low-effort patches (can be pushed in days)
- Console: Add explicit model-fallback banner on route page showing last 30-day fallback events. (1 FE day)
- Docs: FAQ entry \"Why did my route switch models?\" with steps to opt into reject_on_fallback. (0.5 day)
- Support playbook: template email + auto-snippet to escalate high-severity cost-surprise tickets. (0.5 day)

Measurement plan
- Baseline KPIs: first-token P50/P95 for affected routes (from telemetry), fallback events per 10k requests, per-route token cost variance (7-day rolling).
- Target windows: 7-day rolling windows; report at Aug 12 customer forum.
- Dashboard: temporary dashboard card in Console -> Route -> Signals showing warmup status, fallback count, and estimated cost variance.

Risks & mitigations
- Risk: background preheat increases GPU bill for low-value routes. Mitigation: preheat only runs if route has >X revenue/requests or explicitly enabled by customer. Use budget cap per route.
- Risk: reject_on_fallback causes visible errors for user-facing apps. Mitigation: default is off; provide safe mode and clear error handling docs.

Open questions (needs input from Eng/Product):
- What is an acceptable budget cap for preheat per route before we require a reserved-capacity flag? (Eng infra input)
- Can the SDK/console expose preflight cost estimates without leaking internal cost models? (Security/product)
- For private deployments, what telemetry is permissible for fallback banners? (Compliance)

Recommended owners & immediate next steps (week 0–2)
- Owner: Ava Chen (CS) — coordinate pilots and customer comms.
- Eng lead: Marcus Patel (serving-runtime) — scope warmup-lite feasibility.
- Product PM: Priya Rao (product-hosted-api) — decide fallback UX and cost-profile flags.
- Week 0: quick alignment call (30m) with Eng/Product/SRE + 3 pilot customers.
- Week 1: land console banner + doc FAQ.
- Week 2: implement warmup-lite toggle behind feature flag for 2 pilot customers.

Appendix: snippets and raw signals (extracts)
- Support ticket #12984 (FinLens): \"After lunch idle period, report generation times out. Restarting helps — looks like a warm cache requirement.\"
- Slack clip (SRE): \"noticed 02:13 rollout correlated with quant tweak and small embedding shift for health-prod-1\"
- Pilot call quote (ChatMate): \"We lost a build of trust when the assistant started making stuff up — hard to debug when we see no fallback logs.\"

Notes to self / tactical items:
- Tag telemetry team to add fallback_occurred boolean event to ingestion schema (ticket: JIRA-INC-3482).
- Put preheat-policy in a config RFC and move to eng backlog if pilot metrics look good.

End of note.

- Action: Draft updated terms for FinEdge showing 12-month commit + 5% discount bands (owner: Owen) — draft to Legal (ticket LEG-933) by 2026-07-16.
- Question: RetailCo pilot end date — confirm with AE; if pilot ends before 2026-07-30, prepare a targeted retention offer.
- Note: Consider running a micro-sim: 3 scenarios (conservative, base, optimistic) where probabilities are scaled by 0.75/1.0/1.2 to generate ARR capture ranges; embed column "Chorus scenario" to show variant outputs.

- Optimistic capture estimate = weighted ARR * 1.2 = $1,602,774

Meeting prep (QBR touchpoints):
- Create 10-minute briefing slides for execs focused on top-5 deals affecting ARR > $300k — attach to QBR deck.
- Template: talk track = (1) current capture estimate, (2) top blockers, (3) proposed commitments (legal/tech/discount), (4) ask for exec sponsor or SLA carveout.

Notes to self (informal):
- This sheet is intentionally compact — we'll split to per-region tabs if >50 rows.
- Keep a short audit trail of pricing offers (link to doc store) to avoid re-negotiation mismatches.

References / links to playbooks:
- dedicated-capacity-play (confluence link above)
- private-deployments-checklist (confluence)
- churn-mitigation-kit (CS playbook)

- 2026-07-15: probability updates after AE outreach (Diego)
- 2026-07-20: added weighted ARR summary + open actions (Ava)

title: SDK async digest - firebreak notes and action sync
preview:
Weekly async digest + firebreak notes (short form)

Meeting context:
- Purpose: lightweight firebreak/standup when most folks are heads-down on the quickstart push. Capture blockers, decisions, action-owners for next week. Keep it very pragmatic.
- Attendees (rotating): Ava, Liam, Priya, Diego (+ async contributions from Sophie and Noor).
- Timeframe covered: 2026-07-07 -> 2026-07-14

Top outcomes (TL;DR):
1) Quickstart v2 template merged into sdk-templates (PR#88) but docs still need ergonomics pass.
2) Samples lab infra prototype stable on staging; cost estimate spike needed before can scale to CI.
3) Decision: postpone CLI telemetry change to next sprint to avoid regressions in onboarding funnel.

Notes / progress by area:
- Quickstarts and onboarding
  - PR merged: https://github.com/redwood-inference/sdk-templates/pull/88 (Liam) - includes Python + Node minimal flows, streaming example, function calling skeleton.
  - Docs gap: quickstart README has code snippets but no troubleshooting section for common env issues (CUDA vs CPU). Action: Priya will draft a troubleshooting FAQ (target: 2026-07-20).
  - Onboarding metrics: funnel drop at "run example" step persists for Windows users. Unclear if it's doc clarity or packaging. Need telemetry.

- SDK APIs and ergonomics
  - Beta API change: shortlist of breaking changes for tokenization helpers. After discussion, we will add adapter layer rather than breaking users. Action: Ava to author adapter shim RFC by 2026-07-21.
  - Node SDK: failing CI on ARM runners intermittently — suspected flakiness in native bindings. Diego to pair with infra to capture reproducible trace (JIRA-DEVEXP-421).

- Samples & templates
  - Samples lab: prototype uses ephemeral containers + cached model artifacts. Performance looks reasonable on m6i-like hosts, but cost for larger sequences spikes. Need capacity planning. Action: Sophie to run a 72-hour cost mock and report by 2026-07-23.
  - New sample: "redwood-chatbot-starter" skeleton (typescript + serverless) has a TODO to wire up streaming UI. Priya will add a minimal client example and screenshots for the README.

- Docs and quick wins
  - Doc ergonomics pass: reduce long code blocks and add "copy runnable" buttons. We should try the snapshot plugin from docs repo next week. Action: Liam to prototype on one page (quickstart-python) and share screenshots by 2026-07-19.
  - Internal FAQ: compile the top 10 support questions from last quarter into a short doc to seed troubleshooting. Diego to pull support convo IDs.

Blockers / risks
- Telemetry gap: onboarding failure points are opaque because we do not capture environment metadata at run-example step. This blocks prioritization. Mitigation: add lightweight opt-in telemetry in SDK that exports environment + error codes (opt-out by default). Security review required. Action: create security checklist ticket and loop in security by 2026-07-16 (Ava).
- CI cost cap: samples lab scalability will hit budget if run unrestricted. Need spike and guardrails.
- Node/native flakiness: blocks some e2e tests on CI; risk to release cadence.

Action items (owner / due / notes)
- Priya Nair / 2026-07-20 / Draft troubleshooting FAQ for quickstart README (include Windows notes + sample env vars)
- Ava Chen / 2026-07-21 / RFC for adapter shim for tokenization helpers (aim to avoid breaking change)
- Liam O'Rourke / 2026-07-19 / Prototype docs snapshot with copy-run buttons (quickstart-python)
- Sophie Martinez / 2026-07-23 / 72-hour cost mock for samples lab (include cloud cost per token/seq-length)
- Diego Morales / 2026-07-18 / Repro trace for Node ARM CI flake (attach logs to JIRA-DEVEXP-421)
- Ava Chen / 2026-07-16 / Create security checklist ticket for opt-in telemetry and ping security team

Open questions / decisions needed
- Do we accept opt-in telemetry in the SDK for debugging onboarding? (needs security + privacy signoff)
- How aggressively do we reduce sample permutations? (tradeoff: coverage vs cost/time-to-onboard)
- Windows packaging: do we bake a Conda env or document WSL as the recommended path?

Notes to self (rough)
- If telemetry accepted: default to anonymized env metadata (os, node/python version, whether GPU available), capture stacktrace + minimal error code; store in short-lived S3 bucket with 30-day TTL; ensure KMS-managed keys.
- Consider a small onboarding script that runs a smoke check and prints human-friendly hints ("GPU not detected -> run this command"). Could be shipped as a single-file appliance in quickstart.

Meeting attachments / refs
- quickstart-python.md (draft) -> link is in PR#88 + Liam's branch
- samples-lab-proto.md (internal) -> Sophie
- JIRA-DEVEXP-421 -> Node ARM flake trace

Next sync (async):
- Quick check-in next Thursday (2026-07-22). If major security items surface, schedule a short live huddle.

Misc (brain dump)
- Try a tiny UX test: ask 5 external new users to run quickstart and record time-to-first-response. Can do as a small recruiting job with community contributors.
- Low-hanging UX: move config examples to top-level code block and avoid nested explanation until after the runnable snippet.

End notes: keep this doc as a living async digest during the firebreak. If you add comments, prefix with your name and date so it's easy to attribute.

-- Ava

- Windows packaging: do we bake a Conda env or document WSL as the recommended path?

Notes to self (rough)
- If telemetry accepted: default to anonymized env metadata (os, node/python version, whether GPU available), capture stacktrace + minimal error code; store in short-lived S3 bucket with 30-day TTL; ensure KMS-managed keys.
- Consider a small onboarding script that runs a smoke check and prints human-friendly hints ("GPU not detected -> run this command"). Could be shipped as a single-file appliance in quickstart.

Meeting attachments / refs
- quickstart-python.md (draft) -> link is in PR#88 + Liam's branch
- samples-lab-proto.md (internal) -> Sophie
- JIRA-DEVEXP-421 -> Node ARM flake trace

Next sync (async):
- Quick check-in next Thursday (2026-07-22). If major security items surface, schedule a short live huddle.

Misc (brain dump)
- Try a tiny UX test: ask 5 external new users to run quickstart and record time-to-first-response. Can do as a small recruiting job with community contributors.
- Low-hanging UX: move config examples to top-level code block and avoid nested explanation until after the runnable snippet.

End notes: keep this doc as a living async digest during the firebreak. If you add comments, prefix with your name and date so it's easy to attribute.

-- Ava

title: Incident customer FAQ + decision board: Partial tokenization latency spike (draft)
preview:
Purpose and audience: This is a customer-facing draft intended for (1) the public status page short update, (2) a longer post-incident FAQ that we can send to affected customers, and (3) a short decision board so customers can choose next steps (replay, opt-in compensation, or hands-off closure). Treat as draft: placeholders for timing/metrics are flagged with <>. Internal reviewers: please sanity-check the timeline and the suggested compensation options before we send.

1) One-line status page snippet (90–140 chars):
Partial tokenization service latency spike affecting a subset of requests; degraded throughput for short-lived sessions. Root cause under investigation; mitigations in progress.

2) Longer status page / customer alert (for status.redwood.ai):
We experienced a partial latency spike in our tokenization and prefixes cache tier starting at <2026-07-14 10:12 UTC> that affected approximately <3%> of generation requests for ~125 minutes. While the majority of requests continued to return successful responses, affected requests saw higher tail latency and occasional timeouts. Our engineers applied a staged cache reset and routing policy at <11:57 UTC>, and full normal traffic routing resumed by <12:17 UTC>.
What customers saw: slower responses (100–1,500 ms additional latency) and in a small number of cases failed requests that were retried by our SDKs. Streaming sessions were also intermittently impacted, with certain streams pausing briefly before resuming.

3) Plain-language post-incident summary for customers (2–3 paragraphs):
What happened: A change in our token prefix caching subsystem caused an uneven eviction pattern that overwhelmed a subset of backend nodes responsible for initial token lookups. That led to higher queuing and elevated tail latency for a subset of requests, most commonly short, bursty sessions and requests using certain model variants.
What we did: We detected the pattern via alerting on token lookup P95/P99, scaled an emergency routing rule to avoid the hot nodes, and performed a controlled cache reset with a short warmup window. We also rolled back a recent cache allocation tweak that contributed to the uneven eviction behavior.
Impact: The incident affected ~3% of requests during the window and increased the P99 latency for those requests by up to 1.5s. No customer data loss or model output integrity issues were detected.

4) Technical summary (for customers who request more detail):
- Root cause: regression in cache partitioning logic (new heuristic introduced in /repo/serving-runtime@deploy/2026-07-14-03) that under certain request distributions concentrated hot prefixes on a subset of nodes.
- Failure mode: cache eviction storms that caused synchronous token store lookups to back up request processing threads; coupled with an inadvertent downgrade in async queue capacity due to a config flip.
- Why some customers were unaffected: traffic pattern and model variant mix — customers with long-lived streaming sessions and broader sequence reuse primarily hit warm caches and saw minimal impact.

5) Timeline (concise):
- 10:12 UTC — Alerts triggered: token-lookup P99 and queue depth spike (S2).
- 10:18 UTC — Pager escalations; engineers begin triage, correlate to recent cache config change.
- 10:34 UTC — Short-term routing rule applied to bypass 3 affected nodes for new sessions (reduced error rate by ~40%).
- 11:02 UTC — Investigations identify cache partitioning heuristic as likely cause; prepare rollback plan.
- 11:57 UTC — Controlled cache reset + rollback of heuristic pushed.
- 12:17 UTC — Routing rules removed; traffic flow normalized; monitoring confirms stabilizing P99.
- 12:45 UTC — Post-mortem capture and customer-facing draft begins.

6) Evidence and customer verification:
If you need logs or request traces for replay/verification, we can provide a package containing (a) request ids (range) observed during your org's peak window, (b) latency histograms, and (c) evidence that model outputs were unchanged for retried requests. Submit a request to cs-incident-requests@redwood.ai with your tenant id and preferred time window.

7) Recommended customer actions (decision board):
Option A — Auto-closure (default): No action needed. We consider this incident resolved; no replay or credits unless you believe you saw customer-facing impact.
Option B — Opt-in replay and verification: If you want us to replay affected requests (best-effort, non-destructive) and provide a verification package showing outputs and hashes, opt in by replying to this message with your tenant id. Expected turnaround: 2–5 business days.
Option C — Accountability package + one-time credit: If you experienced measurable business impact (failed customer flows, revenue impact), opt in to receive a tailored incident accountability package plus a discretionary one-time service credit. We will ask for impact evidence during intake.

8) Suggested short FAQ (copy-pasteable for customer emails):
Q: Were my prompts or data exposed?
A: No. This was a performance incident; there is no indication of data exfiltration or integrity issues.

Q: Will you automatically retry my failed requests?
A: We do not automatically replay customer requests without consent. The SDK includes transparent retry behavior for idempotent requests; where retries occurred they are logged. If you want a full replay of affected requests, choose Option B.

Q: How do I know which requests were affected?
A: Provide your tenant id and a time window (we recommend +-20 minutes around when you observed issues) and we will produce a request-id list and latency histogram for verification.

Q: Is this likely to recur?
A: The problematic heuristic has been removed and we have added a canary test and a traffic pattern emulator to catch this specific partitioning regression. We also tightened alert thresholds and added a secondary async queue fallback. Residual risk is low but not zero; we will continue monitoring.

9) Customer-facing templates (pick one):
- Short status update for your status page: "Partial latency spike in tokenization service between <10:12–12:17 UTC>. Most requests unaffected. We're offering opt-in replay and verification"
- Email/notice snippet to customers: "We experienced a partial latency incident that affected a small percentage of requests. No data loss. If you saw impact, reply to this message to request a replay or credits."

10) Intake form and next steps (internal):
If a customer selects Option B or C, CS should open INC-4721-<tenant> and tag with "replay-request" or "credit-intake". Provide the customer intake checklist: tenant id, observed times, example request ids (if available), business impact estimate (for credit requests), POC contact. Assign to CS engineer and inform eng-incidents and eng-applied-ml channels.

11) Internal notes for reviewers (do not send to customers):
- Confirm compensation policy for incidents with <3% request impact before offering credits. Proposed credit band: $500–$5,000 depending on demonstrated impact; escalate beyond that to customer-sfx.
- Ensure replay is rate-limited and does not re-trigger downstream caches or billing anomalies. Use a controlled replay worker pool.
- Engineering to provide replay script, hash verification method, and a list of request ids scoped per-tenant.

12) Approvals required: CS lead, Incident Commander, Eng-ServRuntime manager.

13) Sign-off and suggested send cadence:
- Quick status page update now (short snippet).
- Detailed email to affected customers in 1–2 hours after sign-off (use the FAQ and decision board).
- Follow-up with a full postmortem and code-level mitigation plan within 72 hours.

Draft end. Please comment inline on any phrasing that sounds too technical for customer audiences or on the proposed credit bands.

title: slice-level utilization sampler - weekly rollup
preview:
Purpose:
Provide a compact, slice-level weekly sampler of GPU utilization across a representative subset of clusters and pools. Not a full fleet export — this is intended for quick triage and to highlight latent fragmentation / microburst patterns that the daily totals miss.

Notes on methodology:
- Sampling window: 2026-07-02T00:00 -> 2026-07-08T23:59 (ISO week)
- Sample selection: 18 clusters chosen to cover large/medium/small pools, one cluster per region priority (NA/EU/APAC) and 3 cross-region multi-pool clusters.
- Metric aggregation: per-pool per-cluster rolling 5m avg, median, p95, peak-minute, and microburst_count (>= 80% -> 100% spike within 60s).
- Fragmentation index: heuristic = 1 - (sum(continuous-slot-chunks)/total-available-slots) -> 0..1 where higher means more fragmentation.
- Cold-starts excluded for pools with >50% cold-start rate during window (see notes column).

TABLE (comma-separated for sheet import):
Cluster,Pool,Region,WeekAvgUtil%,Median%,P95%,PeakMinute%,MicroburstCount,FragIndex,WoWDelta%,Notes
cl-nyc-01,high-priority-48,na,72.4,71.8,96.2,100.0,14,0.31,+3.2,"steady high load; 80% of bursts tied to interactive inference routes"
cl-sfo-03,shared-small-8,na,34.7,30.1,62.9,84.0,5,0.58,-6.5,"fragmentation high; small pools starved midday"
cl-lon-02,dedicated-64,eu,88.9,89.0,98.7,100.0,28,0.12,+1.1,"predictable steady throughput; low fragmentation"
cl-fra-01,batch-16,eu,22.5,18.3,47.6,64.0,2,0.67,-4.0,"mostly batch; long tails during nightly jobs"
cl-sin-04,mixed-32,apac,61.2,59.9,87.3,98.0,9,0.39,+5.6,"noticeable surge during APAC morning; consider burst pool"
cl-syd-01,edge-4,apac,12.8,7.5,39.5,56.0,1,0.74,-2.0,"edge tests and canaries dominate; low sustained demand"
cl-chi-02,high-priority-48,na,79.6,78.3,97.1,100.0,21,0.28,+4.8,"similar profile to cl-nyc-01; tail latency sensitivity"
cl-ams-03,shared-small-8,eu,29.1,26.3,59.8,82.0,4,0.61,-1.2,"apps migrating; fragmentation remains"
cl-iad-05,reserve-128,na,53.0,52.7,75.0,92.0,11,0.22,+0.0,"reserved capacity in steady refill pattern"
cl-hkg-02,mixed-32,apac,66.8,65.5,90.2,100.0,12,0.36,+7.9,"multi-tenant spikes from new customer; recommend pinning test"
cl-chi-03,batch-16,na,19.2,15.4,40.2,52.0,0,0.69,-3.9,"batch window moved earlier this week; lower peak"
cl-ams-05,dedicated-64,eu,85.4,84.5,97.5,100.0,19,0.15,+0.6,"good stability; p95 close to 100 for short periods"
cl-nyc-07,burst-12,na,48.6,44.8,79.9,94.0,6,0.49,+2.3,"burst pool used for online experiments; short-lived spikes"
cl-wlg-01,edge-4,apac,9.7,5.1,33.0,46.0,0,0.81,-1.5,"low overall; consider consolidation with syd edge"
cl-lon-07,shared-small-8,eu,31.6,29.7,63.1,86.0,3,0.59,+0.8,"gradual increase in shared usage"
cl-sfo-09,reserve-128,na,49.9,49.7,73.3,88.0,8,0.24,-0.7,"reserve pool with predictable diurnal"
cl-sgp-02,high-priority-48,apac,74.3,73.9,95.6,100.0,16,0.29,+6.0,"customer rollout this week; watch p95"
cl-chi-07,mixed-32,na,58.1,56.2,85.4,99.0,10,0.41,-0.4,"stable but many small bursts from multi-tenant jobs"

Observed signals / quick takeaways:
- Fragmentation hotspots: small shared pools (8,4 slots) consistently show FragIndex >= 0.58. These pools have many intermittent small jobs and long-lived pinned slices that increase fragmentation.
- Heavy microburst clusters: dedicated/high-priority pools in NA/EU show frequent microbursts (10-28 events/week). These correlate with function-call style interactive workloads.
- Regional surge pattern: APAC peaks earlier in UTC and caused a few 100% peak-minute incidents in mixed pools; surge correlated with a new customer rollout in SGP.
- Batch smoothing: batch pools have low microburst counts but high fragmentation indices when orchestration moved windows earlier (observed in FRA/CHI clusters).
- Edge pools: extremely low average util but very high fragmentation -> candidate for consolidation.

Anomalies flagged for follow-up:
- cl-hkg-02: +7.9% WoW delta and p95 nearing 100% — trace to a tenant test rollout (tagging shows new model versions). Recommend temporary pin or migration to a reserve pool to protect latency-sensitive services.
- cl-sfo-03: FragIndex 0.58 with 34% avg util — possible scheduling inefficiency. Suggest running heavy-defrag policy (evict small long-living jobs to defrag window) during low-traffic hours.
- cl-nyc-01 and cl-chi-02: correlated microburst phases at similar timestamps (regionally offset) — could be botched traffic fanout from a shared cache warm-up; investigate upstream traffic patterns.

Suggested immediate actions (1 week):
- Run defrag job in small shared pools (ams-*, sfo-03) on Tue 02:00->03:00 UTC. Owner: Priya Kapoor.
- Provision one additional reserve-128 slot in SGP for rolling customer test for 72 hours. Owner: Diego Morales.
- Add p95-weighted alert for high-priority pools to detect sustained p95 > 95% for >10m. Owner: Samir Patel.

Follow-ups / experiments (2-6 weeks):
- A/B test automatic microburst absorption using short-lived burst-queues vs direct allocation in mixed pools (sample cl-sin-04 & cl-hkg-02).
- Evaluate consolidation of edge pools (syd/wlg) into a shared 8-slot edge cluster during low-demand hours and measure impact on FragIndex.
- Backfill missing telemetry for cold-start rates in 6 clusters where data quality flagged as low.

Open questions / TODOs:
- Are internal schedulers reporting pod-level fragmentation that would allow a more precise FragIndex? (ticketed: JIRA-OPS-1256)
- Do we have request-level annotations to map microbursts to specific API keys? If yes, run correlation for cl-nyc-01 and cl-chi-02 spikes.

Raw export note:
This is a sampler intended to fit in a single sheet for quick ops triage. Full fleet exports still required for contractual capacity planning and financial forecasting. If you want CSV/Google Sheet import, paste the TABLE block above into a new sheet (comma-separated).

Prepared by: Ava Chen (infra capacity) — quick draft for weekly ops sync.
Comments welcome — ping in #infra-capacity or add to linked JIRA.

title: Renewal velocity rollup + heatmap Q3 2026
preview: Tracker intentionally mixes quantitative fields (probability, ARR, open tickets) with qualitative signals (last_contact, notes). Use 'Risk Level' to triage weekly: at_risk (<40%), negotiation (40-70%), on_track (>70%). This sheet is a working artifact for the CS weekly sync and should be exported to the QBR template folder when a renewal moves to 'finalize'.

title: Statuspage stepdown notice and reconciliation guide
preview:
Purpose: Draft copy + short reconciliation kit for customers after a controlled model stepdown that caused degraded generations and intermittent embedding mismatches. Use this for a short statuspage update, a concise post-incident customer summary, and a short self-serve reconciliation checklist.

Audience: Customers affected by the stepdown (hosted API + dedicated) and partners running private/private-vpc with automatic fallbacks. Primary goal: be actionable and concise; secondary goal: reassure customers we preserved integrity and provide steps to verify outputs.

=== One-line statuspage update (short) ===
Status: Degraded generation quality observed after automated model stepdown; mitigation in progress.
Impact: Some requests returned fallback-model outputs with altered phrasing and rare embedding score variance.
Current status: Mitigation rollout started — routing and model selectors reverted for affected tenants; elevated monitoring is active.
ETA next update: 30–60 minutes.

=== Longer statuspage blurb (for status.redwood.ai incident entry) ===
Summary: At 2026-06-29 18:12 UTC our automated model stewardship system initiated a model variant stepdown for capacity reasons. A subset of traffic was routed to a compatibility variant which has different tokenization/quantization characteristics. This caused occasional degraded generation quality for text generation and rare embedding similarity score shifts for long-context requests.
What we're doing: We detected the change, paused automated stepdowns, reverted route policies for affected tenants back to their pinned model variants, and are performing controlled verification. We have increased sampling of recent requests and are rolling back the compatibility variant for any tenant that reports regressions.
Customer impact: Temporary changes in phrasing and slight similarity differences in embeddings for requests between 2026-06-29 18:12 UTC and 2026-06-29 18:46 UTC for ~1.8% of requests globally. No data loss reported.
Next update: 2026-06-30 00:00 UTC or sooner.

=== Customer-facing post-incident summary (to send to affected customers) ===
Subject: Short summary of June 29 model stepdown and how to reconcile outputs
Hi <CustomerName>,
TL;DR: On 2026-06-29 a staged model stepdown routed a fraction of traffic to a compatibility model variant which produced occasional lower-quality text and minor embedding score drift. We reverted routing for affected tenants, validated request integrity, and can help replay or re-run affected requests.
What happened (plain language): Our serving controller triggered a policy-driven stepdown to a capacity-sparing model variant. That variant uses different tokenization and quantization paths; for some prompts the generated text tone and structure changed, and for a small number of long-context embedding requests similarity scores shifted enough to affect retrieval ordering.
Scope: Affected requests: ~1.8% of global gen/embedding requests between 2026-06-29 18:12–18:46 UTC. Affected customers: list available on request (we flagged tenants with >0.05% impacted requests).
Data integrity: No customer payloads were dropped. All requests logged; we have request-level evidence for replay. If you need us to re-run a bounded set of requests for reconciliation, we will assist.
Remediation: We have paused automated stepdowns, reverted policy for pinned tenants, and initiated a targeted replay test using a canary set of customer prompts. We will provide a replay report for customers who request one (see reconciliation steps below).
Customer actions (self-serve reconciliation):
  - Option A — Quick check: Re-run a small sample of prompts that matter to you (10–50) with the timestamped requests. If you see differences in phrasing or ranking, escalate to cs@redwood.ai and include request_ids.
  - Option B — Automated replay request: Provide a CSV of request_ids (max 5k) and a preferred time window; we will re-run those on your pinned model variant and provide a diff package (tokens, scores, generated_text).
  - Option C — Retrieval/embeddings reconciliation: If you use embeddings for retrieval, re-index with the replayed vectors we provide or run a spot-check on top-10 hits for a sample set. Typical rebuild is quick; we can prioritize for enterprise customers.
Questions to expect from customers: Did my data change? No. Can you re-run my traffic? Yes — submit request via the customer portal or send to cs@redwood.ai with the CSV. Will billing be affected? No retro charges for replays performed by Redwood as part of remediation.

=== Short technical postmortem summary (customer-friendly) ===
Root cause (summary): An autoscaler/serving policy triggered an automated fallback to a compatibility-quantized variant intended for headroom preservation. The variant has a different tokenizer normalization path and quantization profile which, for certain long prompts and edge-case decoding beams, produced altered outputs.
Why it wasn't caught: The compatibility variant passed our standard smoke checks (latency, throughput), but our recent quality regression signals (prompt-level hallucination metrics and embedding drift monitors) had an alert threshold set too permissive for this variant change.
Fixes implemented:
  - Short-term: Disable automated stepdowns; revert routes for pinned tenants; run targeted replay and spot-check.
  - Medium-term: Adjust quality gating thresholds for model-stepdown decisions; add a lightweight similarity check for embeddings during rollouts; expand smoke checks to include a small but representative QA prompt set per customer segment.
  - Long-term: Improve canary sampling (per-tenant QA sets), better telemetry for tokenizer/path divergences, and add an automated rollback trigger based on quality deltas rather than only latency/throughput.

=== Reconciliation checklist for customers (practical) ===
1) Identify impacted timeframe: 2026-06-29 18:12–18:46 UTC.
2) Decide reconciliation mode: spot-check vs replay.
3) If spot-check: re-run 10–50 critical prompts on your pinned model variant and compare outputs.
4) For embeddings: pick 100 queries, compute top-10 retrievals pre/post and check for hit-rate delta > 2%.
5) If you want Redwood to run a replay: attach CSV with request_id, timestamp, tenant_id. We'll return a zipped report with diffs and a short human summary.
6) Request timeline: small replays (<=5k requests) usually completed within 4 business hours; larger replays scheduled with you.

=== FAQ for customers ===
Q: Did this affect my billing or token counts?
A: No billing changes for remediation replays performed by Redwood labs. Normal usage billing applies for the original requests.
Q: Do we need to change any of our client-side settings?
A: No. If you pin a model variant in your tenant settings that will prevent automatic stepdowns; if you rely on autoretry using lower-tier variants you may see worse quality during similar events.
Q: What if my application observed user-visible regressions?
A: Contact cs@redwood.ai with request_ids and sample outputs; we'll prioritize a replay and remediation plan.

=== Templates / Snippets for status updates and emails ===
Short in-product status (30 words): We observed temporary quality regressions after an automated model stepdown. We reverted policies for affected tenants and are offering replays. No data loss.
Email subject line options: 
  - "Incident summary: model stepdown and replay options (June 29)"
  - "Action available: Replay and reconciliation after brief model fallback"

=== Internal notes / To-do (notes to self) ===
- Coordinate replay automation with infra -> ticket INC-2026-731 (Jira).
- Build per-tenant sample set generator (skeletal work: GH PR #4621).
- Confirm list of affected tenants with product telemetry and attach to customer outreach list.
- Draft final postmortem for ops channel and product review by 2026-07-02.
- Check legal/compliance for audits on replayed requests (Priya).

Open questions:
- Do we want to auto-offer replays to all flagged tenants or wait for explicit requests? (ops+cs decision)
- Should we publish a short KB article on how to pin model variants and best practices? (dx owns)

=== Example customer email body (editable) ===
Hi <Name>,
We wanted to let you know about a brief service event on 2026-06-29 that routed a small portion of traffic to a compatibility model variant. You might see slightly different phrasing or retrieval ordering for requests made between 18:12–18:46 UTC.
We have reverted the routing for affected tenants and can re-run requests on your pinned model variant and provide a diff. If you’d like us to run a replay for your tenant, please reply to this message with a CSV of request_ids (max 5k) or open a ticket in the customer portal.
Thanks — Ava Chen, Customer Success

=== End ===

Notes: keep iterative tone, leave a final checkbox for legal and product review before sending external emails.

title: Customer Rapid Update + Postmortem Slim Summary Template
preview:
Purpose:
This working template is for rapid customer-facing status updates and a short, customer-friendly postmortem summary. Keep language non-technical where possible, call out impact and next steps clearly, and provide an empathetic tone.

Usage guidance (quick):
- Use the top 'Initial update' block within 5–15 minutes of customer-impact confirmation.
- Publish concise status-page updates every 15–60 minutes while active; longer cadence once stabilized.
- Convert the final status page text into the slim postmortem summary for customers within 48 hours.
- Use the FAQ and escalation lines for direct enterprise customers.

---

1) Initial update (status page / email subject examples)
Subject (status page): "Service Degradation — Investigating"
Subject (email to affected customers): "Redwood Inference — We're investigating reduced API availability"
Body (short):
Status: Investigating
Impact: Some customers may experience increased latency and errors for text generation and embeddings in the hosted API (region: us-west-1).
Time detected: 2026-05-09T09:12 UTC (internal detection)
What we're doing: Our on-call and infra teams are actively investigating the root cause and routing traffic to healthy capacity where possible. We'll share another update within 30 minutes.
Where to check: Status page (link) and dedicated incident Slack channel (link).
Contact: customersuccess@redwood.ai for account-specific questions.

Tone notes: short sentences, avoid blaming downstream services publicly, prioritize clarity over full technical detail.

---

2) Follow-up update (status page templates for stages)
A) Investigating -> Identified
Status: Identified
Impact: Latency spikes and 5xx errors impacting ~12% of requests across the hosted API in us-west-1. Some Dedicated pods saw degraded throughput.
Root-cause (customer-facing): A memory growth in a recently deployed routing component caused nodes to become overloaded, triggering partial capacity loss.
Mitigation: We have routed traffic away from affected nodes and scaled up reserved capacity in the region. Error rates are falling; customers may still see intermittent errors.
Estimated time to resolution: 1–3 hours (we will update as we have better ETA).

B) Identified -> Mitigated
Status: Mitigated
Impact: No new errors in the last 20 minutes; customer-facing call success rate improved to >99% for most workloads.
What we did: Applied an emergency rollback of the routing change and restarted impacted workers. We also applied a temporary autorestart policy while a permanent fix is tested.
Next steps: Run verification tests, monitor for 3 hours, and prepare the customer postmortem summary.

C) Mitigated -> Resolved
Status: Resolved
Impact: Service fully restored. If you continue to see issues, please create a support ticket and include request IDs and timestamps.
Root cause (concise): Regression from a routing deployment that caused memory growth under certain traffic patterns; reverted.
Permanent fixes: Patch to prevent memory growth, improved deployment canary rules, and pre-deploy load tests.

---

3) Slim customer-facing postmortem (target <= 300–500 words)
Title: 2026-05-09 Partial Hosted API Outage — Customer Summary
Summary (one paragraph): On 2026-05-09 between ~09:12–12:45 UTC, customers in the us-west-1 region experienced increased latency and a subset of 5xx errors for the Redwood hosted API. We identified a memory-regression in a routing component introduced in a recent deployment; we reverted the change and restored normal service.
Impact: Customers using the hosted API in us-west-1 may have seen failed requests or degraded throughput during the incident window. Dedicated customers with pinned capacity were partially affected if their traffic hit the degraded nodes; no data loss occurred.
Customer-facing root cause: A code change in the routing layer caused inefficient memory retention under specific request sizes, leading to node overload and cascading request failures.
Remediation performed: Emergency rollback of the routing deployment, restart of affected workers, and temporary scaling of healthy capacity to absorb residual traffic.
Permanent and near-term actions:
- Ship a patch that eliminates the memory regression (owner: eng-serving-runtime, ETA: 2026-05-17).
- Improve canarying to include memory/steady-state checks at higher QPS (owner: release-engineering, ETA: 2026-05-24).
- Add synthetic traffic tests and automated pre-deploy thresholds for memory growth (owner: platform-engineering, ETA: 2026-06-01).
Compensation & SLA notes (customer-facing): We are evaluating uptime impact against SLA entitlements for affected customers and will follow up with account teams about any credit or compensation. Enterprise customers with impacted SLAs should contact their account manager.
How to get help: Opening a support ticket (support.redwood.ai) with request IDs and timestamps helps us prioritize investigation for high-impact customers.

---

4) Ready-to-send email template (short)
Subject: Update: Incident on 2026-05-09 — Service Restored
Hi <Customer Name>,
We wanted to follow up: service in the us-west-1 region has been restored as of 2026-05-09T12:45 UTC. During the incident window you may have seen failed requests or slower responses. We believe no customer data was lost. Our team has reverted the change that triggered the issue and is shipping a fix to prevent recurrence. We will send a concise postmortem within 48 hours with timelines and next steps.
If your application continues to show issues, please reply to this email or open a support ticket and include the affected request IDs and timestamps.
Thanks for your patience,
Ava Chen
Customer Success — Redwood Inference

---

5) Quick FAQ for customer-facing support replies (snippets)
Q: Is my data safe?
A: Yes. There is no evidence of data loss or unauthorized access. The incident was caused by a routing component regression that affected availability only.

Q: Will you credit us for downtime?
A: We are calculating impact for customers against their SLA entitlements. Account teams will reach out if a credit is applicable.

Q: What about Dedicated/private deployments?
A: Private deployments were not impacted unless traffic flowed through affected hosted routing components (rare). If you have a Dedicated contract and saw issues, we'll work with your account team immediately.

---

6) Internal notes / reminders (working list — leave in doc)
- Confirm exact percentage of failed requests and affected customers (Data team: Noah).
- Prepare per-customer impact snippets for account execs (Sofia).
- Run longer verification window before closing incident (10–30 min depending on traffic).
- Draft short Confluence postmortem with full technical timeline (applied-ml + serving-runtime).
- Review rollback process in release runbook and add a memory-monitoring step.

Unresolved / TODO:
- Draft customer-specific subject lines for enterprise accounts (tone check with legal if compensation promised).
- Decide whether to proactively notify customers on next-day scheduled maintenance to roll the permanent fix.

---

7) Short checklist for publishing status page updates (keep near keyboard)
- [ ] Confirm detection time and update T0 in timeline.
- [ ] State severity and impacted regions.
- [ ] Provide clear remediation steps in plain English.
- [ ] Give ETA or say 'we will update in X minutes' — avoid empty promises.
- [ ] Link to support and account contacts.
- [ ] Coordinate with PR/legal for external press if incident escalates beyond customers.

Notes (meta):
This is intentionally a slim, repeatable template to speed external comms. Keep the customer postmortem <= 500 words when possible; link to the detailed Confluence postmortem for technical customers. Save iteration history in the incident Slack channel and attach final draft to the Confluence incident page.

Draft history and small edits:
- v1: initial quick template — 2026-05-10 11:02 by AC
- v2: added mitigation language + FAQ — 2026-05-11 09:30 by NP
- v3: tone adjustments for enterprise customers — 2026-05-12 14:18 by SM

(Notes to self) keep the slim postmortem separate from the technical one — customers want to know impact + fix, not debugging logs.

- [ ] Confirm detection time and update T0 in timeline.
- [ ] State severity and impacted regions.
- [ ] Provide clear remediation steps in plain English.
- [ ] Give ETA or say 'we will update in X minutes' — avoid empty promises.
- [ ] Link to support and account contacts.
- [ ] Coordinate with PR/legal for external press if incident escalates beyond customers.

Notes (meta):
This is intentionally a slim, repeatable template to speed external comms. Keep the customer postmortem <= 500 words when possible; link to the detailed Confluence postmortem for technical customers. Save iteration history in the incident Slack channel and attach final draft to the Confluence incident page.

Draft history and small edits:
- v1: initial quick template — 2026-05-10 11:02 by AC
- v2: added mitigation language + FAQ — 2026-05-11 09:30 by NP
- v3: tone adjustments for enterprise customers — 2026-05-12 14:18 by SM

(Notes to self) keep the slim postmortem separate from the technical one — customers want to know impact + fix, not debugging logs.

title: Voice Handoff — Gap Analysis and Requests (May 2026)
preview:
TL;DR:
A compact synthesis of recent CS-to-Engineering handoffs around customer runtime problems (latency spikes, stale KV cache behavior, and model-fallback surprise). Customers are asking for: clearer fallbacks, per-client caching controls, deterministic canary controls, and a lightweight diagnostic envelope they can ship to ops. This note consolidates raw threads, prioritizes requests by customer impact and implementation risk, and proposes two small experiments that could buy time while larger platform changes are scoped.

Background / Why this matters:
- Over the last 8 weeks the CS team handled ~18 escalations that surfaced the same root ambiguities: when Redwood falls back to a cheaper/shorter model variant, customers often see inconsistent outputs and don’t know whether fallback occurred.
- Several customers using Dedicated pools also reported inconsistent KV cache hits after scaling events (autoscale up/down) — manifests as higher token costs + occasional latency regressions for returning users.
- These signals are becoming blocking for 3 prospects in late-stage evaluation. Quick low-risk fixes could materially reduce churn and speed Dedicated sales.

Signal summary (synthesized):
- Reproducible problems (6 cases): KV cache resets after scale-to-zero/scale events; customers report cold-start token cost spikes and time-to-first-token regressions.
- Observability gaps (10 cases): no explicit flag in API response or logs when model fallback occurs; customers must infer via artifacts or call support.
- Control surface gaps (8 cases): customers want policy-level config for fallback behaviour (e.g., prefer-latency, prefer-quality, no-fallback) and a way to pin a session to a model family during rollout windows.
- Diagnostics/telemetry asks (12 cases): small envelope with request_id + model_variant + cache_hit boolean + routed_region that customers can surface to their logs and include in support tickets.

Representative customer quotes (raw):
- "We couldn't tell if the bot changed models or if it was our prompt — it just sounded different. We lost trust." — Enterprise X
- "After a scaling event our costs spiked and performance went downhill for a few hours. We want an opt-out from fallback during sale windows." — Marketplace Y
- "If we had a single bit saying cache_hit=true we could triage in minutes instead of hours." — Analytics startup Z

Top requests (ranked by frequency x customer-revenue exposure):
1) Explicit fallback flag in response and simple SDK hook (low effort, high impact)
   - What: Include model_variant and fallback=true/false in standard response metadata and expose via SDK 'onRouting' callback.
   - Why: Removes ambiguity, cuts CS TTR by ~30–60min per ticket.
   - Quick win ETA: 2 sprints (backend + SDK minor change).

2) Per-customer fallback policy (medium effort)
   - What: Allow customer-level policy: prefer_latency | prefer_quality | no_fallback, attachable to API keys or route configs.
   - Why: Enterprise customers want deterministic behavior for critical workflows or sales events.
   - Risk: Complexity when combined with cross-region capacity constraints; needs graceful denial/fallback messaging.

3) Lightweight diagnostic envelope (very low effort)
   - What: Add limited headers/metadata: request_id, model_variant, cache_hit (boolean), routed_region. Document how to surface in logs.
   - Why: Immediate triage enablement, minimal security risk.
   - Quick win ETA: 1 sprint if surfaced at proxy layer.

4) KV cache visibility dashboard + TTL introspection (higher effort)
   - What: For Dedicated customers, a small dashboard showing per-pool cache hit rate, recent evictions, and a timeline aligned to autoscaling events. Also expose TTL/config for KV cache policy.
   - Why: Empowers SREs at customer to correlate scale events and cache resets.
   - Risk/Effort: Requires telemetry plumbing + UI work.

5) Canary pinning API (A/B control) (medium-high effort)
   - What: API to pin sessions/keys to a model variant during a specified window; ephemeral pin should respect capacity.
   - Why: Prevents unexpected quality regressions during rollouts.

Engineering impact / rough effort map (guesstimates):
- Add response metadata & SDK hook: small backend change + SDK patch — 2 dev-weeks + 1 QA-week.
- Diagnostic envelope at proxy level: 1 dev-week if proxy already sees fields, otherwise 3 dev-weeks.
- Per-customer fallback policy: 4–8 dev-weeks (policy layer + config surface + tests + docs).
- KV cache dashboard: 6–10 dev-weeks (telemetry + minimal UI) + ongoing maintenance.
- Canary pinning: 6–12 dev-weeks with rollout testing and policy conflicts resolution.

Proposed immediate plan (next 6–8 weeks):
1) Ship diagnostic envelope + response metadata (requests 1 & 3) as an initial tranche. Rationale: low risk, fast customer value, reduces CS ticket toil.
   - Owner: Eng/Platform (proxy + API) + SDK team.
   - Acceptance: fieldable in Dedicated and Hosted with clear docs snippet.

2) Run an experiment for a manual pin/hold for key accounts (stop-gap)
   - Tactic: Operations can manually route critical API-keys to a pinned model variant for up to 72 hours during events. Implement as an internal override rather than product change.
   - Owner: SRE/Platform operations with CS enablement playbook.

3) Scope per-customer fallback policies and KV cache dashboard as Q3 projects.

Open questions / trade-offs to resolve:
- If we provide per-customer no-fallback, should the system refuse requests that exceed latency SLOs, or queue them? (Customer preference varies.)
- Diagnostic envelope: what PII or sensitive surface might leak? We recommend limiting to non-user-identifying tokens and making it opt-in for Private/On-prem.
- Canary pinning: how do we handle capacity exhaustion gracefully? Should we fall back to 'reject' with clear error code when pinned variant not available?

Suggested tickets / next steps for CS and Product: (short actionable list)
- Create ENG ticket to add response metadata & SDK hook (tag: cs-priority).
- Run 2-week ops experiment to support manual pinning for 3 pilot customers.
- Draft product PRD for per-key fallback policies (include rollout safety ladder and cost/latency guardrails).
- Schedule discovery with Observability team for KV cache telemetry scope.

Appendix — raw notes from recent tickets (abbreviated):
- Ticket CS-1421: marketplace customer noted sudden boost in token usage after 02:13 UTC autoscale; logs show a scale event but no cache hit metric.
- Ticket CS-1519: enterprise reported output style changed mid-session; they inferred fallback after checking latency distribution.
- Ticket CS-1535: dev team wanted a boolean in response to show cache_hit — extremely repetitive ask across small customers.

Notes to self / followups:
- Coordinate with SDK squad before shipping metadata names — avoid breaking existing parsing logic.
- Draft support KB article explaining how to read the diagnostic envelope and include example log snippets.
- Prepare short CS-runbook for manual pinning (who to call, how to escalate).

title: Regional preemption exercise log - 2026-04-21
preview:
Purpose:
This note captures the outcomes of the regional preemption exercise run on 2026-04-21 targeting the EU-west and US-west fleets. The goal was to validate our automated fallback routing and reclaim capacity on preemptible pools without violating latency SLOs for Reserved customers. This is a working log (informal) — please add edits inline.

Attendees:
- Ava Chen (owner) - infra
- Diego Martinez - fleet ops
- Hannah Patel - SRE
- Liam O'Connor - serving-runtime
- Sofia Nguyen - product-dedicated
- Observers: 2 on-call engineers from support

Exercise summary (what we did):
1) Triggered scheduled preemption on a subset of ephemeral nodes in eu-west-2b (approx 160 GPUs) at 14:00 UTC.
2) Simultaneously triggered a low-priority drain on a subset of us-west-1 preemptible cells (approx 120 GPUs) to validate cross-region routing.
3) Enabled automated fallback routing policy (policy: route->dedicated fallback->lower-cost-hosted-model) with a 2s grace window and KV-cache-preserve flag turned on.
4) Observed traffic for 45 minutes, then restored preemptible nodes at 14:50 and confirmed capacity returned.

Hypothesis before the run:
- Automated routing will shift ~92% of at-risk requests to dedicated clusters within the 2s grace window.
- Latency p95 for affected endpoints will remain under the 1200ms SLO for Reserved customers.
- Token-level cost will spike in the fallback period but should be within our emergency budget thresholds.

Key metrics (observed):
- Requests affected: 28k total over 45m (peak concurrent affected streams: ~820)
- Routing success (requests fully handled by fallback path): 89.7%
- p50 latency (affected endpoints): baseline 180ms -> 220ms during fallback
- p95 latency (Reserved endpoints): baseline 580ms -> 1,050ms during fallback (brief spikes to 1,350ms for ~30s)
- Error rate (5xx) for affected endpoints: increased from 0.03% -> 0.28% during exercise window, returned to baseline within 90s of capacity restoration
- Cost delta (prelim): estimated +12% compute cost for the 45m window on dedicated pools (billing visible in daily cost runbook)

Observations and notes (qualitative):
- Routing behavior: Most traffic moved as expected, but ~7% of requests returned transient upstream errors because client-side retries exceeded our 2s grace before failover. We need to tune retry/backoff guidance in SDKs or extend the grace window for some high-tail clients.
- KV cache: We preserved KV caches successfully for dedicated fallback in ~85% of flows. The remaining 15% experienced cache warmups causing extra token generation and higher latency. These cases correlated with longer conversations (>8k tokens session).
- Regional differences: EU-west preemption had smoother cold-starts on dedicated because the eu-west dedicated pool has pre-warmed kernels for the most common model subset. US-west saw higher p95 due to slightly different model compilation cache hit rates.
- Autoscaler interplay: During the preemption, our autoscaler attempted to spin up burst nodes in dedicated pools but hit a rate limit in the cloud provider's API for ~2 minutes. This contributed to the brief latency spike and elevated 5xx.

Incidents or surprises:
1) Cloud API rate limit (us-west dedicated pool). Cause: autoscaler made concurrent requests to create/migrate 12 nodes; provider throttled requests at ~RPS 6. Mitigation: add exponential backoff and request bundling in autoscaler.
2) Client retry loops. Several internal SDK versions use a 1s linear retry with no jitter which led to synchronized retry storms when routing delayed. Mitigation: short-term - publish guidance for SDK owners; medium-term - update default SDK retry to jittered exponential.
3) Stale DNS TTL for fallback endpoints. A handful (~0.4%) of clients still pointed at preemptible IPs because of long TTLs in custom client setups. Mitigation: add DNS invalidation step in runbook and note for Solutions Eng.

Action items (owner - due):
- Diego Martinez - update autoscaler to bundle node create requests and add provider-aware backoff logic (ETA: 2026-05-05).
- Hannah Patel - add smoke-check in fallback path to validate KV-cache hit ratio; create alert if <80% for >5m (ETA: 2026-04-28).
- Liam O'Connor - prototype an extended-grace-window option (configurable per-customer tier) and run a small A/B test (ETA: 2026-05-03).
- Sofia Nguyen - coordinate with SDK leads to publish interim retry guidance and track SDK adoption (ETA: 2026-04-26).
- Ava Chen - update the Regional Preemption Playbook with this exercise's findings and attach run logs (ETA: 2026-04-23).

Follow-up experiments planned:
- Pre-warm test: force compile/cache warming in us-west dedicated pool for top-10 models used by enterprise customers and re-run a small-scale preemption (target: 2026-05-10).
- Grace-window sweep: run experiments with grace window set to 1s/2s/5s for different workload mixes to measure client behavior and cost tradeoffs (target: 2026-05-12 -> 2026-05-15).
- DNS/SDK resilience drill: run targeted client-side retry variability test with Solutions Eng customers in staging to validate updated SDK guidance (target: 2026-05-01).

Run artifacts and raw logs location:
- Fleet playlogs: gs://redwood-fleet-playlogs/preemption/2026-04-21/eu-west-2b-run.log
- Autoscaler traces: gs://redwood-fleet-traces/autoscaler/2026-04-21/trace-44321.json
- Latency heatmaps & dashboards: https://console.redwood.ai/dashboards/fleet/preemption-2026-04-21

Notes to self / loose thoughts (informal):
- Might be worth exposing a customer-level knob for prioritized KV-cache persistence across fallbacks (tradeoff: memory pressure on fallback nodes).
- Consider bumping the dedicated pool pre-warm window for enterprise customers approaching heavy weekend usage.
- We should add an explicit step in the runbook to communicate to SOC & Support during exercises so they can anticipate short-lived billing/cost alerts.

Appendix (short timeline):
- 13:50 UTC: pregame checks and reconcile model-version mappings
- 14:00 UTC: trigger preemption (eu-west-2b first tranche)
- 14:03 UTC: initial routing shift observed; p95 begins to climb
- 14:05 UTC: autoscaler 429s observed against provider API (us-west)
- 14:12 UTC: transient 5xx spike; mitigations triggered (circuit breaker engaged for some routes)
- 14:30 UTC: traffic stabilizes for majority; lingering cache-warmups continue
- 14:50 UTC: restore nodes; full capacity back by 14:55 UTC

If you were a participant and have extra logs or notes, please append them here (informal).

-- Ava
