title: Cicada microburst profile — annotated notes (Apr 2026)
preview:
Purpose:
This is a working sketch of a small-burst morphology weve been calling the 'cicada' pattern: short, irregular spikes at 50-400ms duration that repeatedly punctuate an otherwise low baseline. The goal is to capture observed latency shape, cost impact by model size and quant profile, and quick ideas for tooling/alerts. Not a polished report — notes, ASCII charts, and quick heuristics for ops/product to chew on.

Experiment summary:
- Date range: 2026-04-06 -> 2026-04-11 (sampled 1s resolution traces)
- Targets: hosted-api fleet (region: us-west-2), vCPU+GPU slot model variants: tiny/compact/standard (approx 3B/7B/13B)
- Conditions: 1) cold-start disabled (warm pool), 2) KV-cache enabled for session prefix where noted, 3) quantized vs fp16 models compared
- Key metrics captured: p50/p90/p95/p99 latency (ms), tokens/sec throughput, cost-per-token (USD per 1k tokens), tail-duration distribution (ms), hit-rate of prefix KV cache.

Workload definitions (abbrev):
- micro-q: 1-2 short turns, 10-30 tokens per response (interactive queries)
- micro-chat: 3-6 turn conversational microbursts, each response 20-80 tokens
- embed-batch: 50 short embedding requests batched (control)

High-level observations (notes):
1) Cicada spikes are not single-shot p99 events; they appear as short clusters with intra-cluster spacing ~120ms. They amplify p95->p99 gap by ~2.3x compared to sustained load.
2) Compact quant (4/8) reduces median latency by ~12% vs fp16, but p99 reduction is smaller (~5%), implying tail dominated by scheduling/tiling, not pure FP compute.
3) Enabling KV-prefix hit-rate >0.6 collapses microburst amplitude by ~30-45% for chat workloads (benefit scales with session length).
4) Cost per effective successful response increases non-linearly with microburst frequency because slot contention reduces packing efficiency; observed cost multiplier 1.15-1.6 across scenarios.

Representative ascii charts (p90 latency over time):
Time axis (1m window sampled) -> each char ~10ms
baseline: |========|========|========|========|========|  (p90 ~ 60ms)
cicada:   |===-===|====--=|==---==|====-==|=--====|  (bursts at irregular intervals)
Legend: '-' short spike (~120ms), '--' extended cluster (~250-400ms).

Mini table: p95/p99 vs model (micro-chat, kv enabled vs disabled)
Model      | kv-hit=0.65 p95 / p99 (ms) | kv-hit=0.10 p95 / p99 (ms) | cost multiplier
compact-3B | 45 / 110                  | 62 / 215                   | 1.25
standard-7B| 72 / 182                  | 98 / 360                   | 1.38
large-13B  | 95 / 260                  | 135 / 490                  | 1.58

Notes on cost multiplier: computed as relative increase in USD per 1k tokens measured during 30m windows where microburst frequency > 12/min vs baseline low-burst windows. Larger models suffer more due to elongated decode time and fewer co-located multiplex slots.

Tail shape sketch (cdf buckets):
0-50ms: ########## (70% baseline)
50-150ms: ###---###  (added 18% cicada mass)
150-300ms: ---***---  (p95-p99 region growth)
>300ms: .....*     (rare extended clusters)

Interpreting roots of the cicada pattern (hypotheses):
- Client-side retry/traffic shaping: many clients re-issue small follow-ups ~100ms after first partial result, generating bursts that align with upstream scheduling windows.
- Scheduler slot coupling: tiny multiplex slots get preempted by larger decodes; small bursts get squeezed into less-efficient tiling, increasing tail.
- Network microqueueing: short-lived packets cause small head-of-line effects when combined with chunked responses.
- KV cache eviction churn: prefix entries evicted/repopulated around rolling restarts cause temporary loss of hit-rate — we see corresponding burst amplitude increases after node restarts.

Quick experiments that moved the needle (lab notes):
- Bumped small-session slot cap from 6->8: p99 down 9% during microburst windows, packing improved.
- Aggressive prefix prefetch (1s lookahead) increased KV hit-rate from 0.42->0.67 on sampled sessions; p95 dropped ~22ms on average for micro-chat.
- Client-endbackoff (150ms jitter) reduced cluster overlap and smoothed p95->p99 gap at cost of +10ms median. Tradeoff seems acceptable for interactive UX.

Recommendations (short-term):
1) Instrument a new microburst detector in console: sliding 30s window counting events where p90 > median*1.8 and p99 > 200ms. Emit cohort tags (kv-hit, model-size, slot-type).
2) Roll out optional client jitter param in SDK (enabled default for small-session clients) with 80-200ms randomized jitter and document UX tradeoffs.
3) Add a prefetched-prefix budget option for high-likelihood sessions (profile-based); default budget 1 prefetch per 30s.
4) Update pricing explainer for Dedicated: show example where burst-heavy chat increases per-query unit-cost by ~25% for 7B models — helps sales set expectations.

Longer-term research topics:
- Scheduling policy: evaluate hybrid tiling that prioritizes low-latency micro requests during detected cicada windows.
- Admission control: soft-throttle small-turn clients on detected microburst cohorts.
- Adaptive KV eviction tuned to session continuity heuristics.

Action items (owners):
- (Ravi) prototype microburst detector and add tags to traces — due 2026-04-26
- (Maya) SDK jitter PR and docs draft — due 2026-05-03
- (Oliver) prefetched-prefix budget prototype for dedicated path — due 2026-05-10
- (Ava) write a 1-pager for GTM/solutions on burst-econ impact for enterprise contracts — due 2026-05-07

Outstanding questions / open checks:
- Do edge regions show different cicada spacing? preliminary indicates shorter spacing in APAC edge (80ms) vs core (120ms) — needs systematic check.
- How much of the tail is client-driven vs server-scheduling? need coordinated SDK+server experiment where we toggle client jitter.

Appendix: quick reproduction notes
- command (sampler): ./bin/trace-collector --range 2026-04-06/2026-04-11 --filter "service:hosted-api region:us-west-2" --sample 1s > cicada-trace-uw2.ndjson
- postproc (python): scripts/microburst_annotate.py --kv-store-metrics kv-snapshot-20260411.json --out cicada-summary.csv
- env: models run on g5-8x (compact) and a100-40GB (standard), autoscaler policy baseline, warm pool size 40 per shard.

If this is useful, I can turn one section (detector spec or cost explainer) into a stand-alone one-pager for product/solutions. For now leaving this as a working sketch — comments welcome.
text:
Purpose:
This is a working sketch of a small-burst morphology weve been calling the 'cicada' pattern: short, irregular spikes at 50-400ms duration that repeatedly punctuate an otherwise low baseline. The goal is to capture observed latency shape, cost impact by model size and quant profile, and quick ideas for tooling/alerts. Not a polished report — notes, ASCII charts, and quick heuristics for ops/product to chew on.

Experiment summary:
- Date range: 2026-04-06 -> 2026-04-11 (sampled 1s resolution traces)
- Targets: hosted-api fleet (region: us-west-2), vCPU+GPU slot model variants: tiny/compact/standard (approx 3B/7B/13B)
- Conditions: 1) cold-start disabled (warm pool), 2) KV-cache enabled for session prefix where noted, 3) quantized vs fp16 models compared
- Key metrics captured: p50/p90/p95/p99 latency (ms), tokens/sec throughput, cost-per-token (USD per 1k tokens), tail-duration distribution (ms), hit-rate of prefix KV cache.

Workload definitions (abbrev):
- micro-q: 1-2 short turns, 10-30 tokens per response (interactive queries)
- micro-chat: 3-6 turn conversational microbursts, each response 20-80 tokens
- embed-batch: 50 short embedding requests batched (control)

High-level observations (notes):
1) Cicada spikes are not single-shot p99 events; they appear as short clusters with intra-cluster spacing ~120ms. They amplify p95->p99 gap by ~2.3x compared to sustained load.
2) Compact quant (4/8) reduces median latency by ~12% vs fp16, but p99 reduction is smaller (~5%), implying tail dominated by scheduling/tiling, not pure FP compute.
3) Enabling KV-prefix hit-rate >0.6 collapses microburst amplitude by ~30-45% for chat workloads (benefit scales with session length).
4) Cost per effective successful response increases non-linearly with microburst frequency because slot contention reduces packing efficiency; observed cost multiplier 1.15-1.6 across scenarios.

Representative ascii charts (p90 latency over time):
Time axis (1m window sampled) -> each char ~10ms
baseline: |========|========|========|========|========|  (p90 ~ 60ms)
cicada:   |===-===|====--=|==---==|====-==|=--====|  (bursts at irregular intervals)
Legend: '-' short spike (~120ms), '--' extended cluster (~250-400ms).

Mini table: p95/p99 vs model (micro-chat, kv enabled vs disabled)
Model      | kv-hit=0.65 p95 / p99 (ms) | kv-hit=0.10 p95 / p99 (ms) | cost multiplier
compact-3B | 45 / 110                  | 62 / 215                   | 1.25
standard-7B| 72 / 182                  | 98 / 360                   | 1.38
large-13B  | 95 / 260                  | 135 / 490                  | 1.58

Notes on cost multiplier: computed as relative increase in USD per 1k tokens measured during 30m windows where microburst frequency > 12/min vs baseline low-burst windows. Larger models suffer more due to elongated decode time and fewer co-located multiplex slots.

Tail shape sketch (cdf buckets):
0-50ms: ########## (70% baseline)
50-150ms: ###---###  (added 18% cicada mass)
150-300ms: ---***---  (p95-p99 region growth)
>300ms: .....*     (rare extended clusters)

- Scheduler slot coupling: tiny multiplex slots get preempted by larger decodes; small bursts get squeezed into less-efficient tiling, increasing tail.
- Network microqueueing: short-lived packets cause small head-of-line effects when combined with chunked responses.
- KV cache eviction churn: prefix entries evicted/repopulated around rolling restarts cause temporary loss of hit-rate — we see corresponding burst amplitude increases after node restarts.

Quick experiments that moved the needle (lab notes):
- Bumped small-session slot cap from 6->8: p99 down 9% during microburst windows, packing improved.
- Aggressive prefix prefetch (1s lookahead) increased KV hit-rate from 0.42->0.67 on sampled sessions; p95 dropped ~22ms on average for micro-chat.
- Client-endbackoff (150ms jitter) reduced cluster overlap and smoothed p95->p99 gap at cost of +10ms median. Tradeoff seems acceptable for interactive UX.

Recommendations (short-term):
1) Instrument a new microburst detector in console: sliding 30s window counting events where p90 > median*1.8 and p99 > 200ms. Emit cohort tags (kv-hit, model-size, slot-type).
2) Roll out optional client jitter param in SDK (enabled default for small-session clients) with 80-200ms randomized jitter and document UX tradeoffs.
3) Add a prefetched-prefix budget option for high-likelihood sessions (profile-based); default budget 1 prefetch per 30s.
4) Update pricing explainer for Dedicated: show example where burst-heavy chat increases per-query unit-cost by ~25% for 7B models — helps sales set expectations.

Longer-term research topics:

- Admission control: soft-throttle small-turn clients on detected microburst cohorts.
- Adaptive KV eviction tuned to session continuity heuristics.

Action items (owners):

- (Maya) SDK jitter PR and docs draft — due 2026-05-03

title: Regional fleet prep — week of 2026-03-22
preview:
TL;DR:
- Focus this week: regional pre-warming and small-capacity handoffs for EU-WEST and AP-SOUTH ahead of customer canaries.
- Shortfall identified in US-WEST burst pool (approx 14% below target for p99 throughput during synthetic load). Temporary mitigation: lightweight routing to dedicated spare nodes + throttled prewarm.
- Action items assigned for kernel fallback tests, KV-cache warm-up script rollout, and cost estimate for additional reserved slots.

1) Meeting summary (2026-03-22 weekly infra sync)
Attendees: Ava Chen (lead), Marcus Li, Sofia Martinez, Priya Raman, Ethan Brooks, on-call rotation.
Purpose: Align on regional capacity ramps & prewarm schedule for next two sprints; confirm rollouts for reduced-latency routing.
Decisions:
- Approve staggered prewarm across regions: EU-WEST first (Mon-Wed), US-WEST second (Wed-Fri), AP-SOUTH light prewarm (Thu).
- Keep 10% of dedicated spare pool unallocated as emergency buffer for 72 hours post-rollout.

2) Capacity snapshot (end of day 2026-03-22)
- Global fleet total GPUs: 5,480 (logical units).
- EU-WEST: target available GPUs 1,200; currently provisioned 1,230; effective after maintenance 1,150 (scheduled kernel patch 2026-03-24 02:00 UTC).
- US-WEST: target 1,800; currently provisioned 1,560 (shortfall due to delayed node procurement + two failed control-plane upgrades).
- AP-SOUTH: target 700; currently provisioned 720 but observed 10% baseline nvlink variance on certain node types.
- Reserved Dedicated commitments: 1,500 units across regions (buffering prioritized for enterprise tenants).

3) Risk & incidents (past 7 days)
- Incident INFRA-3214: intermittent worker node restarts in us-west caused by a driver+kernel mismatch (root cause: partial rollout of qkv-optim kernel). Ongoing: rollback staged to older kernel image for remaining nodes; Marcus tracking.
- Observation: queue latency spikes correlated with DNS flapping in a single AZ (us-west-2b) — SRE suspects transient cloud provider control-plane propagation lag. Mitigation: route traffic away from AZ until provider confirms stability.

4) Prewarm plan details (operational play)
- Strategy: incremental KV-prefill + sequence-distribution warmups to avoid heavy synchronous loading. Use script set: scripts/prewarm/kvcache-prefill-v2 (rolling 5 pods per AZ).
- EU-WEST (priority): start Mon 03:00 UTC, duration window 48–72h; metric targets: p50 latency <= 35ms, generator-p99 <= 220ms under 60% synthetic load.
- US-WEST: reduced ramp until control-plane upgrades complete; only prewarm customer-facing models (top 10 by qps).
- AP-SOUTH: warm only smaller instance types; defer large-batch quantized variants until nvlink variance investigation complete.

5) Canary & fallback routing
- Canary allocation: 60 GPU units in EU-WEST reserved for canary customers; roll traffic 5% -> 25% -> 50% with 1h observation steps.
- Fallback mapping: define config in routing-policy v3: route to compatible-quantized variant when latency breach >20% for 3 consecutive minutes.
- Action: Priya to add automated rollback webhook to rollout pipeline (deadline 2026-03-24 EOD).

6) Observability & signals (what to watch)
- Core metrics: per-AZ p99 latency, token throughput per-second, GPU utilization distribution, KV hit-rate, queue depth (per worker).
- Alert tuning: suppress noisy alerts for token-drop <0.5% to avoid chase loops; raise alerts for KV hit-rate drop >15% for >10m.
- New dashboards: regional-prewarm-overview (Grafana) created; link in Confluence page referenced above.

7) Cost and commitment considerations
- Short-term cost: running full prewarm in US-WEST will increase on-demand spend by estimated $18k/week.
- Suggested mitigation: use spot capacity for non-latency-critical background batches; reserve additional 100 units in next quarter planning cycle if customer canary converts.

8) Blockers
- Procurement delays for next batch of H100-equivalent nodes (expected ETA 2026-04-10). Impact: prolonged use of heterogeneous fleet, more fallbacks required.
- Kernel compatibility tests failing intermittently on qkv-optim branch for a small subset of models.

9) Action items (owner — due)
- Marcus Li — complete kernel rollback for remaining us-west nodes and validate stability (2026-03-23).
- Priya Raman — webhook + automated rollback hook for canary pipeline (2026-03-24).
- Sofia Martinez — update Grafana dashboard panels to include KV hit-rate baseline overlays (2026-03-24).
- Ethan Brooks — run nvlink variance diagnostic suite on AP-SOUTH subset and submit report (2026-03-26).
- Ava Chen — prepare cost addendum for exec review if US-WEST prewarm extends beyond trial window (2026-03-27).

10) Notes / working thoughts (informal)
- Consider moving to a decentralized prewarm controller that uses observed model footprints instead of static counts — could reduce peak by ~8–12%. Prototype idea for next sprint.
- Reminder: make sure customer telemetry consent is respected when seeding KV (use synthetic tokens only).

11) Next sync agenda (2026-03-25)
- Verify kernel rollback status and post-rollback metrics.
- Finalize prewarm completion criteria and canary stop / go thresholds.
- Review nvlink variance findings and decision on AP-SOUTH large instance warmups.

Appendix: quick links and references
- Jira: INFRA-3214 (driver restarts), INFRA-3229 (procurement), GPUTEAM-87 (nvlink diag).
- Confluence: Regional prewarm runbook (link above).

---
Notes to self: keep this doc minimal; move detailed logs into the incident timeline doc per infra/ops/incidents when we have final RCA.

title: Release comms handoff and customer notes template (mar-2026 draft)
preview:
Purpose:
This is a compact, ops-focused communications and handoff template for small-to-medium Redwood inference rollouts (hotfixes, minor model variants, feature toggles). The doc doubles as: 1) an internal ops checklist for release engineers and SREs and 2) a customer-facing release note + comms snippet that PMs / marketing can copy. Keep short; link to longer release notes when needed.

When to use this template:
- Mini-release or patch window (<= 48h stabilization expectation)
- Model variant swaps, routing policy flips, or control-plane config changes
- Customer-impacting behavior where we provide proactive notes (no full PRD change)

Quick release summary (TOP OF MESSAGE - single-paragraph for customers):
- Title: Rolling update: model routing tweak and latency improvements (non-breaking)
- ETA: 2026-03-18 09:00 PT -> 2026-03-18 11:00 PT (2h window)
- Impact: No downtime expected. A small percentage (~5%) of requests may be served by the old variant during the canary ramp. Users do not need to take action.

Internal ops handoff checklist (pre-release):
- [ ] Finalize PRs and attach release checklist: PR-1187 linked above
- [ ] Sign-off from Product: Priya Singh (email thread / slack)

title: Quarterly Orbit: Ops Cookbook & Sprint Planning (Q3 bridge)
preview:
Purpose:
This doc is a lightweight, tactical cookbook for running our Q3-bridge quarter (internal name: "Orbit"). It combines a proposed OKR crosswalk, an all-staff agenda skeleton, operational playbook snippets, and a compact risk/mitigation register for exec review. Intended audience: execs, leads across Eng, Product, GTM, and Ops. Status: working draft — looking for feedback from product-ops, eng-sre, and head of GTM by 2026-03-19.

Executive narrative (1-paragraph):
We are entering a three-month bridge focused on reliability-at-scale while preserving velocity for model onboarding and Dedicated sales. The priority is threefold: (1) harden the serving runtime for predictable tail latency across published models, (2) reduce per-token unit cost on hosted API by 8–12% through batching and caching recommendations, and (3) finalize Private deployment packaging for two pilot customers. These themes balance near-term revenue delivery with foundational work that reduces long-run operational burden.

High-level OKR draft (proposal):
- Objective A: Ensure platform reliability under 2x baseline traffic and reduce customer-visible incidents.
  - KR A1: Achieve 99.95% availability for hosted API routes (per-route SLO) for top 10 routes.
  - KR A2: Reduce 95th-percentile tail latency for text-generation by 20% (measured across top-5 models).
  - KR A3: Automate failover routing so >= 90% of degraded requests are served by compatible fallback within 300ms.

- Objective B: Improve inference economics for self-serve and dedicated customers.
  - KR B1: Reduce hosted per-token cost by 8–12% on target workloads via batching and cache policies.
  - KR B2: Ship model quantization recommendations and two validated quant profiles for popular Llama/Redwood variants.
  - KR B3: Deliver an initial cost-estimator UI in Console for dev teams (alpha).

- Objective C: Close private deployment pilots and operationalize onboarding.
  - KR C1: Complete 2 Private pilot installs (VPC/on-prem) with documented runbook.
  - KR C2: Publish Private deployment security checklist and KMS integration guide.
  - KR C3: Train 3 customer-ops contacts on our support/resolution workflow.

Crosswalk: how priorities map to teams (draft):
- Serving runtime: Objective A KRs A1–A2 (owners: eng-serving-runtime, site-reliability-engineering).
- Smart routing & fallbacks: A3 (owners: platform-engineering, observability).
- Optimize (batching, quantization): Objective B (owners: applied-ml, optimize, product-optimize).
- Private deployment rollout: Objective C (owners: private-deployments, infra, security-and-compliance).
- Console & GTM: B3 + C2 (owners: console-and-rollouts, go-to-market).

All-staff (town) agenda — 60 minutes (proposed):
- 00-05 — Opening: CEO quick context + success metric needle for the quarter (1 slide).
- 05-18 — Engineering deep snapshot: 3 signal cards (latency, availability, unit-cost trend).
- 18-30 — Product ops demo: Console cost-estimator alpha (live) + rollout plan.
- 30-40 — Customer success spotlight: Private pilot customer expectations & timeline.
- 40-50 — Risk & mitigation lightning: 5 major risks and owners.
- 50-60 — Commitments & Q&A: What every team is committing to this quarter.

Prep notes for presenters (internal):
- Eng leads: bring one slide with current trendline + three actions to hit KR A2.
- Applied ML: short demo video of quantized model throughput (20–30s).
- CS & Solutions Eng: readiness checklist for pilot handoffs.
- GTM: simple messaging framework for pilot/enterprise outreach.

Sprint planning cookbook (how to run coordinated 6-week sprints within the quarter):
- Sprint cadence: two 3-week sprints per quarter segment. Each sprint contains: one reliability spike (2 weeks), one delivery milestone (feature/observability), and ongoing support rotation.
- Cross-team sync ritual: weekly 30-min 'Orbit stand' on Tuesdays — owner rotates weekly.
- Decision window: adopt a 48-hr escalation path for any blocker that impacts multiple KRs (see decision flow below).

Decision flow (quick reference):
- If a candidate change increases risk to availability by >5% for top routes -> call SRE lead + platform on-call -> 48-hr decision path (mitigation or rollback).
- If a change reduces estimated per-token cost by >10% but increases tail latency -> run canary and measure 2-week tradeoff analysis before rollout.

Risk register (top 6):
1) Model onboarding surprises: new model behaves badly in tail-latency. Mitigation: expanded pre-commit evals + canary dataset. Owner: applied-ml.
2) Insufficient capacity in key region during Dedicated sales spike. Mitigation: temporary burst pool policy + cloud capacity reservations. Owner: dedicated-team.
3) Console alpha misses critical cost signals. Mitigation: ship telemetry hooks early and do user interviews within 2 sprints. Owner: console-and-rollouts.
4) Private pilot networking delays (customer infra constraints). Mitigation: pre-flight checklist, remote lab tests. Owner: private-deployments.
5) Staffing pinch across SRE during on-call rotation. Mitigation: shift coverage, hire contractor SRE for 8 weeks. Owner: site-reliability-engineering.
6) Security review backlog for on-prem packaging. Mitigation: prioritize KMS guide and designate security SWAT. Owner: security-and-compliance.

Open questions (for execs):
- How aggressive should we be on the per-token cost target vs. latency tradeoffs? Recommend maintain latency SLOs and accept smaller cost wins for Q3 bridge; escalate if CFO expects hard cost targets.
- Will sales commit to two private pilot contracts this quarter (necessary to prioritize packaging)? Need confirmation from VP Sales by 2026-03-19.

Concrete asks (within 2 weeks):
- Product & Eng: finalize KR definitions (metrics, dashboards) — owner: Priya Nair — due 2026-03-19.
- GTM: confirm pilot go/no-go with lead customers — owner: Liam O'Sullivan — due 2026-03-21.
- SRE: propose temporary on-call augmentation plan — owner: Marcelo Rivera — due 2026-03-18.
- Applied ML: deliver two quant profiles and benchmarking notes — owner: Jia Park — due 2026-04-02.

Quick reference links (work-in-progress):
- Canary checklist (draft): https://confluence.redwood.ai/pages/canary-checklist
- KR dashboard template: https://datadog.redwood.ai/dashboards/okr-template
- Pilot runbook template: https://confluence.redwood.ai/pages/pilot-runbook
- Related JIRA epics: OPS-412, INF-887, PRIV-230
- Engineering PR (prototype failover routing): https://github.com/redwood-inference/platform/pull/3421

Notes to self / rough thoughts:
- Keep all-staff short and action-oriented; avoid too many metrics slides — pick 3 critical signals only.
- Consider a 2-week pre-brief with team leads to surface blockers early.
- If we get pushback on KR B1 (cost target), be prepared with a reduced-scope alternative: focus cost wins on self-serve API only.

Appendix: short checklist for private pilot onboarding (alpha)
- Confirm network peering & IP allowlist (network lead + customer).
- KMS key rotation test and audit log ingestion.
- Latency baseline measurement against customer test harness.
- On-call escalation contact list + timezones.
- Post-install evaluation survey (CS).

- v0.1 2026-02-20 Ava Chen: initial draft.
- v0.2 2026-03-05 Marcelo R: added sprint cookbook + risks.
- v0.3 2026-03-12 Priya N: updated OKRs and deadlines (pending exec review).
text:
Purpose:
This doc is a lightweight, tactical cookbook for running our Q3-bridge quarter (internal name: "Orbit"). It combines a proposed OKR crosswalk, an all-staff agenda skeleton, operational playbook snippets, and a compact risk/mitigation register for exec review. Intended audience: execs, leads across Eng, Product, GTM, and Ops. Status: working draft — looking for feedback from product-ops, eng-sre, and head of GTM by 2026-03-19.

Executive narrative (1-paragraph):
We are entering a three-month bridge focused on reliability-at-scale while preserving velocity for model onboarding and Dedicated sales. The priority is threefold: (1) harden the serving runtime for predictable tail latency across published models, (2) reduce per-token unit cost on hosted API by 8–12% through batching and caching recommendations, and (3) finalize Private deployment packaging for two pilot customers. These themes balance near-term revenue delivery with foundational work that reduces long-run operational burden.

High-level OKR draft (proposal):
- Objective A: Ensure platform reliability under 2x baseline traffic and reduce customer-visible incidents.
  - KR A1: Achieve 99.95% availability for hosted API routes (per-route SLO) for top 10 routes.
  - KR A2: Reduce 95th-percentile tail latency for text-generation by 20% (measured across top-5 models).
  - KR A3: Automate failover routing so >= 90% of degraded requests are served by compatible fallback within 300ms.

- Objective B: Improve inference economics for self-serve and dedicated customers.
  - KR B1: Reduce hosted per-token cost by 8–12% on target workloads via batching and cache policies.
  - KR B2: Ship model quantization recommendations and two validated quant profiles for popular Llama/Redwood variants.
  - KR B3: Deliver an initial cost-estimator UI in Console for dev teams (alpha).

- Objective C: Close private deployment pilots and operationalize onboarding.
  - KR C1: Complete 2 Private pilot installs (VPC/on-prem) with documented runbook.
  - KR C2: Publish Private deployment security checklist and KMS integration guide.
  - KR C3: Train 3 customer-ops contacts on our support/resolution workflow.

Crosswalk: how priorities map to teams (draft):
- Serving runtime: Objective A KRs A1–A2 (owners: eng-serving-runtime, site-reliability-engineering).
- Smart routing & fallbacks: A3 (owners: platform-engineering, observability).
- Optimize (batching, quantization): Objective B (owners: applied-ml, optimize, product-optimize).
- Private deployment rollout: Objective C (owners: private-deployments, infra, security-and-compliance).
- Console & GTM: B3 + C2 (owners: console-and-rollouts, go-to-market).

All-staff (town) agenda — 60 minutes (proposed):
- 00-05 — Opening: CEO quick context + success metric needle for the quarter (1 slide).
- 05-18 — Engineering deep snapshot: 3 signal cards (latency, availability, unit-cost trend).
- 18-30 — Product ops demo: Console cost-estimator alpha (live) + rollout plan.
- 30-40 — Customer success spotlight: Private pilot customer expectations & timeline.
- 40-50 — Risk & mitigation lightning: 5 major risks and owners.
- 50-60 — Commitments & Q&A: What every team is committing to this quarter.

Prep notes for presenters (internal):
- Eng leads: bring one slide with current trendline + three actions to hit KR A2.
- Applied ML: short demo video of quantized model throughput (20–30s).
- CS & Solutions Eng: readiness checklist for pilot handoffs.
- GTM: simple messaging framework for pilot/enterprise outreach.

Sprint planning cookbook (how to run coordinated 6-week sprints within the quarter):
- Sprint cadence: two 3-week sprints per quarter segment. Each sprint contains: one reliability spike (2 weeks), one delivery milestone (feature/observability), and ongoing support rotation.
- Cross-team sync ritual: weekly 30-min 'Orbit stand' on Tuesdays — owner rotates weekly.
- Decision window: adopt a 48-hr escalation path for any blocker that impacts multiple KRs (see decision flow below).

Decision flow (quick reference):
- If a candidate change increases risk to availability by >5% for top routes -> call SRE lead + platform on-call -> 48-hr decision path (mitigation or rollback).
- If a change reduces estimated per-token cost by >10% but increases tail latency -> run canary and measure 2-week tradeoff analysis before rollout.

Risk register (top 6):
1) Model onboarding surprises: new model behaves badly in tail-latency. Mitigation: expanded pre-commit evals + canary dataset. Owner: applied-ml.
2) Insufficient capacity in key region during Dedicated sales spike. Mitigation: temporary burst pool policy + cloud capacity reservations. Owner: dedicated-team.
3) Console alpha misses critical cost signals. Mitigation: ship telemetry hooks early and do user interviews within 2 sprints. Owner: console-and-rollouts.
4) Private pilot networking delays (customer infra constraints). Mitigation: pre-flight checklist, remote lab tests. Owner: private-deployments.
5) Staffing pinch across SRE during on-call rotation. Mitigation: shift coverage, hire contractor SRE for 8 weeks. Owner: site-reliability-engineering.
6) Security review backlog for on-prem packaging. Mitigation: prioritize KMS guide and designate security SWAT. Owner: security-and-compliance.

Open questions (for execs):
- How aggressive should we be on the per-token cost target vs. latency tradeoffs? Recommend maintain latency SLOs and accept smaller cost wins for Q3 bridge; escalate if CFO expects hard cost targets.
- Will sales commit to two private pilot contracts this quarter (necessary to prioritize packaging)? Need confirmation from VP Sales by 2026-03-19.

Concrete asks (within 2 weeks):
- Product & Eng: finalize KR definitions (metrics, dashboards) — owner: Priya Nair — due 2026-03-19.
- GTM: confirm pilot go/no-go with lead customers — owner: Liam O'Sullivan — due 2026-03-21.
- SRE: propose temporary on-call augmentation plan — owner: Marcelo Rivera — due 2026-03-18.
- Applied ML: deliver two quant profiles and benchmarking notes — owner: Jia Park — due 2026-04-02.

Quick reference links (work-in-progress):
- Canary checklist (draft): https://confluence.redwood.ai/pages/canary-checklist
- KR dashboard template: https://datadog.redwood.ai/dashboards/okr-template
- Pilot runbook template: https://confluence.redwood.ai/pages/pilot-runbook
- Related JIRA epics: OPS-412, INF-887, PRIV-230
- Engineering PR (prototype failover routing): https://github.com/redwood-inference/platform/pull/3421

Notes to self / rough thoughts:
- Keep all-staff short and action-oriented; avoid too many metrics slides — pick 3 critical signals only.
- Consider a 2-week pre-brief with team leads to surface blockers early.
- If we get pushback on KR B1 (cost target), be prepared with a reduced-scope alternative: focus cost wins on self-serve API only.

Appendix: short checklist for private pilot onboarding (alpha)
- Confirm network peering & IP allowlist (network lead + customer).
- KMS key rotation test and audit log ingestion.
- Latency baseline measurement against customer test harness.
- On-call escalation contact list + timezones.
- Post-install evaluation survey (CS).

End — feedback requested in doc comments or Slack thread #ops-quarterly-orbit.

- v0.1 2026-02-20 Ava Chen: initial draft.
- v0.2 2026-03-05 Marcelo R: added sprint cookbook + risks.
- v0.3 2026-03-12 Priya N: updated OKRs and deadlines (pending exec review).

title: Playground quicksplice: setup, fallbacks, and telemetry notes
preview:
Purpose:
A compact working doc for the new "quicksplice" playground flow — a tiny, low-friction SDK+playground bundle engineers can use to prototype Redwood API integrations in <10 minutes. Focus: clear first-time success, deterministic fallbacks, and lightweight telemetry so we can iterate on onboarding metrics without heavy instrumentation drain.

Context and motivation:
- We keep seeing friction in first 10-minute runs: env var confusion, token quota surprises, and streaming failures that stop the demo.
- Quicksplice = opinionated minimal runner (JS + Python examples) + sandbox prompts + an auto-fallback policy (hosted -> dedicated-dryrun -> noop simulator) so demos never hard-fail.
- This doc captures an initial quickstart script, fallback routing rules, minimal telemetry contract, and short experiment plan.

Goals (MVP):
1) 5-minute developer onboarding path: npm install, export REDWOOD_KEY, run sample app, see streaming reply.
2) Deterministic fallback chain to avoid hard failures in demos.
3) Telemetry events sufficient to measure first-time success, latencies, and number of fallbacks used.
4) Easy recipe for solutions engineers to spin up a sandbox for customers.

Quickstart (developer flow, 5–7 steps):
1) git clone https://github.com/redwood-inference/quicksplice-playground.git
2) cd quicksplice-playground && npm ci (or pip install -r requirements.txt)
3) export REDWOOD_API_KEY=sk_test_xxx (or set env in .env file)
4) node examples/echo-stream.js  --model rdw/gpt-fast --prompt "Summarize: <paste text>"
5) Observe streaming tokens in console. If network blocked, runner switches to simulator mode and prints a clear banner.

Notes on the runner:
- Embedded minimal client wraps the official SDK with a RetryManager that understands client error classes (401/429/5xx) and will: retry in-place up to 3x for transient 5xx; on 429 attempt non-streaming reduced-batch request; on persistent auth failure fallthrough to simulator.
- Simulator mode: local deterministic stub that returns short canned responses and includes a header "X-quicksplice-fallback: simulator" for telemetry.

Fallback routing policy (opinionated):
- Normal: hosted API (low-latency route)
- If client sees region mismatch or capacity throttle (503+ rate-limit): try dedicated-dryrun (recorded but not charged) for developers with test tokens
- If credentials invalid or long network partition: simulator (guaranteed response, not representative of real latency)
- All fallbacks are additive: we emit events and surface banner in console with recommended next steps.

Telemetry contract (minimal):
- Event names and required fields (short list):
  * quicksplice.session_start {session_id, sdk_lang, user_id?, env: dev|ci}
  * quicksplice.request_sent {session_id, request_id, model, streaming: true|false, prompt_len_tokens}
  * quicksplice.response_received {request_id, latency_ms, tokens_out, status: ok|partial|error}
  * quicksplice.fallback_used {request_id, fallback_type: dedicated|simulator|reduced-mode, reason_code}
  * quicksplice.session_end {session_id, success: true|false, steps_completed}
- Only send non-identifiable telemetry in MVP. No prompt text, only hashes (sha256) when needed for dedup.

Local observability (dev console):
- Console banners on fallback with instructions: e.g., "FALLBACK -> simulator (auth failure). Check REDWOOD_API_KEY or run ./scripts/get-test-key.sh"
- Verbose flag (--v) prints raw HTTP status and timing per phase (dns, connect, tls, request, response).

Edge cases and failure modes to capture in tests:
- Streaming interruption mid-token (simulate). Expect runner to: surface partial output, emit response_received status=partial, attempt 1 retry of streaming connection.
- Long coldstart: first token > 2s. Telemetry should record cold_start=true for first request in a session.
- Quota-exceeded: 429 with body {retry_after}. Runner should automatically switch to non-streaming reduced prompt and set fallback reduced-mode.

Experiment plan (2-week sprint):
Week 1: land quicksplice prototype, internal demo to devexp, iterate on messaging and telemetry keys.
Week 2: run A/B with two onboarding scripts (interactive CLI vs single-command demo) measuring time-to-first-token and %sessions needing fallback.

Acceptance criteria for rollout:
- >70% sessions get first token within 5s in lab environment
- Fallback usage rate < 10% for users with valid test keys
- Telemetry events are arriving to analytics with correct schema and no PII

Sample small code snippet (JS runner):
const client = new RedwoodClient({apiKey: process.env.REDWOOD_API_KEY});
try {
  await client.chat.stream({model: 'rdw/gpt-fast', messages});
} catch (e) {
  if (isAuthError(e)) {
    console.warn('FALLBACK -> simulator');
    runSimulator();
  } else {
    throw e;
  }
}

Checklist for solutions engineers (spin up sandbox):
- [ ] create test API key via scripts/get-test-key.sh
- [ ] ensure local port 3000 free for playground UI
- [ ] run ./scripts/start-playground.sh --env=sandbox
- [ ] verify quicksplice.session_start appears in analytics (test endpoint)

Open questions / unresolved:
- Should simulator responses include subtle latency jitter to approximate real world? tradeoff: realism vs predictable demo timing.
- Where do we store session_id mapping for multi-device sessions in devexp analytics? temporary local store vs ephemeral server?
- Do we allow example prompts to be sent to telemetry when hashed? legal check needed.

Next actions (short):
1) PR prototype runner to sdk repo (PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/sdk/pull/482) — owner Marcus
2) Hook minimal telemetry endpoint in analytics (RDW-1273) — owner Priya
3) Draft short SE playbook + one-pager for onboarding (target: internal weekly demo) — owner Ava

References and links:
- quicksplice repo: https://github.com/redwood-inference/quicksplice-playground
- Confluence overview: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/DE/quicksplice-playground
- Jira epic: RDW-1273

Notes to self (scratch):
- keep initial sdk surface tiny: only chat and embeddings examples; don't add rerank or tools until stable
- prefer console-first UX for fastest turnaround
- copy for SE playbook: short checklist + one GIF showing fallback banner

If you want to run this locally and help test, ping #devexp-playground on Slack and I'll add you to the sandbox testers list.

title: Customer Incident Communications: Status & Postmortem Templates
preview:
Purpose: This doc contains customer-facing text drafts for status page updates and a concise postmortem summary. Use these as the canonical starting point when drafting communications for production incidents that impact hosted API or Dedicated customer endpoints. Keep wording plain, transparent, and action-oriented. Replace placeholders ({{incident_id}}, {{start_time}}, etc.) before publishing.

Audience: Customer Success, Support, PS, Product Ops, and anyone drafting public status updates or customer postmortems.

Tone guidance (short): clear, apologetic, factual, remediation-focused, short paragraphs, no internal-only jargon. Lead with impact and resolution status.

Templates:

1) Status page — Initial (0-30 minutes)
Title: Partial outage affecting text generation for some customers — investigating
Public message: We are currently investigating reports of increased error rates and timeouts for the Redwood hosted API text-generation endpoints. Some requests may fail or experience high latency. Our on-call engineering team is actively investigating and working on mitigation.
Impact: Customers may see 5xx errors, timeouts, or slower-than-normal responses for text-generation and chat endpoints in the <region>.
Time started: {{start_time}} (UTC)
What we are doing: Engineers are identifying the root cause and applying targeted mitigations. We will post an update within 30 minutes or sooner.
Customer guidance: If you are experiencing errors, retry with exponential backoff. If you rely on a private Dedicated endpoint, check your console for dedicated-scope alerts.

2) Status page — Update (ongoing, 30-120 minutes)
Title: Mitigation in progress — degraded service for text generation in <region>
Public message: We have identified a subsystem contributing to increased request queuing and are rolling a configuration change to reduce request bursts and stabilize latency. Some customers may continue to experience intermittent errors. We are monitoring metrics and will confirm when service returns to normal.
Impact: Reduced throughput and intermittent 5xx responses. Specific affected models: {{model_list}}.
Progress: Configuration change rolled to 40% of serving fleet at {{time}}. Metrics: error rate down from X% to Y% in the canary group.
What we are doing next: Gradual rollout to remaining fleet and additional safeguard toggles if needed.
Customer guidance: Continue retries, and if you have a critical workload, open a priority support ticket and include your customer id and request ids.

3) Status page — Resolved
Title: Service restored — text generation endpoints returned to normal
Public message: The incident affecting text generation has been resolved. Full service restoration confirmed at {{resolved_time}} (UTC). We validated end-to-end request success across affected regions and models.
Impact summary: Between {{start_time}} and {{resolved_time}}, some customers experienced elevated error rates and latency on text-generation and chat endpoints.
Next steps: We will publish a customer-facing postmortem within 72 hours with root cause, mitigation details, and persistent fixes.
Contact: For concerns or to discuss impact on your SLA, open a support ticket or contact your account team.

4) Customer-facing postmortem (concise, ~1 page)
Title: Postmortem — {{incident_id}}: Elevated errors and latency for text generation ({{start_time}} — {{resolved_time}} UTC)
Executive summary: On {{start_date}} between {{start_time}} and {{resolved_time}} UTC, customers experienced higher-than-normal error rates and increased latency for hosted text-generation endpoints in <region>. The issue was caused by a configuration regression in request batching and queue backpressure that led to uneven request distribution across the serving fleet. We deployed a rollback of the configuration and a targeted load-shedding fix to restore normal operation.
Customer impact: Affected customers saw intermittent 5xx responses and timeouts. Estimated percentage of global traffic affected: ~{{percent}}%. No persistent data loss or model corruption observed. Dedicated private deployments not connected to the public control plane were unaffected unless explicitly mentioned.
Root cause (concise): A change to the adaptive batching controller increased per-worker queue depth under bursty traffic patterns. The controller's heuristic incorrectly prioritized latency targets causing queue saturation on a subset of workers, which in turn triggered cascading timeouts and per-request failures.
Timeline (abridged):
- {{start_time}} — First error spike detected by automated alert (error rate > alert threshold).
- +7m — Engineering paged. Initial triage points at request queuing and worker saturation in metrics.
- +15m — Identified recent config push to adaptive batching (deploy id: {{deploy_id}}) correlated with affected worker groups.
- +24m — Rolled back the config to previous version for canary group; error rate fell in canary.
- +45m — Full rollback across fleet completed.
- +60m — Load-shedding safeguard toggled and traffic stabilized.
- {{resolved_time}} — Metrics returned to baseline; incident declared resolved.
Actions taken during incident: rollback of batching config, throttling of high-burst clients via temporary routing policy, and toggling of load-shedding parameters.
Short-term remediation (next 7 days):
- Lock the adaptive-batching heuristic behind a feature flag with stricter canary rollout rules.
- Add additional alerting for per-worker queue depth and tail latency divergence.
Medium/long-term prevention (30–90 days):
- Rework controller heuristic to consider per-worker tail latency and historic burstiness.
- Expand canary automation to simulate multi-tenant bursts (synthetic traffic tests).
- Formalize a rollback playbook and reduce rollback-to-revert time through CI changes.
Customer guidance and mitigations:
- If you saw errors, retry requests with exponential backoff and jitter.
- For critical production workflows, consider pinning requests to a Dedicated capacity pool or using the retryable queue pattern we recommend in the SDK docs (link to best practice).
Contact & SLA notes:
- If you experienced measurable business impact, open a priority support ticket referencing {{incident_id}} and include request ids and timestamps. We will attach usage/latency logs and a tailored impact statement.
Frequently asked questions (short):
Q: Will Redwood credit usage for downtime?
A: For customers on a usage plan, we evaluate credits case-by-case for incidents that breach SLOs. Contact your account team.
Q: Were any customer models or data exposed or lost?
A: No. There was no data leakage or model corruption.

Communications checklist for the incident owner (CS or comms lead):
- Confirm incident_id and timeline with engineering lead.
- Publish initial status page update within 15 minutes of confirmed customer impact.
- Send an in-product banner for high-severity incidents (if available).
- Prepare customer-facing postmortem draft within 72 hours; get engineering and legal signoff.
- Notify enterprise customers proactively via account teams and include any compensation info if SLA breached.
- Post follow-up in #incident-response with links to public status and postmortem.

Suggested cadence and channels:
- Initial: status.redwood.ai incident update + Slack #customers-prod-ops ping (0–15m).
- Ongoing: status page updates every 30–60 minutes until stabilized.
- Resolved: final status page + short postmortem announcement + private outreach to impacted accounts within 24–72 hours.

Internal notes to drafters: Keep the public postmortem under one page. Be explicit about impact windows and give next steps. Avoid deep internal mechanics—refer customers to a longer technical postmortem available on request when appropriate. For Dedicated/private customers, tailor the message to their deployment model and include contact/PS paths.

Appendix: Quick examples of short email to impacted customers
Subject: Incident {{incident_id}} — short summary and next steps
Body: Hi <Customer>,
We experienced degraded service affecting text-generation between {{start_time}} and {{resolved_time}} UTC. Our engineering team identified and rolled back a configuration change that caused request queuing, and full service has been restored. We are preparing a postmortem that will describe root cause and next steps; if you need logs or an impact assessment for your team, reply and we will prioritize. Sorry for the disruption — Ava Chen, Customer Success.

Version history: Draft created 2026-03-10 by Ava Chen. Review updates: 2026-03-11 (added timeline template), 2026-03-12 (clarified rollout language and added FAQ).

End of doc.
text:
Purpose: This doc contains customer-facing text drafts for status page updates and a concise postmortem summary. Use these as the canonical starting point when drafting communications for production incidents that impact hosted API or Dedicated customer endpoints. Keep wording plain, transparent, and action-oriented. Replace placeholders ({{incident_id}}, {{start_time}}, etc.) before publishing.

Audience: Customer Success, Support, PS, Product Ops, and anyone drafting public status updates or customer postmortems.

Tone guidance (short): clear, apologetic, factual, remediation-focused, short paragraphs, no internal-only jargon. Lead with impact and resolution status.

Templates:

1) Status page — Initial (0-30 minutes)
Title: Partial outage affecting text generation for some customers — investigating
Public message: We are currently investigating reports of increased error rates and timeouts for the Redwood hosted API text-generation endpoints. Some requests may fail or experience high latency. Our on-call engineering team is actively investigating and working on mitigation.
Impact: Customers may see 5xx errors, timeouts, or slower-than-normal responses for text-generation and chat endpoints in the <region>.
Time started: {{start_time}} (UTC)
What we are doing: Engineers are identifying the root cause and applying targeted mitigations. We will post an update within 30 minutes or sooner.
Customer guidance: If you are experiencing errors, retry with exponential backoff. If you rely on a private Dedicated endpoint, check your console for dedicated-scope alerts.

2) Status page — Update (ongoing, 30-120 minutes)
Title: Mitigation in progress — degraded service for text generation in <region>
Public message: We have identified a subsystem contributing to increased request queuing and are rolling a configuration change to reduce request bursts and stabilize latency. Some customers may continue to experience intermittent errors. We are monitoring metrics and will confirm when service returns to normal.
Impact: Reduced throughput and intermittent 5xx responses. Specific affected models: {{model_list}}.
Progress: Configuration change rolled to 40% of serving fleet at {{time}}. Metrics: error rate down from X% to Y% in the canary group.
What we are doing next: Gradual rollout to remaining fleet and additional safeguard toggles if needed.
Customer guidance: Continue retries, and if you have a critical workload, open a priority support ticket and include your customer id and request ids.

3) Status page — Resolved
Title: Service restored — text generation endpoints returned to normal
Public message: The incident affecting text generation has been resolved. Full service restoration confirmed at {{resolved_time}} (UTC). We validated end-to-end request success across affected regions and models.
Impact summary: Between {{start_time}} and {{resolved_time}}, some customers experienced elevated error rates and latency on text-generation and chat endpoints.
Next steps: We will publish a customer-facing postmortem within 72 hours with root cause, mitigation details, and persistent fixes.
Contact: For concerns or to discuss impact on your SLA, open a support ticket or contact your account team.

4) Customer-facing postmortem (concise, ~1 page)
Title: Postmortem — {{incident_id}}: Elevated errors and latency for text generation ({{start_time}} — {{resolved_time}} UTC)
Executive summary: On {{start_date}} between {{start_time}} and {{resolved_time}} UTC, customers experienced higher-than-normal error rates and increased latency for hosted text-generation endpoints in <region>. The issue was caused by a configuration regression in request batching and queue backpressure that led to uneven request distribution across the serving fleet. We deployed a rollback of the configuration and a targeted load-shedding fix to restore normal operation.
Customer impact: Affected customers saw intermittent 5xx responses and timeouts. Estimated percentage of global traffic affected: ~{{percent}}%. No persistent data loss or model corruption observed. Dedicated private deployments not connected to the public control plane were unaffected unless explicitly mentioned.
Root cause (concise): A change to the adaptive batching controller increased per-worker queue depth under bursty traffic patterns. The controller's heuristic incorrectly prioritized latency targets causing queue saturation on a subset of workers, which in turn triggered cascading timeouts and per-request failures.
Timeline (abridged):
- {{start_time}} — First error spike detected by automated alert (error rate > alert threshold).
- +7m — Engineering paged. Initial triage points at request queuing and worker saturation in metrics.
- +15m — Identified recent config push to adaptive batching (deploy id: {{deploy_id}}) correlated with affected worker groups.
- +24m — Rolled back the config to previous version for canary group; error rate fell in canary.
- +45m — Full rollback across fleet completed.
- +60m — Load-shedding safeguard toggled and traffic stabilized.
- {{resolved_time}} — Metrics returned to baseline; incident declared resolved.
Actions taken during incident: rollback of batching config, throttling of high-burst clients via temporary routing policy, and toggling of load-shedding parameters.
Short-term remediation (next 7 days):
- Lock the adaptive-batching heuristic behind a feature flag with stricter canary rollout rules.
- Add additional alerting for per-worker queue depth and tail latency divergence.
Medium/long-term prevention (30–90 days):
- Rework controller heuristic to consider per-worker tail latency and historic burstiness.
- Expand canary automation to simulate multi-tenant bursts (synthetic traffic tests).
- Formalize a rollback playbook and reduce rollback-to-revert time through CI changes.
Customer guidance and mitigations:
- If you saw errors, retry requests with exponential backoff and jitter.
- For critical production workflows, consider pinning requests to a Dedicated capacity pool or using the retryable queue pattern we recommend in the SDK docs (link to best practice).
Contact & SLA notes:
- If you experienced measurable business impact, open a priority support ticket referencing {{incident_id}} and include request ids and timestamps. We will attach usage/latency logs and a tailored impact statement.
Frequently asked questions (short):
Q: Will Redwood credit usage for downtime?
A: For customers on a usage plan, we evaluate credits case-by-case for incidents that breach SLOs. Contact your account team.
Q: Were any customer models or data exposed or lost?
A: No. There was no data leakage or model corruption.

Communications checklist for the incident owner (CS or comms lead):
- Confirm incident_id and timeline with engineering lead.
- Publish initial status page update within 15 minutes of confirmed customer impact.
- Send an in-product banner for high-severity incidents (if available).
- Prepare customer-facing postmortem draft within 72 hours; get engineering and legal signoff.
- Notify enterprise customers proactively via account teams and include any compensation info if SLA breached.
- Post follow-up in #incident-response with links to public status and postmortem.

Suggested cadence and channels:
- Initial: status.redwood.ai incident update + Slack #customers-prod-ops ping (0–15m).
- Ongoing: status page updates every 30–60 minutes until stabilized.
- Resolved: final status page + short postmortem announcement + private outreach to impacted accounts within 24–72 hours.

Internal notes to drafters: Keep the public postmortem under one page. Be explicit about impact windows and give next steps. Avoid deep internal mechanics—refer customers to a longer technical postmortem available on request when appropriate. For Dedicated/private customers, tailor the message to their deployment model and include contact/PS paths.

Appendix: Quick examples of short email to impacted customers
Subject: Incident {{incident_id}} — short summary and next steps
Body: Hi <Customer>,
We experienced degraded service affecting text-generation between {{start_time}} and {{resolved_time}} UTC. Our engineering team identified and rolled back a configuration change that caused request queuing, and full service has been restored. We are preparing a postmortem that will describe root cause and next steps; if you need logs or an impact assessment for your team, reply and we will prioritize. Sorry for the disruption — Ava Chen, Customer Success.

Version history: Draft created 2026-03-10 by Ava Chen. Review updates: 2026-03-11 (added timeline template), 2026-03-12 (clarified rollout language and added FAQ).

End of doc.

title: Fortnight Drift Hypothesis Log — Onboarding Evals
preview:
Purpose:
A compact, actionable log of the last fortnight's onboarding eval regressions and the leading hypotheses we want to test. This is deliberately a working doc — quick notes, examples, and narrow experiments we can run in the next 2 weeks.

Executive summary (TL;DR):
- Overall pass rate on new onboarding prompt sets dropped ~4.8 percentage points vs baseline (7-day rolling).
- Largest degradation is in 'intent-preservation' tests for short chat prompts (avg. F1 down 9%).
- Three dominant failure clusters: hallucinations about proprietary product names, truncation-related context loss for >512-token histories, and temperature-sensitive creativity mismatches.
- Immediate actions: 1) pin a canary model variant for the most-critical customers, 2) run targeted KV/cache sanity checks for long-turn conversations, 3) start 2 small experiments (context-window padding vs. explicit system priming).

Observed metrics snapshot (fortnight window 2026-02-25 -> 2026-03-10):
- eval_prompt_sets_total: 128 prompt sets onboarded
- failing_prompt_sets: 22 (17.2%)
- baseline_pass_rate (prior fortnight): 86.9%
- current_pass_rate: 82.1%
- intent_preservation_f1_delta: -9.0%
- hallucination_rate_delta: +3.2%
- mean_response_length_change: -6 tokens
- latency_p50 change: +12 ms (no correlation with failures found)

Top failure clusters (examples + quick notes):
1) Hallucinations on product metadata
   - Example prompt: "Does Redwood offer on-prem billing connectors?"
   - Expected: "No, routing to Private deployments is supported; billing connectors require VPN setup..."
   - Actual: Model invented a third-party partner name and claimed an integration that doesn't exist.
   - Notes: Mostly shows up on prompts that reference less-common product terms introduced in Feb product release notes. Triggered on both opt-1.3 and rwd-2.1 variants.

2) Context truncation for long chat histories (>512 tokens)
   - Example prompt chain: multi-turn customer conversation with code snippets + earlier constraints. Final user message references earlier system instruction to "always respond with approval code format". Model ignores instruction ~40% of failing cases.
   - Notes: Failure correlated with batch size bump on serving nodes (see infra spike 2026-03-03). Also shows increased KV cache miss ratio in traces.

3) Temperature-sensitive response style mismatch
   - Symptoms: Short classification-style prompts become unexpectedly verbose or add speculative steps when deployed temperature bumped from 0.0->0.2 (auto-burst policy applied to reduce tail latency in region eu-west-1).
   - Example: "Is this invoice overdue? Answer Yes/No." -> model returns multi-sentence justification instead of short label.

Quick root-cause hypotheses (ranked):
A) Model variant selection and recent rollout of rwd-2.1 quantized microvariant introduced subtle decoding differences for rare tokens (product names) -> hallucination uplift.
B) KV cache eviction policy change (recent autoscaler config) increased cache misses for long histories, causing loss of earlier instruction tokens.
C) Autoscaling burst policy in eu-west-1 temporarily switches to a lower-temperature variant for certain latency windows; mismatch between evaluation harness temp config and serving runtime.
D) Prompt set changes: several onboarding prompt sets added more adversarial/edge-case sequences in Feb; coverage gap not represented in training-sim evals.

Immediate small experiments (can run in isolated A/B):
1) Canary rollback experiment (Owner: Priya Patel)
   - Description: Pin a 10% traffic subset to previous verified variant (rwd-2.0) for customers flagged high-sensitivity.
   - Success criteria: reduction of hallucination_rate by >=2% in canary cohort within 72 hours.
   - Tickets: JIRA:ONB-118, GH PR: github.com/redwood-inference/pr/678

2) Cache sanity test (Owner: Ethan Li)
   - Description: Force no-cache runs on 100 failing conversation traces and compare instruction visibility in tokens returned by model tracing. Also simulate increased batch sizes to reproduce miss ratio.
   - Success criteria: reproduce failure >80% of time when cache eviction forced; root-cause confirmation if so.

3) Prompt-engineering mitigation: explicit system-priming (Owner: Maya Thompson)
   - Description: Add short, high-salience system priming line to onboarding prompt templates: "Do not invent third-party partners; if unsure, say 'unknown'."
   - Success criteria: hallucination_count drops in A/B by >=1.5% on affected sets. Quick deployable change, low risk.

4) Temperature control audit (Owner: Diego Martínez)
   - Description: Audit serving autoscaling/policy code that toggles temperature or model variant during bursts. Add a hard guard for label-style prompts to keep temperature at 0.0.
   - Success criteria: label-style prompt pass rate returns to previous baseline in eu-west-1 within 48h of guard deploy.

Data & evaluation notes:
- Eval harness mismatch: our local evals run with deterministic sampling (temp=0) while hosted can apply microvariant or burst routing. Need to add 'serving parity' checks to onboarding pipeline (ticket: ML-4523).
- Suggested new eval stage: 'serving-parity smoke' that spins up test requests through the same routing logic as production for 500 random onboarding prompt samples.

Action items and owners (2-week horizon):
- Priya: schedule canary pin + customer cohort list (due 2026-03-14)
- Ethan: reproduce cache-miss locally and attach 10 failing traces to ML-4523 (due 2026-03-13)
- Diego: audit autoscaling variant switching, PR for guard (due 2026-03-16)
- Maya: implement priming template and A/B rollout (due 2026-03-17)
- Ava (owner): consolidate results back into this doc and brief product ops (sync 2026-03-18)

Risks & blockers:
- Customer impact: two enterprise onboarding flows (account-ACME, account-Boreal) already logged complaints; consider temporary rate-limited rollback if canary shows benefit.
- Infra scheduling: autoscaler change freeze scheduled 2026-03-15 (coord with SRE).
- Eval latency: serving-parity smoke will cost ~10k eval tokens to run — budget approval needed from product ops.

Open questions / things to validate:
- Are hallucinations concentrated by tokenization boundary? (Priya/Ethan)
- Is there a correlation between prompt author and failure mode (new vs. migrated prompt sets)? (Ava)
- Should we add a short taxonomy label to each onboarding prompt set to help routing (e.g., "label-style", "fact-query", "multi-turn-constraint")?

Notes to reviewer:
- This is intentionally concise; append traces and eval-run ids to comments below. If you have a short repro, paste it under 'repro-traces' in the comment thread.
- If you disagree with a hypothesis, please add counter-evidence as a one-liner with a link to the trace/PR.

Appendices (trace ids, sample traces, quick grep commands):
- failing-trace-ids-to-check: [trace: onb-20260304-a12b, trace: onb-20260306-c43d]
- quick grep: "rg 'INVENTED_PARTNER' traces/ | head -n 10"
- eval-run ids: onb-eval-2026-03-05-9832, onb-eval-2026-03-09-0071

End of doc.

title: three-month-northstar-focus-campaign-and-invite-plan-v0-1
preview:
Purpose:
A compact, cross-functional plan to align the company around a single 90-day north star, map the company-level OKR drafts, and coordinate the all-hands + exec update cadence and comms. This is intentionally terse — use as the single source for invites, rehearsals, and the first round of leadership sign-offs.

Audience:
- Exec team
- All managers (ICs receive a simplified version for sync)
- GTM + CS leads (to prepare customer messaging)

Context / why now:
- Q2 capacity and Dedicated ramp require clear priority narrowing to avoid scope bleed into product roadmap (Hosting infra + Optimize teams flagged as potential bottlenecks).
- We need rapid alignment for customer-facing SLA language prior to sales kickoff (target 2026-05-01).

Suggested 90-day North Star (proposal):
"Ship and operationalize predictable inference for scale: 3x sustained throughput with <=15% cost/unit delta vs baseline across three benchmark workloads (chat, completion, embeddings)."
- Why: combines performance, cost, and reliability (the three commercial levers that customers ask about).
- Measurement window: rolling 7-day median for throughput and cost/unit; incidents measured by P95 latency regressions and SLO breaches.

Company-level OKR drafts (placeholders for team input):
O: Deliver a production-grade scale step that lowers cost/unit and improves throughput for priority workloads.
KR1: Deploy Dedicated pool autoscaling + burst policies to 2 enterprise customers with 99.9% acceptance SLA by 2026-04-20.
KR2: Reduce per-token cost for chat workload by 18% via batching + quantization profiles (measured on benchmark harness).
KR3: Demonstrate 3x sustained throughput on serving-runtime internal benchmark with P95 latency within target.
KR4: Add KV cache metrics and rollback playbook to Redwood Console; onboard 4 routes to use auto-caching.

O: Reduce operational toil and increase observability to detect quality regressions earlier.
KR1: Instrument token-level tracing for top 10 customer routes and create alerting playbooks.
KR2: Ship regression alerting tied to prompt suites and add two regression-runbook playbooks.
KR3: Cut incident MTTR by 30% in staged incidents (SRE measurement).

Notes on drafting process:
- These are intentionally aggressive. Product + Infra owners must provide scoped plans and dependencies by 2026-03-18 (see action owners below).
- If a team cannot provide a plan that maps to a KR, flag in Slack #lead-prioritization with a short cause and mitigation.

All-hands & Exec update plan (timeline):
- 2026-03-22: Leadership sync — approve north star and OKR framing (30m).
- 2026-03-29: First-draft manager digest sent company-wide (one-pager + playbook links).
- 2026-04-12: All-hands rehearsal (full run-through with AV, script owners, safety scripts).
- 2026-04-15: All-hands town (exec update + Q&A).
- 2026-04-20: OKR sign-off deadline (for teams to lock scope for Q2 execution).

All-hands run-of-show (sketch):
- 0:00–0:05 — Intro + framing from CEO (north star & why we narrowed).
- 0:05–0:12 — Product spotlight (hosted API & dedicated improvements; demo of throughput dashboard).
- 0:12–0:22 — Engineering brief (SRE + serving-runtime): what to expect, fallbacks, and runbooks.
- 0:22–0:30 — Customer stories (CS highlights of partners on Dedicated pilot).
- 0:30–0:45 — Breakout lightning: teams share 2-minute blockers & asks (pre-submitted).
- 0:45–1:00 — Live Q&A + close.

Comms calendar (owner: Priya):
- 2026-03-23: Manager guide + slide template (internal).
- 2026-03-29: First digest + OKR draft attached.
- 2026-04-01: External partner note for pilots (GTM review).
- 2026-04-10: Rehearsal recap + final slides to all managers.
- Post all-hands (2026-04-15): Public minutes + short recording, release of team-level OKR intake form.

Action owners (first pass):
- Product/Hosted API: Noah Patel — scope demo, tie KRs to model catalog changes.
- Serving-runtime/Infra: Miguel Torres — throughput benchmark plan, dependency list, fallback policies.
- Console & Observability: Lena Fischer — build dashboard mock + KV cache metrics.
- GTM/CS: Owen Brooks — customer narrative for Dedicated pilots + partner comms.
- Comms + Ops: Priya Kulkarni — synthesize manager digest and run all-hands logistics.
- Project coordinator: Ava Chen — track sign-offs, pulse, and decision log.

Risk ladder (high-level):
1) Delivery slippage (root causes: infra contention, model onboarding delays). Mitigation: thin-slice deliverables, ship behind feature flags, and two-week cadence demos.
2) Quality regressions after quantization / kernel optimizations. Mitigation: gated rollout, canary traffic, rollback playbook.
3) Customer confusion about SLA semantics (Dedicated vs Hosted). Mitigation: clear one-pager for GTM and sales FAQ.
4) Over-commitment across teams (too many KRs). Mitigation: enforce max 2 KRs per team that map to company-level objectives.

Rehearsal checklist (pre-all-hands):
- Slides locked + under 25 slides (Priya)
- AV test in meeting room + remote stream validated (IT)
- CEO talking points + 3 backup remarks for tough Qs (Exec Ops)
- Pre-submitted questions triaged and assigned (People Ops)
- Recording and minutes owner assigned (Project coordinator)
- Incident fallback: short holding statement prepared (SRE)

Decision log (open items):
- Should the north star explicitly include "cost/unit" or only implicitly via KRs? (owner: CEO)
- Final KR thresholds (numbers) need benchmarks from infra by 2026-03-15 (owner: Miguel)
- Customer-facing SLA language: final draft by 2026-04-07 (owner: Owen)

Open questions / parking-lot:
- Do we require a soft freeze for feature merges the week of 2026-04-10 to ensure stability for demo builds?
- Are we comfortable with a two-track rollout (pilot customers + public feature flag) or do we want a gated invite-only approach?
- How will success be communicated to non-engineering teams (one-pager vs interactive dashboard)?

Next immediate steps (by 2026-03-18):
1) Team owners submit scoped plan + dependencies (1 page each).
2) Infra publishes initial benchmark numbers and measurement rubric.
3) Comms drafts manager digest and slide template.
4) Exec meeting to finalize north star wording (2026-03-22).

Notes to self / rough thoughts:
- Keep the north star customer facing but actionable for engineering (avoid vague phrasing).
- Make OKRs small enough that progress is visible biweekly; avoid month-end-only pushes.
- Use the all-hands not just for announcements; make it a decision forum for sign-offs.

Appendix (links to artifacts to populate before finalization):
- Benchmark harness: github.com/redwood-inference/benchmarks (PR #4123 open)
- Confluence draft: TEAM-3month-northstar (linked above)
- OKR intake form: internal form (to be created)

"Notes to reviewers: edit inline, add a single sentence plan for your team under 'Action owners' by 2026-03-18. If you cannot hit dates, flag in #lead-prioritization with expected slippage and reason.
