- If a team cannot provide a plan that maps to a KR, flag in Slack #lead-prioritization with a short cause and mitigation.

All-hands & Exec update plan (timeline):
- 2026-03-22: Leadership sync — approve north star and OKR framing (30m).
- 2026-03-29: First-draft manager digest sent company-wide (one-pager + playbook links).
- 2026-04-12: All-hands rehearsal (full run-through with AV, script owners, safety scripts).
- 2026-04-15: All-hands town (exec update + Q&A).
- 2026-04-20: OKR sign-off deadline (for teams to lock scope for Q2 execution).

All-hands run-of-show (sketch):
- 0:00–0:05 — Intro + framing from CEO (north star & why we narrowed).
- 0:05–0:12 — Product spotlight (hosted API & dedicated improvements; demo of throughput dashboard).
- 0:12–0:22 — Engineering brief (SRE + serving-runtime): what to expect, fallbacks, and runbooks.
- 0:22–0:30 — Customer stories (CS highlights of partners on Dedicated pilot).
- 0:30–0:45 — Breakout lightning: teams share 2-minute blockers & asks (pre-submitted).
- 0:45–1:00 — Live Q&A + close.

Comms calendar (owner: Priya):
- 2026-03-23: Manager guide + slide template (internal).
- 2026-03-29: First digest + OKR draft attached.
- 2026-04-01: External partner note for pilots (GTM review).
- 2026-04-10: Rehearsal recap + final slides to all managers.
- Post all-hands (2026-04-15): Public minutes + short recording, release of team-level OKR intake form.

Action owners (first pass):
- Product/Hosted API: Noah Patel — scope demo, tie KRs to model catalog changes.
- Serving-runtime/Infra: Miguel Torres — throughput benchmark plan, dependency list, fallback policies.
- Console & Observability: Lena Fischer — build dashboard mock + KV cache metrics.
- GTM/CS: Owen Brooks — customer narrative for Dedicated pilots + partner comms.
- Comms + Ops: Priya Kulkarni — synthesize manager digest and run all-hands logistics.
- Project coordinator: Ava Chen — track sign-offs, pulse, and decision log.

Risk ladder (high-level):
1) Delivery slippage (root causes: infra contention, model onboarding delays). Mitigation: thin-slice deliverables, ship behind feature flags, and two-week cadence demos.
2) Quality regressions after quantization / kernel optimizations. Mitigation: gated rollout, canary traffic, rollback playbook.
3) Customer confusion about SLA semantics (Dedicated vs Hosted). Mitigation: clear one-pager for GTM and sales FAQ.
4) Over-commitment across teams (too many KRs). Mitigation: enforce max 2 KRs per team that map to company-level objectives.

Rehearsal checklist (pre-all-hands):
- Slides locked + under 25 slides (Priya)
- AV test in meeting room + remote stream validated (IT)
- CEO talking points + 3 backup remarks for tough Qs (Exec Ops)
- Pre-submitted questions triaged and assigned (People Ops)
- Recording and minutes owner assigned (Project coordinator)
- Incident fallback: short holding statement prepared (SRE)

Decision log (open items):
- Should the north star explicitly include "cost/unit" or only implicitly via KRs? (owner: CEO)
- Final KR thresholds (numbers) need benchmarks from infra by 2026-03-15 (owner: Miguel)
- Customer-facing SLA language: final draft by 2026-04-07 (owner: Owen)

Open questions / parking-lot:
- Do we require a soft freeze for feature merges the week of 2026-04-10 to ensure stability for demo builds?
- Are we comfortable with a two-track rollout (pilot customers + public feature flag) or do we want a gated invite-only approach?
- How will success be communicated to non-engineering teams (one-pager vs interactive dashboard)?

Next immediate steps (by 2026-03-18):
1) Team owners submit scoped plan + dependencies (1 page each).
2) Infra publishes initial benchmark numbers and measurement rubric.
3) Comms drafts manager digest and slide template.
4) Exec meeting to finalize north star wording (2026-03-22).

Notes to self / rough thoughts:
- Keep the north star customer facing but actionable for engineering (avoid vague phrasing).
- Make OKRs small enough that progress is visible biweekly; avoid month-end-only pushes.
- Use the all-hands not just for announcements; make it a decision forum for sign-offs.

Appendix (links to artifacts to populate before finalization):
- Benchmark harness: github.com/redwood-inference/benchmarks (PR #4123 open)
- Confluence draft: TEAM-3month-northstar (linked above)
- OKR intake form: internal form (to be created)

"Notes to reviewers: edit inline, add a single sentence plan for your team under 'Action owners' by 2026-03-18. If you cannot hit dates, flag in #lead-prioritization with expected slippage and reason.

title: Pep Rally all-hands template and exec walkthrough (draft)
preview:
Context and intent:
This doc is a working template for a high-energy, short-format company all-hands (internal codename: "Pep Rally"). It combines an exec-level readout, a curated OKR highlight section, and a pragmatic run-of-show for a 60-minute session. Use it as the baseline for speaker prep, slide checklist, and follow-up asks.

Primary goals:
- Re-center the company on our production-first thesis: serving reliability, predictable economics, and private deployment momentum.
- Signal concrete OKR progress and where we need cross-org lifts for the next quarter.
- Energize teams with quick wins, customer war stories, and an explicit list of executive asks.
- Reduce post-all-hands confusion by mapping owners to follow-ups.

Audience:
- All employees (engineers, product, go-to-market, SRE, support).
- Executive leadership will use the 15-minute readout to set strategic context.

Success metrics for this event (how we measure whether it worked):
- Survey pulse: >= 70% of respondents report "clear on priorities for next 90 days".
- Action mapping: 90% of follow-ups assigned with owners and due dates within 48 hours.
- Slide hygiene: < 10% of submitted slides flagged for revision during speaker rehearsal.

High-level timing (60 minutes)
- 0:00–0:05: Welcome + housekeeping (MC) — logistics: captions, ask channel, Slack thread for follow-ups.
- 0:05–0:20: Exec readout (CEO + CTO) — 15 min: top-line progress, critical signals, three strategic asks.
- 0:20–0:35: OKR highlights and risk spotlight (Product VP) — 15 min: Quick wins, at-risk items, mitigation asks.
- 0:35–0:45: Customer story + demo snippet (Solutions/CS) — 10 min: Live customer excerpt or playback.
- 0:45–0:55: Team rapid-fire wins (4 teams x 2.5 min) — 10 min: short celebratory moments and ship highlights.
- 0:55–1:00: Calls-to-action and next steps (Head of Ops) — 5 min: Assign follow-ups, link to action board.

Draft company-level OKRs to surface (TL;DR for slides)
- OKR 1: Reliability & uptime (platform)
  - KR1.1: Maintain 99.95% platform availability for all hosted APIs (measured by region).
  - KR1.2: Reduce P95 latency for standard token generation by 15% through batching/kernels.
  - Signals to share: Dedicated customer SLA tests completed, 2 regressed endpoints identified.

- OKR 2: Cost-per-inference optimization
  - KR2.1: Reduce average per-token infra cost by 18% on production traffic via batching and quantized variants.
  - KR2.2: Pilot Redwood Optimize recommendations on three high-volume routes with measurable savings.
  - Signals: Pilot design doc ready; initial sim shows ~12% savings—need infra time to implement.

- OKR 3: Enterprise/private deployments momentum
  - KR3.1: Close two Private VPC deals (pilot contract signed) by end of quarter.
  - KR3.2: Ship on-prem install docs and HSM integration blueprint.
  - Signals: Legal has two redlines; field engineering capacity limited.

Exec readout talking points (concise bullets for CEO/CTO slides)
- Where we are: strong growth in hosted API adoption, Dedicated renewals holding, Private deals long but strategic.
- What matters next 90 days: stabilize top customer SLAs, accelerate Model Variant fallback work, and clear infra backlog for Optimize pilots.
- Three asks to org: (1) Prioritize two infra stories (P95 kernel patches), (2) Cross-team support for Private pilot onboarding, (3) Bring one high-impact eval to production for quality regression detection.

Run-of-show operational checklist (pre-week)
- Week -2: Ask leads to submit 1 slide per segment + speaker notes in shared folder.
- Week -1: Rehearsal scheduling — dry run with MC and AV.
- 72 hours out: Publish pre-read (1 pager) with 3 OKR signal charts and explicit asks.
- 24 hours out: Final slides submitted, accessibility check (captioning, color contrast).
- Day-of: MC script printed, Zoom link tested, fallback dial-in ready.

Slide checklist (per speaker)
- One-slide slide: 1 headline, 2 supporting bullets, one visual (chart or screenshot).
- Minimize text: aim for 6 lines max per slide.
- Add owner footer: name + follow-up JIRA/owner tag.

Customer story prep (CS)
- Two-minute recorded clip option (preferred) to avoid live flub.
- Focus: measurable impact (latency/cost/regulatory win).
- Ask customer for a 1-sentence quote and permission to share.

Rapid-fire wins guidance (team leads)
- Each team: 2x bullets (one shipped win, one near-term ask/need).
- Keep it human (names, shout-outs).

Planned follow-up workflow (post-all-hands)
- Immediately after: Publish meeting notes + slide deck to Team Drive and company channel.
- Action board (Trello/JIRA/Asana): create tickets for all asks. Owner + due date required within 48 hours.
- 1-week check-in: company-ops to surface any unowned items; escalate to exec if still unassigned.

Contingencies and fallbacks
- If key slide not ready: swap with pre-approved backlog slide (talk track prepped).
- If CEO/CTO delayed: MC delivers pre-read highlights and extends Q&A via Slack AMA.
- If AV fails: shift to audio-only, record locally, and post to drive.

Open questions / decisions to resolve in planning sync:
- Who will maintain the live action board post-event? (ops or product program manager?)
- Approve the 48-hour ownership SLA? Execs prefer 72h; ops prefers 48h for momentum.
- Which private pilot customer gets the lead engineer allocation (capacity conflict with Dedicated renewals).

Next steps (owners and dates)
- Ava Chen: assemble pre-read + one-pager by 2026-03-01.
- Marcus Díaz: collect slides and coordinate rehearsal slots by 2026-03-05.
- Leah Kapoor: confirm customer clip permissions and source quote by 2026-03-03.
- Jonah Rivera: create action-board template and post-event workflow doc by 2026-03-12.

Notes to speakers (informal):
- Keep tone crisp and optimistic; avoid long technical digressions in the exec readout.
- Use the "what we did / why it matters / what we need" cadence for every slide.
- Mark any sensitive legal/regulatory content with "[CONFIDENTIAL]" in the slide footer — we'll gate distribution.

Draft rehearsal script snippet (MC + CEO):
- MC: "Welcome everyone — quick housekeeping — captions enabled — slides and recording will be posted — three primary asks at the end. Over to Sam."
- CEO: "Thanks — quick thank you, top signals, and three asks — then hand to Mei for OKR highlights."

Appendix (supporting links & references)
- Template deck: team-drive:/allhands/templates/pep-rally-2026-deck (placeholder)
- Action-board sample: JIRA:RDW-1289 (board template)
- Pre-read examples: confluence:RDW-OKR-2026

Status: draft — this doc intentionally concise. Iterate in planning sync (owners above).

Quick micro-retro (post-event questions to include in pulse survey)
- Did the all-hands clarify priorities for your team? Y/N + short why
- Was the cadence useful (length/timing)? 1–5
- Which of the three exec asks are you already aligned to? (checkboxes)

END OF DRAFT

title: Transformer Codecrafting Regression Checklist (2026)
preview:
Purpose:
This is a working checklist and a compact prompt-set catalogue for onboarding transformer-based models into our code generation + refactor regression harness. Focus: ensure basic compilation, unit-test correctness, and adherence to style constraints (linting, docstrings, explicitness) across a representative code surface.

Scope / constraints:
- Target models: open transformer variants and quantized variants we might route to under Redwood Optimize.
- Languages covered in this first pass: Python (primary), a small curated set of JS/TS snippets.
- Tests are lightweight by design so they can run in CI for fast feedback; heavier integration tests live in the nightly pipeline.
- This doc intentionally mixes checklist items with exemplar prompts and quick evaluation heuristics — use as a living scratchpad for the onboarding engineer.

Quick summary of objectives:
1) Assert that generated code compiles for simple-to-medium snippets without manual edits.
2) Verify unit-test compatibility: generated code should pass small, deterministic unit tests derived from the prompt.
3) Measure style adherence with a simple rubric: lint-passes, docstring presence, clear variable names, no eval/exec usage.
4) Detect regressive behaviors: hallucinated APIs, incorrect imports, or brittle one-off heuristics.

How to use this doc (workflow):
- Pick model -> run canonical prompt-set (fast suite) -> collect compilation and unit test results -> score style heuristics -> manual spot-check on failures -> file JIRA with failing prompt and model outputs + minimal reproduction.
- After fixes/rollouts, re-run the same prompt-set and ensure regression delta <= threshold (see metrics below).

Prompt-set taxonomy (fast suite categories):
A. Minimal compile-only prompts (quick sanity checks)
  - \"Write a Python function named add that returns the sum of two integers.\"
  - \"Create a TypeScript function to debounce a callback with a given wait time.\"
B. Unit-test-driven generation (assertions included inline)
  - Provide function + 3 pytest cases; require function to satisfy tests.
  - Example: prompt asks for is_palindrome(s: str) with tests for typical and edge cases.
C. Refactor prompts that must preserve behavior (input-output contract)
  - Given a working snippet, ask the model to refactor to make it iterative/functional/typed without changing tests.
  - Include explicit instructions: \"Do not change public API. Keep behavior identical for given tests.\"
D. Style-constrained outputs
  - Linting rules enforced: max-line-length 100, no trailing whitespace, variable names > 2 chars, add docstrings to public functions.
  - Ask model to follow PEP8 and include module-level docstring.
E. Edge-case / API hallucination detection
  - Prompts that require using standard library only; ensure model doesn't invent third-party imports.

Canonical test cases (examples):
1) Fibonacci (memoized) - compile + small performance sanity: 10th fib matches expected.
2) CSV parser for two-line CSV with headers - unit tests check parsing and header normalization.
3) Simple HTTP client wrapper (JS) - must not import non-standard modules. Tests stub fetch.
4) JSON schema validator (small subset) - ensure exceptions and type checks preserved after refactor.

Sample prompt (unit-driven example):
\"Write a Python function normalize_phone(phone: str) -> str. Requirements: normalize to E.164-like digits-only string (country code optional). Do not call external services. Add a docstring and include three pytest cases below. The function should raise ValueError for empty input.\"

Expected generated structure (for CI parsing):
- One code block containing the function implementation.
- A separate code block containing pytest functions named test_* (3 tests).
- No extra markdown or commentary.

Scoring heuristics (fast pass):
- Compilation score (0/1): does the snippet import/run without SyntaxError or immediate runtime error for the test harness?
- Unit-test score (0..1 scale): percent of small pytest cases passing in an isolated environment.
- Style score (0..1): weighted average of Lint pass (0.5), docstring presence (0.3), secure patterns (no exec/eval) (0.2).
- Hallucination flag (boolean string): true if any non-standard import or fake API is present.

Thresholds for regression alerting:
- Fail if compilation score == 0 for >5% of prompts in fast suite.
- Alert if avg unit-test score drops by >0.1 (10 percentage points) from baseline for a given model family.
- Block rollout (stop onboarding) if hallucination flag true for critical prompts (I/O contracts, security related).

Manual review guidance (spot-checking):
- Always inspect the top 5 failing prompts for: incorrect edge-case handling, missing error checks, and unsafe ops.
- Compare variable names and API choices to a short oracle of expected patterns (e.g., prefer pathlib over string concatenation for file paths in Python).

Examples of failure modes to capture with prompts:
- Silent incorrect behavior: function returns plausible but incorrect value for edge case.
- Partial code + comments: model outputs explanatory text mixed with code leading to CI parsing failures.
- Overfitting to training examples: model emits a hardcoded solution (e.g., using a fixed dataset id).
- Style regressions: missing docstrings, single-letter vars, or unsafe dynamic code.

CI integration notes (practical):
- We wrap each generated snippet into a small harness that extracts the first code block and pytest blocks. If parser fails, mark compilation failure with the raw output attached.
- Use sandboxed runner (Docker) pinned to reproducible runtime: python:3.11-slim for these tests. JS snippets run in node:18-slim with jest-lite harness.
- Save artifacts: generated prompt, model id + config, raw output, parsed code, test harness logs. Attach to bug reports.

Rolling baseline / metrics storage:
- Baseline per-model family is computed from the first successful onboarding run (fast suite). Store: compilation_rate, mean_test_pass_rate, mean_style_score, hallucination_rate.
- Keep a 30-day rolling window for trend detection.

Prompt engineering tips to reduce flakiness:
- Keep tests small and deterministic. Avoid randomized seeds unless explicitly required.
- Force output format: \"IMPLEMENTATION:
<code>
TESTS:
<code>\" - increases parser reliability.
- Encourage explicit imports in prompt (\"Import only from the Python standard library\").

Unresolved questions / TODOs:
- Decide canonical JS testing harness (jest vs mocha) for small snippets. (Priya action)
- Add medium-weight integration cases to nightly pipeline (Ethan action).
- Evaluate whether to extend style rubric to include type annotations coverage.

Meeting notes from 2026-02-26 sync (quick):
- Miguel suggested adding a \"no network calls\" hard assertion to critical security prompts.
- Agreement to treat refactor prompts as high-signal for hallucination because they often reword dependencies.

Practical next steps for onboarding an example model (checklist):
- [ ] Run fast suite (A..E) and record metrics.
- [ ] Manually review top-5 failures and mark false positives.
- [ ] Create JIRA with minimal reproduction for true failures.
- [ ] Re-run after retrain/tune; confirm regression delta within thresholds.
- [ ] Promote model to canary routing if success metrics above threshold for 72h.

Appendix: short prompt-set (copy-paste ready)
1) COMPILE: \"Write a Python function multiply(a: int, b: int) -> int. Only output code.\"
2) UNIT: \"Implement is_anagram(s1: str, s2: str) -> bool. Include 3 pytest cases under TESTS: block. Do not include any prose.\"
3) REFACTOR: \"Refactor the following function to be iterative without changing its I/O. CODE: <insert snippet>. Keep same function signature and include tests.\"
4) STYLE: \"Rewrite module to follow PEP8. Add docstrings and type hints. Break long lines to <=100 chars.\"

Contact / owners:
- Primary: Ava Chen (eng-applied-ml)
- Secondary reviewers: Miguel Torres, Priya Nair

Notes to self: keep this doc terse — move long experiment logs into confluence page linked above. When adding new prompts, prefer short functional tests rather than large dataset-driven examples.

Sample prompt (unit-driven example):
\"Write a Python function normalize_phone(phone: str) -> str. Requirements: normalize to E.164-like digits-only string (country code optional). Do not call external services. Add a docstring and include three pytest cases below. The function should raise ValueError for empty input.\"

Expected generated structure (for CI parsing):
- One code block containing the function implementation.
- A separate code block containing pytest functions named test_* (3 tests).
- No extra markdown or commentary.

Scoring heuristics (fast pass):
- Compilation score (0/1): does the snippet import/run without SyntaxError or immediate runtime error for the test harness?
- Unit-test score (0..1 scale): percent of small pytest cases passing in an isolated environment.
- Style score (0..1): weighted average of Lint pass (0.5), docstring presence (0.3), secure patterns (no exec/eval) (0.2).
- Hallucination flag (boolean string): true if any non-standard import or fake API is present.

Thresholds for regression alerting:
- Fail if compilation score == 0 for >5% of prompts in fast suite.
- Alert if avg unit-test score drops by >0.1 (10 percentage points) from baseline for a given model family.
- Block rollout (stop onboarding) if hallucination flag true for critical prompts (I/O contracts, security related).

Manual review guidance (spot-checking):
- Always inspect the top 5 failing prompts for: incorrect edge-case handling, missing error checks, and unsafe ops.
- Compare variable names and API choices to a short oracle of expected patterns (e.g., prefer pathlib over string concatenation for file paths in Python).

Examples of failure modes to capture with prompts:
- Silent incorrect behavior: function returns plausible but incorrect value for edge case.
- Partial code + comments: model outputs explanatory text mixed with code leading to CI parsing failures.
- Overfitting to training examples: model emits a hardcoded solution (e.g., using a fixed dataset id).
- Style regressions: missing docstrings, single-letter vars, or unsafe dynamic code.

CI integration notes (practical):
- We wrap each generated snippet into a small harness that extracts the first code block and pytest blocks. If parser fails, mark compilation failure with the raw output attached.
- Use sandboxed runner (Docker) pinned to reproducible runtime: python:3.11-slim for these tests. JS snippets run in node:18-slim with jest-lite harness.
- Save artifacts: generated prompt, model id + config, raw output, parsed code, test harness logs. Attach to bug reports.

Rolling baseline / metrics storage:
- Baseline per-model family is computed from the first successful onboarding run (fast suite). Store: compilation_rate, mean_test_pass_rate, mean_style_score, hallucination_rate.
- Keep a 30-day rolling window for trend detection.

Prompt engineering tips to reduce flakiness:
- Keep tests small and deterministic. Avoid randomized seeds unless explicitly required.
- Force output format: \"IMPLEMENTATION:
<code>
TESTS:
<code>\" - increases parser reliability.
- Encourage explicit imports in prompt (\"Import only from the Python standard library\").

Unresolved questions / TODOs:
- Decide canonical JS testing harness (jest vs mocha) for small snippets. (Priya action)
- Add medium-weight integration cases to nightly pipeline (Ethan action).
- Evaluate whether to extend style rubric to include type annotations coverage.

Meeting notes from 2026-02-26 sync (quick):
- Miguel suggested adding a \"no network calls\" hard assertion to critical security prompts.
- Agreement to treat refactor prompts as high-signal for hallucination because they often reword dependencies.

Practical next steps for onboarding an example model (checklist):
- [ ] Run fast suite (A..E) and record metrics.
- [ ] Manually review top-5 failures and mark false positives.
- [ ] Create JIRA with minimal reproduction for true failures.
- [ ] Re-run after retrain/tune; confirm regression delta within thresholds.
- [ ] Promote model to canary routing if success metrics above threshold for 72h.

Appendix: short prompt-set (copy-paste ready)
1) COMPILE: \"Write a Python function multiply(a: int, b: int) -> int. Only output code.\"
2) UNIT: \"Implement is_anagram(s1: str, s2: str) -> bool. Include 3 pytest cases under TESTS: block. Do not include any prose.\"
3) REFACTOR: \"Refactor the following function to be iterative without changing its I/O. CODE: <insert snippet>. Keep same function signature and include tests.\"
4) STYLE: \"Rewrite module to follow PEP8. Add docstrings and type hints. Break long lines to <=100 chars.\"

Contact / owners:
- Primary: Ava Chen (eng-applied-ml)
- Secondary reviewers: Miguel Torres, Priya Nair

Notes to self: keep this doc terse — move long experiment logs into confluence page linked above. When adding new prompts, prefer short functional tests rather than large dataset-driven examples.

title: company-all-hands-facilitator-script-and-rapid-qna-v0-1
preview:
Purpose: A concise facilitator script, contingency playbook, and rapid Q&A format for the upcoming company all-hands. Use this as the single source for run-of-show, speaker prompts, timeboxing rules, and follow-ups. This doc is intentionally prescriptive so the MC can keep pace and protect speaker time while surfacing high-impact questions and OKR decisions.

Objectives (top-level):
- Surface 3 cross-company decisions that need exec alignment this quarter.
- Communicate high-level OKR progress and 2 notable pivots.
- Maintain a 60-minute total runtime with a structured 10-minute rapid Q&A at the end.
- Capture and triage every question to an owner within 24 hours.

Audience: All employees (engineering, product, GTM, SRE, CS, security). Expect ~140 attendees live + stream.

Pre-reads (send 48h before):
- 1-page OKR snapshot (what’s green/yellow/red) — link above.
- Short exec update deck (5 slides): priorities, capacity, customer signal.
- Two short ask artifacts for decisions (policy doc + proposal).

Run-of-show (60 minutes) — facilitator cues included:
00:00–03:00 — Welcome + housekeeping (MC: opening line)
  - Script: "Good afternoon — quick logistics: live captioning on, recording on, use the Q&A box for long questions and 'raise hand' for lightning asks. We'll capture everything and assign owners."
  - House rules: cameras optional, mute when not speaking, 1 follow-up per person in live Q&A.

03:00–12:00 — CEO state of the company (9 min)
  - Cue slide 1: vision -> 90 sec recap.
  - Cue slide 2: market momentum & customer signals (2 min).
  - Cue slide 3: three decisions requiring alignment (5 min) — each decision: one-sentence context, recommended option, ask for thumbs up.
  - MC prompt: "After each decision, we'll capture reactions in the Q&A and record a small decision note in the doc."

12:00–28:00 — Product + Platform update (16 min)
  - Owner: Head of Product (8 min) — product wins, one product pivot, ask for resource trade-off.
  - Owner: Head of Serving Runtime (8 min) — latency/cost metrics & Dedicated/Private rollout status.
  - Facilitator tag-lines: "Call out the metrics that map to customer SLAs" and "State mitigation steps for any red metrics."

28:00–38:00 — Customer & GTM highlight (10 min)
  - Customer story (3 min) + net-new pipeline signal (3 min) + go-to-market ask (4 min).

38:00–48:00 — Ops, Security & Reliability brief (10 min)
  - SRE quick wins (4 min), Security posture (3 min), Compliance/regulatory note for Private deployments (3 min).
  - If any item is high-risk, MC to call for a follow-up decision session (owner + deadline).

48:00–58:00 — Rapid Q&A (10 min) — structured format
  - Format: 6 lightning slots (90 seconds each) + 1 wildcard slot (2 minutes).
  - Selection: pre-filtered by moderators; priority to cross-team, decision-critical, customer-impacting questions.
  - MC script: "We'll take six lightning questions. Each speaker: 60 seconds to ask, 30 seconds for a direct answer. If the topic needs more, it goes to the parking lot with an owner."
  - Moderator instructions: tag each question with one of: [#decision, #bug, #followup, #context]. Assign owner in real time.

58:00–60:00 — Close & next steps (2 min)
  - Quick recap of decisions made, owners, and deadlines. Reminder: survey link + office hours for deeper dives.

Facilitator playbook (practical tips):
- Timeboxing: visible countdown in the presenter view. Stop speakers at the minute mark if overrunning (polite interjection script provided below).
- Polite interjection script: "Great signal — to keep us on time, let's park the deep dive and assign an owner. Can you capture this in the doc and lead a 30-min follow-up?"
- Tone: low-friction, upward-facing candor, remind people of safety to speak up.
- Decision framing: for each 'decision' slot, state context, options, recommended option, and 'ask' (approve/revisit/defer).

Rapid Q&A moderation guide:
- Moderator roles: 1 primary moderator (Priya Shah) to curate, 1 backchannel moderator (Slack) to route live reactions, 1 note-taker (Jon Park) to capture owners and action items.
- Filtering heuristics: prioritize questions that: affect more than one team; block delivery; concern a public customer; relate to safety/compliance.
- If >12 high-priority Qs, run a 30-min follow-up office hours and communicate time/window in chat.

Contingency & outage scripts:
- Streaming failure (common): immediate step — MC says "We're experiencing streaming issues; we'll continue the in-room session and post the recording. Please use the Q&A thread — we'll triage." Assign streaming owner and post status in #all-hands.
- Speaker no-show: have a 3-minute placeholder (short customer video or pre-recorded highlight). If the missing speaker is critical, CEO to offer a 2-minute bridge and reschedule a follow-up session.
- Major incident occurring same day: postpone the all-hands or move to a 'status-focused' format. Decision to postpone rests with CEO + Head of SRE. Notify company within 30 minutes of decision.

Decision capture template (to be filled live by note-taker):
- Decision ID (e.g., AH-2026-03-1)
- Short statement (one sentence)
- Options considered (bullet)
- Chosen option
- Owner (name + email)
- Deadline (YYYY-MM-DD)
- Follow-up meeting link (if scheduled)

Post-meeting follow-up timeline:
- Within 6 hours: publish recording + raw Q&A export + decision notes.
- Within 24 hours: owners assigned on each action and initial contact to requesters.
- Within 72 hours: schedule any post-decision working sessions.

Rehearsal checklist (48–24 hours prior):
- Confirm slides for all speakers (size & font).
- AV check (mic, camera, room mix, captions) — run once with CEO and Product lead.
- Moderator dry run (Priya + Jon + MC) for rapid Q&A flow.
- Confirm recording destination and SEO-friendly title for the published video.

Speaker-facing scripts (short snippets to reduce ad-libbing):
- Overrun nudge: "Wrap in 30s: key punchline + one action for the audience."
- Decision ask: "We are asking you to approve XYZ so we can move forward; this enables [benefit]."
- Vulnerability line (for red metrics): "We expect this to be uncomfortable — here is why, and here is how we are mitigating it."

Feedback and measurement:
- Survey (2 minutes) distributed after the event: 3 questions (useful? decisions clear? any blocking asks?).
- Metrics to monitor: live attendance, recording plays (7-day), % of questions closed within 72 hours, NPS-like item for all-hands utility.

Parking lot & follow-ups (live capture examples):
- "OKR cadence: deeper sync" -> Owner: Marcus Rivera -> Deadline: 2026-03-18
- "Dedicated capacity pricing clarity" -> Owner: Elena Morales -> Deadline: 2026-03-12
- "Security audit schedule for Private" -> Owner: Security Lead (TBD) -> Deadline: 2026-03-20

Open questions for planning team (to resolve in rehearsal):
- Do we want a live polling block to sense consent on the three exec decisions, or do thumbs-up in chat suffice?
- Confirm: who publishes the final decision note (CEO staff or Ops)?

Appendix: sequences to preserve sanity during the show
- If one segment runs long by >2 min, compress the product and GTM sections by 50/50.
- Keeper rule: never cut the CEO's decision block. Shorten narrative, not decisions.

Notes to self (facilitator): be prescriptive, show visible time, and protect the audience's time. This doc is intentionally concise — do not read it verbatim, use as cues.

End of doc.

title: Stability Sherpa: Launch checklist + KPI guide
preview:
Purpose:
This is a compact, action-oriented launch readiness and KPI playbook with a bias for stability-first decisions. The "Stability Sherpa" concept: guide the launch through terrain (ramp phases) with conservative checkpoints, clear signal thresholds, and pre-approved fallback moves so engineering and product can move quickly without guessing.

Scope:
- Applies to any product-ops led feature activation that routes traffic to Redwood hosted inference (hosted API) or Dedicated pools.
- Focus: preflight signals, KPI targets per phase, quick triage/contingency actions.
- Not covered: long-term post-mortem templates (link to PMR).

Quick summary (one-line):
If 3 consecutive signal probes breach thresholds in a phase, pause ramp and run the phase rollback checklist.

Launch phases and goals:
1) Canary (0.1% traffic, 1–2 hours)
   - Goal: functional correctness, no infra error spikes, latency P95 within +30% of baseline.
   - KPIs to watch: errors/sec <= 0.5x baseline spike, P95 latency <= baseline*1.3, token-cost drift <= +10%.
   - Probes: smoke tests, 10 scripted user journeys, two external customers on opt-in test.

2) Gradual ramp (0.1% -> 5% over 24–72h)
   - Goal: operational stability under realistic concurrency.
   - KPIs: error rate trending flat (<= x1.2 baseline), rate-limited 95th latency <= SLT (see targets), token-cost per request within model budget.
   - Probes: continuous load test (15m every hour), KV-cache hit rate, model fallback invocations.

3) Scale to cohort (5% -> 30% over 7 days)
   - Goal: economic signal + reliability across clusters and regions.
   - KPIs: sustained throughput, SLO attainment >= 99.5% for dedicated customers, cost per 1k tokens within projected range.

4) Full rollout (30% -> 100%)
   - Goal: steady state. metrics stable across 72h.

Per-phase KPI table (baseline = last week median):
- P50 latency target: baseline * 1.05 (Canary), *1.10 (Gradual), *1.20 (Scale), *1.25 (Full)
- P95 latency target: baseline *1.3, *1.5, *1.6, *1.8 respectively
- Error rate: no >3x sudden spike sustained >5 minutes; rolling 5m error rate must return to <=1.2x baseline within 15m after mitigation move
- Cost per 1k tokens: budget delta <= +15% during Gradual; re-evaluate quantization config if >+20%

Preflight checklist (run within 48h of Canary):
- [ ] Smoke coverage for top 20 user prompts (owner: Solutions Eng)
- [ ] Canary keys and quotas provisioned (owner: Console)
- [ ] Dedicated pool autoscale policy validated in staging (owner: Dedicated infra)
- [ ] Observability: dashboards and alerting configured (SRE)
- [ ] Canary rollback playbook approved with on-call (owner: SRE lead)
- [ ] Load ramp script and synthetic dataset checked in (owner: Applied ML)
- [ ] Security scan / KMS access verified for customer-owned keys (owner: Security)
- [ ] Legal/compliance signoff for data handling (owner: Legal)
- [ ] Customer communications draft for beta cohort (owner: CS)

Critical run signals (immediate triage if observed):
- Sustained P95 latency > threshold for 3 consecutive probes (see phase targets)
- Error rate increase >3x baseline for >5 minutes
- Model fallback or degraded model used >2% of requests over 10 minutes
- KV cache hit rate drops >15% compared to baseline leading to token-cost spike
- Any customer-reported data mishandling incident (immediate pause)

Decision matrix (Go / Pause / Rollback) - shorthand:
- Green: All signals within phase targets -> proceed to next ramp step after holding period
- Amber: One or two non-critical signals out of band but remediated within 30m -> hold and observe 2x probe cycles
- Red: Critical signal persists (see above) -> PAUSE traffic, trigger rollback plan, notify execs + customers if affected

Rollback plan (pre-approved):
- Step 1: Pause new traffic routing (Console) and pin requests to previous model or pool variant.
- Step 2: Trigger automated rollback job (PR failover route) and re-route 100% of canary cohort to previous model.
- Step 3: Open triage channel: #launch-sherpa (Slack) with SRE, Applied ML, Product Ops, Solutions Eng, CS.
- Step 4: Post-rollback health probes for 30m; if stable, incrementally reattempt after root cause patch.

Alerts and paging rules to validate before launch:
- Pager on-call for infra SRE for P95 latency > threshold for >10m
- Pager for increase in 5m error rate >5x baseline
- Pager for model fallback >5% sustained for 15m
- Email to stakeholders for cost drift >+25% for 1h window

Stakeholders and owners (primary contact):
- Product Ops (launch lead): Ava Chen (ava.chen@redwood.ai)
- SRE / Observability: Ethan Lewis (ethan.lewis@redwood.ai)
- Applied ML: Priya Nair (priya.nair@redwood.ai)
- Dedicated Infra: Marco Alvarez (marco.alvarez@redwood.ai)
- Customer Success: Lila Morgan (lila.morgan@redwood.ai)
- Security: Noemi Park (noemi.park@redwood.ai)

Triage playbook (first 30 minutes):
- 00–05m: Acknowledge alert, post initial details to #launch-sherpa, identify if signal is infra, model, or config.
- 05–15m: Run fast diagnostics: review recent deploys, kernel selection changes, quantization toggles, autoscale events.
- 15–30m: Apply one safe mitigation (route to degraded model, increase pool capacity or throttle) and observe; escalate to pause if no improvement.

Metrics to collect for post-mortem and decisioning (automated wherever possible):
- Per-route P50/P95/P99 latencies, errors/sec, traced requests with stack traces, request/response token counts, KV-cache hit rate, model-fallback counts, CPU/GPU utilization per node, autoscale events, billing burn rate by route.

Open questions / TODOs (work-in-progress):
- Q1: Define conservative fallback model variant naming convention (owner: Applied ML) - TODO by 2026-03-05
- Q2: Add synthetic user story for rare edge-case prompt that caused earlier hallucination (owner: Solutions Eng)
- Q3: Formalize customer communication templates (owner: CS) - require legal review

Post-launch acceptance criteria (for marking phase as stable):
- 72h continuous retention of KPIs within phase targets after final ramp step
- No rollback events during those 72h other than transient (<=10m) remediation
- Customer satisfaction metric: no >3 support tickets attributable to model regressions in cohort per week

Notes / rough heuristics (internal):
- When in doubt, prefer pausing traffic and investigating. The cost of a short pause + fast rollback is typically far lower than chasing intermittent degradations in prod.
- Use the Stability Sherpa glossary: "probe" = short synthetic run, "hold window" = 2 consecutive probe cycles (~30–60m), "phase anchor" = baseline for the phase.

Signoffs (pre-assigned approvers for go/no-go):
- Product Ops lead: Ava Chen (digital sign-off)
- SRE lead: Ethan Lewis
- Applied ML lead: Priya Nair
- Legal/compliance: Security counsel (Noemi Park)

Appendix A — Example alert thresholds (fill with actual baseline numbers before canary):
- P95 latency threshold (Canary): < 650ms
- 5m error rate threshold: < 0.5%
- KV cache hit rate: > 70%
- Model fallback rate: < 1%
- Billing burn alert: +20% over projected cost for 1h

Appendix B — Quick links & artifacts:
- Launch dashboard (placeholder): https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/launch-stability-sherpa
- Canary synthetic runbook: github.com/redwood-infra/canary-scripts/blob/main/runbook.md
- Postmortem template: https://confluence.redwood.ai/pages/POSTMORTEM-TPL

End notes:
This doc is intentionally concise — keep it as the single source of triage-first actions for launches. Expand per-feature with embedding of baselines and per-route numbers in the project-specific subfolder (link to project page).

Quick edit history (notes):
- 2026-02-15 Ava: initial draft
- 2026-03-02 Priya: added rollback playbook and model fallback signals
- 2026-03-10 Ethan: refined probe cadence and alert thresholds

4) Full rollout (30% -> 100%)
   - Goal: steady state. metrics stable across 72h.

Per-phase KPI table (baseline = last week median):
- P50 latency target: baseline * 1.05 (Canary), *1.10 (Gradual), *1.20 (Scale), *1.25 (Full)
- P95 latency target: baseline *1.3, *1.5, *1.6, *1.8 respectively
- Error rate: no >3x sudden spike sustained >5 minutes; rolling 5m error rate must return to <=1.2x baseline within 15m after mitigation move
- Cost per 1k tokens: budget delta <= +15% during Gradual; re-evaluate quantization config if >+20%

Preflight checklist (run within 48h of Canary):
- [ ] Smoke coverage for top 20 user prompts (owner: Solutions Eng)
- [ ] Canary keys and quotas provisioned (owner: Console)
- [ ] Dedicated pool autoscale policy validated in staging (owner: Dedicated infra)
- [ ] Observability: dashboards and alerting configured (SRE)
- [ ] Canary rollback playbook approved with on-call (owner: SRE lead)
- [ ] Load ramp script and synthetic dataset checked in (owner: Applied ML)
- [ ] Security scan / KMS access verified for customer-owned keys (owner: Security)
- [ ] Legal/compliance signoff for data handling (owner: Legal)
- [ ] Customer communications draft for beta cohort (owner: CS)

Critical run signals (immediate triage if observed):
- Sustained P95 latency > threshold for 3 consecutive probes (see phase targets)
- Error rate increase >3x baseline for >5 minutes
- Model fallback or degraded model used >2% of requests over 10 minutes
- KV cache hit rate drops >15% compared to baseline leading to token-cost spike
- Any customer-reported data mishandling incident (immediate pause)

Decision matrix (Go / Pause / Rollback) - shorthand:
- Green: All signals within phase targets -> proceed to next ramp step after holding period
- Amber: One or two non-critical signals out of band but remediated within 30m -> hold and observe 2x probe cycles
- Red: Critical signal persists (see above) -> PAUSE traffic, trigger rollback plan, notify execs + customers if affected

Rollback plan (pre-approved):
- Step 1: Pause new traffic routing (Console) and pin requests to previous model or pool variant.
- Step 2: Trigger automated rollback job (PR failover route) and re-route 100% of canary cohort to previous model.
- Step 3: Open triage channel: #launch-sherpa (Slack) with SRE, Applied ML, Product Ops, Solutions Eng, CS.
- Step 4: Post-rollback health probes for 30m; if stable, incrementally reattempt after root cause patch.

Alerts and paging rules to validate before launch:
- Pager on-call for infra SRE for P95 latency > threshold for >10m
- Pager for increase in 5m error rate >5x baseline
- Pager for model fallback >5% sustained for 15m
- Email to stakeholders for cost drift >+25% for 1h window

Stakeholders and owners (primary contact):
- Product Ops (launch lead): Ava Chen (ava.chen@redwood.ai)
- SRE / Observability: Ethan Lewis (ethan.lewis@redwood.ai)
- Applied ML: Priya Nair (priya.nair@redwood.ai)
- Dedicated Infra: Marco Alvarez (marco.alvarez@redwood.ai)
- Customer Success: Lila Morgan (lila.morgan@redwood.ai)
- Security: Noemi Park (noemi.park@redwood.ai)

Triage playbook (first 30 minutes):
- 00–05m: Acknowledge alert, post initial details to #launch-sherpa, identify if signal is infra, model, or config.
- 05–15m: Run fast diagnostics: review recent deploys, kernel selection changes, quantization toggles, autoscale events.
- 15–30m: Apply one safe mitigation (route to degraded model, increase pool capacity or throttle) and observe; escalate to pause if no improvement.

Metrics to collect for post-mortem and decisioning (automated wherever possible):
- Per-route P50/P95/P99 latencies, errors/sec, traced requests with stack traces, request/response token counts, KV-cache hit rate, model-fallback counts, CPU/GPU utilization per node, autoscale events, billing burn rate by route.

Open questions / TODOs (work-in-progress):
- Q1: Define conservative fallback model variant naming convention (owner: Applied ML) - TODO by 2026-03-05
- Q2: Add synthetic user story for rare edge-case prompt that caused earlier hallucination (owner: Solutions Eng)
- Q3: Formalize customer communication templates (owner: CS) - require legal review

Post-launch acceptance criteria (for marking phase as stable):
- 72h continuous retention of KPIs within phase targets after final ramp step
- No rollback events during those 72h other than transient (<=10m) remediation
- Customer satisfaction metric: no >3 support tickets attributable to model regressions in cohort per week

Notes / rough heuristics (internal):
- When in doubt, prefer pausing traffic and investigating. The cost of a short pause + fast rollback is typically far lower than chasing intermittent degradations in prod.
- Use the Stability Sherpa glossary: "probe" = short synthetic run, "hold window" = 2 consecutive probe cycles (~30–60m), "phase anchor" = baseline for the phase.

Signoffs (pre-assigned approvers for go/no-go):
- Product Ops lead: Ava Chen (digital sign-off)
- SRE lead: Ethan Lewis
- Applied ML lead: Priya Nair
- Legal/compliance: Security counsel (Noemi Park)

Appendix A — Example alert thresholds (fill with actual baseline numbers before canary):
- P95 latency threshold (Canary): < 650ms
- 5m error rate threshold: < 0.5%
- KV cache hit rate: > 70%
- Model fallback rate: < 1%
- Billing burn alert: +20% over projected cost for 1h

Appendix B — Quick links & artifacts:
- Launch dashboard (placeholder): https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/launch-stability-sherpa
- Canary synthetic runbook: github.com/redwood-infra/canary-scripts/blob/main/runbook.md
- Postmortem template: https://confluence.redwood.ai/pages/POSTMORTEM-TPL

End notes:
This doc is intentionally concise — keep it as the single source of triage-first actions for launches. Expand per-feature with embedding of baselines and per-route numbers in the project-specific subfolder (link to project page).

Quick edit history (notes):
- 2026-02-15 Ava: initial draft
- 2026-03-02 Priya: added rollback playbook and model fallback signals
- 2026-03-10 Ethan: refined probe cadence and alert thresholds

title: Orbit Energy QBR narrative + renewal playbook (draft)
preview:
Purpose: quick, slide-forward narrative for the Orbit Energy QBR (customer exec + SWAT). Goal = align on value delivered, surface adoption blockers, propose renewal path (12/24 mo) and optional Dedicated pilot.

Meeting format (40 minutes):
- 5m: Welcome + agenda (Exec sponsor, CSM)
- 10m: What went well (metrics + short customer quotes)
- 10m: Where we saw friction + product asks (concrete examples)
- 10m: Renewal options & commercial proposal (choices + impact)
- 5m: Next steps / action owners

Slide-by-slide outline (speaker bullets / prompts):
1) Title slide
   - Speaker: Ava (CS lead)
   - Prompt: Thank you for partnership; quick agenda; confirm timebox.

2) Executive summary (1 slide)
   - 30s: Headline: "Orbit Energy: 3.4x inference throughput, 22% lower token cost month-over-month" (call out period Jan–Feb 2026).
   - 10s: Renewal ask preview: "Recommend 12-mo committed Dedicated + 2-month proof-of-concept for predictive maintenance workload."

3) Business outcomes and KPIs (2 slides)
   - Adoption: active routes increased from 2 -> 7 (Q4'25 -> Q1'26).
   - Cost: unit cost (tokens) decreased 22% after caching + batching config change (see appendix for config).
   - Latency: p50 down from 230ms -> 110ms (important for control-room UX).
   - Speaker note: cite Orbit internal metric dashboard (linked).

4) Customer stories & quotes (1 slide)
   - Pull 2 short quotes from product and reliability leads (Marcus to confirm exact phrasing).
   - Quick annotation: which feature drove the win (KV caching + model variant routing).

5) Technical deep-dive (1 slide + appendix refs)
   - Short summary: migration to optimized quantized variant (r-infer-v2-q4) during high-load windows.
   - Highlight: one incident (2026-01-12) resolved by auto-fallback policy; show time-to-recovery improvement.

6) Current friction / open requests (1–2 slides)
   - Biggest pain: S3-integrated prompt store sync has ~7% missing snippets for long tails — causes human QA rework.
   - Feature ask: partial KV eviction policy + improved SDK error codes (Orbit asked for clearer retry semantics).
   - Risk: headcount change at Orbit (platform lead leaving) increases risk for complex onboarding.

7) Renewal options (1 slide)
   - Option A (Recommended): 12-month Dedicated pool (x GPUs) + 2-month migration aid; 10% discount vs list; SLA 99.9% uptime.
   - Option B: 6-month Dedicated + incremental burst capacity; 5% discount.
   - Option C: Stay hosted, scale with usage-based pricing (no commitment).
   - Financial framing: show ARR delta, estimated unit cost, and cost-per-inference saved. (Finance to verify final numbers)

8) Objection handling / negotiation playbook (1 slide)
   - If they push on price: reframe as predictable unit cost + lower ops overhead; offer pilot that demonstrates 8–12% additional unit-cost savings.
   - If they push on feature roadmap timing: offer prioritized backlog slot (one sprint) conditional on 12-mo commitment.

9) Proposed timeline & next steps (1 slide)
   - Sign by Apr 15 -> Dedicated provisioning and migration kickoff by May 1 -> Migrate pilot routes in 3–4 weeks -> Full cutover by Q3.
   - Owners: Ava (CS), Marcus (Solutions Eng), Sophie (Account Exec).

10) Appendix / data backup (multiple slides)
   - Usage graphs, latency percentiles, cost breakout per route, incident timeline, config diffs, contract language block.

Tailored talking points (for exec + platform lead):
- Exec: "Orbit has seen measurable reduction in per-call variability and a predictable decrease in forecasting error for the predictive maintenance model. That translates directly to fewer false dispatches and ~$$ saved per month."
- Platform lead: "We can reduce your tail-latency by shifting less-sensitive routes to q4-quantized models and reserving Dedicated pool for control-room heavy routes — this avoids overprovisioning for the rest of your systems."
- Procurement/legal: "We offer standard SOC2 addendum and a managed key solution; we can include a 30-day rollback clause for the first migration."

Data points to confirm before final deck (TODO):
- Verify exact monthly cost savings number (Finance: Diego).
- Confirm quotes and permission to publish in deck (Orbit legal).
- Pull incident post-mortem link to include in appendix (SRE).

Renewal checklist (internal):
- [ ] Commercial memo completed (Sophie)
- [ ] Custom contract language for KMS / data residency (Legal)
- [ ] Migration runbook + SRE oncall window (Marcus + SRE)
- [ ] Pilot success criteria (latency target, error-rate target, cost target)
- [ ] Exec sponsor alignment from Orbit (Ava to confirm)

Negotiation levers (internal playbook):
- Trade: discount % vs committed GPUs months.
- Add-on: longer data retention in Console logs for 90 days (paid).
- Accelerators: technical hours for migration (onboarding credit).

Notes to self / rough language for slide speaker notes: 
- "We recommend locking in Dedicated capacity for your control-room routes to guarantee latency under 150ms p95 during peak window. For less latency-sensitive routes, we will keep them on hosted with autoscaling. This hybrid approach reduces overall spend while keeping the latency SLAs you care about."
- "We propose a 60-day success window for the pilot: if latency targets and cost-savings are met, we transition remaining routes into the Dedicated pool."

Potential risks (for leadership):
- Account churn risk if staffing change delays migration > 8 weeks.
- Feature gap (partial eviction + SDK error semantics) not shipped by expected date could increase support load.

Attachments to produce for final deck:
- Migration runbook (one-pager)
- Sizing estimate for Dedicated pool (by GPU type)
- Sample contract clause for data residency + rollback
- Appendix charts (latency, cost per route, usage growth)

Ownership & next steps after this draft:
- Marcus: produce pilot runbook and sizing (due 2026-03-17)
- Diego: cost reconciliation and outbound financial slide (due 2026-03-18)
- Sophie: finalize commercial options and draft redlines (due 2026-03-20)
- Ava: confirm Orbit exec sponsor + get permission to show internal quotes (due 2026-03-12)

Misc notes (informal):
- Keep appendix heavy — Orbit loves data.
- Use control-room screenshots sparingly (ask legal).
- If they ask for on-prem, flag as long lead; push for VPC Private deployment as near-term compromise.

Revision log (brief):
- v1: initial notes (2026-02-15) by Ava
- v2: added pricing options and pilot language (2026-03-02) by Sophie
- v3: updated metrics after Feb usage (2026-03-10) by Diego — this is current draft

Conversations to link: Slack thread #cs-orbit-energy, PRD: linear CS-789, Confluence hub above.

--- END ---

title: Code polish probe promptset and onboarding playbook
preview:
Purpose:
This is a working promptset + playbook for onboarding new generation/refactor-capable models into the canned code-refactor regression harness. The focus is small-to-medium code rewrite tasks that must preserve behavior (compile + unit tests) and meet stylistic constraints (linting, idiomatic patterns).

Scope and goals:
- Evaluate model ability to perform targeted refactors (extract method, rename symbol, simplify control flow, reduce cyclomatic complexity) for Python, JavaScript, and Java. C++ smoke cases included for compilation sensitivity.
- Gate models using three concrete signals: compile success rate (per-language), unit-test pass rate (preserve behavior), and style regressions (lint/diff score).
- Create short, repeatable prompts that can be used in canary/onboarding runs and regression checks.

Why this is different from existing prompt suites:
- Emphasizes incremental, local edits (avoid full-file rewrites) and requires runnable outputs (we run compile/test/lint).
- Adds explicit negative tests (mutation/fuzzing checks) to catch silent behavior changes.
- Provides clear remediation paths and short, prescriptive prompt variants for failure modes.

Evaluation rubric (scoring):
- Compile success rate: fraction of examples where the model output compiles/builds (threshold: >= 95% for PR pass).
- Unit-test preservation: fraction of examples where the provided unit tests still pass after the change (threshold: >= 92%).
- Behavioral fuzz delta: synthetic input fuzz tests; allowed delta in outputs (where nondeterministic) <= 1% failing cases.
- Style delta: measured by linter failures delta and token-level diff magnitude (threshold: <= 10% more linter errors than reference).
- Patch conciseness: average LOC changed; we flag large rewrites ( > 30% of file) as potential regression risk.

Prompt patterns (canonical templates):
1) Local-extract-method (Python) - minimal edit constraint
Prompt: "You are given a Python file and a short test file. Perform a focused refactor: extract lines X..Y into a new helper function named <SUGGESTED_NAME>. Keep all imports and public interfaces unchanged. Do not change the test file. Return only the modified file contents (single file) with the helper inserted above the original function. Ensure the code passes the tests when run with pytest."
Notes: prefer single-file outputs for the harness; include // or # markers to indicate file boundaries when multiple files required.

2) Rename-symbol-safe (JavaScript) - cross-file safe rename
Prompt: "Given these two JS modules (A.js and B.js), rename the exported function `computeSum` to `sumValues` across the project. Update imports/exports accordingly. Keep behavior identical and do not change tests. Return the two files concatenated with separators."

3) Simplify-branch (Java) - preserve public API
Prompt: "Refactor the given method to simplify nested conditionals while preserving behavior. Do not change the method signature or public API. Keep comments that clarify logic. Ensure the project compiles with `mvn -q test` and that provided unit tests pass."

4) Fix-and-provide-tests (multi) - patch + unit test add
Prompt: "There is a failing unit test below. Modify the implementation to fix the failing case and add at most one new focused test to prevent regression. Keep changes minimal. Explain the root cause in one sentence (in a comment)."

5) C++ compile-safety check (smoke)
Prompt: "Apply the micro-refactor described (rename local variable, reduce duplication) to this .cpp file. Make no changes to build scripts. Output must compile with g++ -std=17. If you cannot safely perform this change, return the original file unchanged and explain why in a trailing comment."

Example testcases (short):
- Python: small CLI parser function with duplicated block; extract into `_parse_flags` helper; tests check feature flags and edge-case parsing.
- JavaScript: frontend util `formatCurrency` used across two files; rename and update imports; jest snapshot test included.
- Java: legacy method with nested ifs causing a branch explosion; simplify using early returns; maven unit test verifies behaviour.
- C++: header include ordering and name collisions; small rename to avoid conflict; compile step finds missing include.

Failure modes and how to detect them:
- Silent behavioral change: compile + unit tests still pass but logic subtly changed; detect with fuzzing harness and additional invariants (property tests).
- Overzealous rewrites: model rewrites entire file; detect by LOC-changed > threshold and reject or ask for smaller patch variant.
- Missing imports/includes: compile fails; common in JS/Java when rename breaks package references.
- Test modification: model modifies tests to make them pass; detect by diffing test file contents and rejecting outputs that touch tests unless prompt specifically allows it.

Automated regression gating policy (proposal):
- Stage 0 (local smoke): compile + unit tests + lint run on a small sample (20 cases). If compile >= 90% and test pass >= 88% -> advance.
- Stage 1 (canary): run full promptset (200 cases across languages). Thresholds: compile >= 95%, test pass >= 92%, style delta <= 10% -> qualify for broader rollout.
- Blockers: any case with tests modified by the model, or LOC-change > 50% on > 5% of cases, triggers human review.

Telemetry and logs to capture in harness runs:
- input_id, language, prompt_variant, model_version, output_blob, compile_stdout/stderr, test_stdout/stderr, lint_report, LOC_changed, timestamp, run_status (pass/fail/partial), fuzz_delta_score.
- For failed cases capture reproducible minimal example and attach to triage issue.

Operational playbook for triage (when the model fails canary):
1) Run targeted diagnostics on failing examples to classify failure mode (compile, test, style, rewrite-size, test-mod).
2) For compile/test failures, try prompt variants: "constrain edits to region X..Y" and "prefer patch format (unified diff)". Rerun harness.
3) If still failing, roll back model to previous version in canary and open an RCA ticket with failing examples and suggested prompt mitigations.
4) Add regression test to the promptset where appropriate (convert failing example to seed case).

Prompt variants and escalation ladder (short):
- Variant A: minimal-edit constraint + explicit "do not modify tests" line.
- Variant B: produce unified diff only; keep change under N lines.
- Variant C: if variant B is ambiguous, require a short rationale comment and a unit-test run summary in the assistant response.

Quick onboarding checklist for a new model (engineer running initial onboarding):
- [ ] Run Stage 0 smoke with language subset the model claims to support.
- [ ] Verify compile and test thresholds locally.
- [ ] Check top-5 failure examples for rewrite-size and test-mod issues.
- [ ] If green, schedule canary (Stage 1) run; otherwise try variants A/B.
- [ ] If canary fails, create RCA and block rollout until addressed.

Examples of prompt->expected harness interaction (short):
- Input: Python file with duplicate email parsing block. Prompt: extract helper.
  Expected model behavior: return single-file patch with helper function, tests still pass, LOC_changed <= 12.
- Input: JS modules A/B rename.
  Expected: two files returned, imports updated, jest tests unchanged and pass.

Open questions / TODOs:
- Should style delta use a combined metric (pylint + black format diff) or separate signals per linter? (Priya to prototype).
- Add randomized property-based tests for Python examples (Ethan to draft 25 generators).
- Decide exact LOC-change thresholds per-language (Marco to propose numbers based on repo stats).

Appendix: sample harness commands
- Python: pytest -q {case_dir}
- Java: mvn -q -DskipITs=false test
- JS: npm ci && npm test --prefix {case_dir}
- C++: g++ -std=17 -I{include_paths} {files} -o /tmp/a && /tmp/a || true

Notes to reviewers:
This is a living doc; it's intentionally prescriptive because we want repeatability in auto-gating. Please add failing examples directly to the linked Github PR branch and tag the corresponding Jira ticket.

Signed-off-by: Ava Chen (eng-applied-ml)

Prompt: "You are given a Python file and a short test file. Perform a focused refactor: extract lines X..Y into a new helper function named <SUGGESTED_NAME>. Keep all imports and public interfaces unchanged. Do not change the test file. Return only the modified file contents (single file) with the helper inserted above the original function. Ensure the code passes the tests when run with pytest."
Notes: prefer single-file outputs for the harness; include // or # markers to indicate file boundaries when multiple files required.

2) Rename-symbol-safe (JavaScript) - cross-file safe rename
Prompt: "Given these two JS modules (A.js and B.js), rename the exported function `computeSum` to `sumValues` across the project. Update imports/exports accordingly. Keep behavior identical and do not change tests. Return the two files concatenated with separators."

3) Simplify-branch (Java) - preserve public API
Prompt: "Refactor the given method to simplify nested conditionals while preserving behavior. Do not change the method signature or public API. Keep comments that clarify logic. Ensure the project compiles with `mvn -q test` and that provided unit tests pass."

4) Fix-and-provide-tests (multi) - patch + unit test add
Prompt: "There is a failing unit test below. Modify the implementation to fix the failing case and add at most one new focused test to prevent regression. Keep changes minimal. Explain the root cause in one sentence (in a comment)."

5) C++ compile-safety check (smoke)
Prompt: "Apply the micro-refactor described (rename local variable, reduce duplication) to this .cpp file. Make no changes to build scripts. Output must compile with g++ -std=17. If you cannot safely perform this change, return the original file unchanged and explain why in a trailing comment."

Example testcases (short):
- Python: small CLI parser function with duplicated block; extract into `_parse_flags` helper; tests check feature flags and edge-case parsing.
- JavaScript: frontend util `formatCurrency` used across two files; rename and update imports; jest snapshot test included.
- Java: legacy method with nested ifs causing a branch explosion; simplify using early returns; maven unit test verifies behaviour.
- C++: header include ordering and name collisions; small rename to avoid conflict; compile step finds missing include.

Failure modes and how to detect them:
- Silent behavioral change: compile + unit tests still pass but logic subtly changed; detect with fuzzing harness and additional invariants (property tests).
- Overzealous rewrites: model rewrites entire file; detect by LOC-changed > threshold and reject or ask for smaller patch variant.
- Missing imports/includes: compile fails; common in JS/Java when rename breaks package references.
- Test modification: model modifies tests to make them pass; detect by diffing test file contents and rejecting outputs that touch tests unless prompt specifically allows it.

Automated regression gating policy (proposal):
- Stage 0 (local smoke): compile + unit tests + lint run on a small sample (20 cases). If compile >= 90% and test pass >= 88% -> advance.
- Stage 1 (canary): run full promptset (200 cases across languages). Thresholds: compile >= 95%, test pass >= 92%, style delta <= 10% -> qualify for broader rollout.
- Blockers: any case with tests modified by the model, or LOC-change > 50% on > 5% of cases, triggers human review.

Telemetry and logs to capture in harness runs:
- input_id, language, prompt_variant, model_version, output_blob, compile_stdout/stderr, test_stdout/stderr, lint_report, LOC_changed, timestamp, run_status (pass/fail/partial), fuzz_delta_score.
- For failed cases capture reproducible minimal example and attach to triage issue.

Operational playbook for triage (when the model fails canary):
1) Run targeted diagnostics on failing examples to classify failure mode (compile, test, style, rewrite-size, test-mod).
2) For compile/test failures, try prompt variants: "constrain edits to region X..Y" and "prefer patch format (unified diff)". Rerun harness.
3) If still failing, roll back model to previous version in canary and open an RCA ticket with failing examples and suggested prompt mitigations.
4) Add regression test to the promptset where appropriate (convert failing example to seed case).

Prompt variants and escalation ladder (short):
- Variant A: minimal-edit constraint + explicit "do not modify tests" line.
- Variant B: produce unified diff only; keep change under N lines.
- Variant C: if variant B is ambiguous, require a short rationale comment and a unit-test run summary in the assistant response.

Quick onboarding checklist for a new model (engineer running initial onboarding):
- [ ] Run Stage 0 smoke with language subset the model claims to support.
- [ ] Verify compile and test thresholds locally.
- [ ] Check top-5 failure examples for rewrite-size and test-mod issues.
- [ ] If green, schedule canary (Stage 1) run; otherwise try variants A/B.
- [ ] If canary fails, create RCA and block rollout until addressed.

Examples of prompt->expected harness interaction (short):
- Input: Python file with duplicate email parsing block. Prompt: extract helper.
  Expected model behavior: return single-file patch with helper function, tests still pass, LOC_changed <= 12.
- Input: JS modules A/B rename.
  Expected: two files returned, imports updated, jest tests unchanged and pass.

Open questions / TODOs:
- Should style delta use a combined metric (pylint + black format diff) or separate signals per linter? (Priya to prototype).
- Add randomized property-based tests for Python examples (Ethan to draft 25 generators).
- Decide exact LOC-change thresholds per-language (Marco to propose numbers based on repo stats).

Appendix: sample harness commands
- Python: pytest -q {case_dir}
- Java: mvn -q -DskipITs=false test
- JS: npm ci && npm test --prefix {case_dir}
- C++: g++ -std=17 -I{include_paths} {files} -o /tmp/a && /tmp/a || true

Notes to reviewers:
This is a living doc; it's intentionally prescriptive because we want repeatability in auto-gating. Please add failing examples directly to the linked Github PR branch and tag the corresponding Jira ticket.

Signed-off-by: Ava Chen (eng-applied-ml)

title: Candidate Perturbation Protocol and Slice Atlas (2026)
preview: A working protocol and dataset manifest for validating candidate ranking changes using controlled perturbations, pairwise preference labels, and slice-based NDCG auditing. Focused on producing reproducible regression checks and actionable slice-level diagnostics for model onboarding.

title: Early Impact Onboarding: 30/60/90 Experiment Plan
preview:
Purpose:
This is a lightweight, experiment-driven 30/60/90 onboarding plan designed to surface early product impact and technical blockers for customers adopting Redwood Inference. The emphasis is on short, measurable experiments that validate integration assumptions (latency, cost per token, SDK fit) and produce at least one customer-visible win within 30 days.

Audience:
- Customer Success Manager (CSM) owning the engagement
- Customer engineering lead / ML engineer
- Solutions engineer assigned to the account

Outcome hypothesis (example):
Within 90 days the customer has:
- Integrated Redwood API for a single, high-value feature (chat or summarization) in staging,
- Measured end-to-end median P95 latency and unit cost under target thresholds,
- A documented rollout plan for production and a renewal signal tied to usage and SLA requirements.

Success metrics (agree these with customer at kickoff):
- 30-day: working integration in staging returning valid responses for 80% of test prompts; initial P95 latency benchmark collected.
- 60-day: feature exercised by internal users, token cost per 1k tokens within target +/-10%, two regression prompts passing quality gates.
- 90-day: production pilot active, usage >= X requests/day (customer-specific), contract renewal conversation initiated using observed ROI.

Kickoff checklist (pre-30 days):
- Schedule 60m kickoff: introduce CSM, SE, and stakeholders; align success metrics.
- Share SDK quickstart + example repo link; verify customer can call the API.
- Agree on test data set (10–50 prompts) that represent critical user flows.
- Confirm access model (hosted vs dedicated vs private) and any compliance needs.
- Create a shared tracker (sheet) with experiments, owners, and acceptance criteria.

30-day sprint (goal: integration + baseline):
- Experiment A: "Hello, real data" integration
  - Owner: Customer eng + SE
  - Acceptance: API calls succeed for 80% of sample prompts; sample responses captured in tracker.
  - Tasks: import SDK, wire auth, call model endpoint, capture raw outputs.
- Experiment B: latency baseline
  - Owner: SE + customer infra
  - Acceptance: end-to-end P95 latency measured for 99th percentile prompt length; note network contribution.
  - Tasks: run 1000 synthetic calls, record latencies, annotate outliers.
- Quick wins: turn on streaming if applicable; enable prefix cache for repeated prompts.

Artifacts to produce by day 30:
- Baseline metrics snapshot (latency, token counts, errors) in shared sheet.
- List of top 5 integration blockers with owners and ETA.
- Demo script to reproduce the integration in <30s.

60-day sprint (goal: quality + cost controls):
- Experiment C: quality gate and regression checks
  - Owner: Customer product + CSM
  - Acceptance: 2 regression prompts pass human review or scoring threshold; log where outputs deviate.
- Experiment D: cost optimization pilot
  - Owner: CSM + SE
  - Acceptance: recommended batching/quantization or model variant reduces projected per-token cost by target percent without breaking quality SLA.
  - Tasks: trial a smaller or quantized variant on a subset of traffic, compare outputs.
- Run a short internal pilot with 5–10 users to gather usability feedback and failure modes.

Artifacts to produce by day 60:
- Quality regression report with example failures and mitigation proposals.
- Cost model: expected cost per feature at 1k/10k/100k monthly tokens and sensitivity to model choice and batching.
- Operational playbook draft: alert thresholds, fallback model policy, escalation path.

90-day sprint (goal: pilot-to-prod readiness + renewal signal):
- Experiment E: production pilot runbook
  - Owner: Customer ops + CSM
  - Acceptance: pilot traffic flowing; no critical outages; SLO targets observed for 2 consecutive weeks.
- Experiment F: renewal metrics package
  - Owner: CSM
  - Acceptance: one-pager ROI + usage trends prepared and reviewed with customer execs.
- Plan for canary rollout and automatic fallback to cheaper/compatible model variants when latency or capacity breach occurs.

Artifacts to produce by day 90:
- Pilot retrospective: wins, gaps, and recommended scope for next quarter.
- Renewal brief: explicit renewal asks, negotiated SKU/commit, and next-step timeline.

Risks & mitigation (living list):
- Data privacy / compliance surprises: raise to security early, map required controls for Private deployment.
- Unexpected latency from customer network: propose edge routing or region change; quantify impact.
- Regression in output quality after cost optimizations: schedule human-in-the-loop monitoring and rollback criteria.

Engagement cadence (example):
- Week 0: kickoff (60m)
- Weekly: 30m sync with eng owners (standup on experiments)
- Every 2 weeks: tactical review with product stakeholder (30–45m)
- End of each sprint: deliver metrics snapshot and decisions document.

Customer communication templates (short snippets to copy):
- Kickoff invite: "Agenda: success metrics, integration plan, required access. Please bring a sample of high-value prompts."
- Weekly update: "This week: completed latency baseline; blockers: auth token rotation (owner: X). Next week: start quality pilot."
- Pilot complete ask: "We have 2 weeks of pilot traffic with median latency X and projected cost $Y/month. Would you like to discuss a production commit?"

Hand-off to renewal / account exec:
- Transfer packet: baseline + 60-day cost model + 90-day pilot retrospective + recommended SKU/commit.
- Suggested renewal trigger: sustained usage above agreed threshold for 30 days OR demonstrated business KPI lift (example: NPS increase on support summaries).

Notes & tips for CSMs:
- Keep experiments timeboxed; prefer 1–2 focused experiments per sprint.
- Capture decision artifacts as single-line bullets in the shared tracker so Sales can consume them.
- Use model variants as a safety valve — enable rapid fallback when cost or latency breaches occur.
- Drive toward at least one customer-visible positive outcome in first 30 days to build momentum.

Appendix: shared tracker columns (one row per experiment)
- Experiment ID | Owner | Start date | End date | Acceptance criteria | Status | Notes | Links

Last updated: 2026-03-05
